chapters.summary: Council staff welcomes attendees to the joint Committee on Education and Technology hearing, reminds audience to silence devices and not approach the dais, and invites those wishing to testify to fill out appearance cards.
chapters.title: Council staff opens hearing and announces procedures

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to today's New York City Council hearing for the Committee on Education, joined with the Committee on Technology.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please sign all cell phone and electronic devices, and as a friendly reminder, do not approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you wish to testify today, please fill out an appearance card with one of the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Without further ado, chairs, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Dinowitz introduces himself as Education Committee Chair and former teacher, framing the hearing around AI proliferation in schools, DOE's preliminary AI guidelines issued in May, and the intense debate over student-facing AI tools.
chapters.title: Chair Dinowitz opening remarks on AI landscape in NYC schools

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Eric Dinowitz, New York City Public School alum, although with a different signature on my diploma,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: public school special education teacher and chair of the Committee on Education, and I am excited to be joined by my friend from the South,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Chair of the Committee on Technology, Councilmember De La Rosa, for today's hearing on artificial intelligence, student data, and privacy in New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Today's hearing will examine the landscape of AI use in New York City public schools, particularly as it relates to academic integrity and student data and privacy.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The rapid proliferation of AI tools has sparked intense citywide debate about the role and use of AI in schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In May, DOE issued preliminary guidelines for AI in public schools, but the guidance has faced strong opposition from parents and advocacy groups alike.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz details calls from council members and CECs for a two-year moratorium on student-facing AI, cites a 2026 state comptroller audit finding DOE lacks a data classification policy, and references the PowerSchool breach exposing millions of students' data. He expresses concern about cognitive offloading and algorithmic bias.
chapters.title: Dinowitz on moratorium calls, data breaches, and cognitive offloading

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Many council members, including myself and numerous community education councils, have gone as far as calling for a two-year moratorium on the use of student-facing AI in schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: They point to ongoing worries about data privacy breaches and lack of transparency.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And very troublingly for me as a former teacher, cognitive offloading and algorithmic bias.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Others, however, argue that the question of allowing AI in schools is no longer relevant.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: National survey data show that the majority of students are using some form of AI for school-related activities.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Instead, they argue the question should be, how do we harness it safely, equitably, and effectively for our students?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Underlying this debate is the issue of data collection by third-party vendors and whether DOE's policy aligns with national standards.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: A 2026 audit by the state comptroller found that DOE does not have a documented data classification policy and does not maintain a comprehensive list of all applications used by each school.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: With a number of high-profile data breaches, including the PowerSchool breach that exposed personally identifiable information for tens of millions of students and educators.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: nationwide.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Questions remain around what information is being collected about students and who has access to this information.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Now as someone who doesn't even believe calculators should be used in elementary school classrooms, I look forward to hearing today about how DOE is planning to protect student data

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: plans for updated DOE guidelines and AI, and how DOE plans to ensure academic integrity and rigor in light of AI.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'd now like to turn it to Chair De La Rosa for opening remarks.

chapters.summary: Councilmember De La Rosa introduces herself as Technology Committee Chair and public school parent, outlining concerns about AI tools collecting student data including names, behavioral analytics, medical history, and facial geometry. She cites 141 DOE data breaches between January 2023 and February 2025.
chapters.title: Chair De La Rosa opening remarks on AI privacy and safety risks

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to our hearing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Carmen De La Rosa, and I'm the chair of the Committee on Technology.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm happy to join my neighbor to the north.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Chair Dinowitz today to discuss an issue that I care about deeply, not only as a chair of this committee, but also as a public school parent who is concerned about the future we are building for the next generation.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Technology has become an unavoidable part of our lives, with artificial intelligence changing the way we work, learn, and make decisions.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: This is why we have an obligation to ensure that innovation serves people and that technological progress is guided by accountability, transparency, safety, and public interest.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Artificial intelligence tools are used by the Department of Education in its daily operations, as well as in classrooms.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The use of AI in education has raised concerns that it may affect students'critical thinking.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: and compromise the traditional learning process.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Another concern that people have is related to digital privacy, safety, and inequality.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Some AI tools use personal data as training data.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That data could include students'information such as names, dates of birth, behavioral analytics, medical history, family income, ethnicity, and even facial geometry.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Without proper safeguards, this information could be used to profile students, predict or surveil their behavior, or make assumptions about their abilities and future opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: This information can also be breached.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: In fact, based on a recent New York State Comptroller's report, between January 2023 and February 2025, DOE experienced 141 data breaches incidents.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Based on our conversations and public reporting, some tools used by the DOE may collect data without parents'knowledge or consent.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa warns that AI tools may profile students, surveil behavior, or make biased assumptions about abilities. She notes she joined colleagues in calling for a two-year moratorium and expresses disappointment that the Chief Privacy Officer and Office of Algorithmic Accountability are absent from the hearing.
chapters.title: De La Rosa on AI bias, discrimination, and need for safeguards

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: While AI may offer potential benefits in education, its ethical use must be carefully considered.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Many tools may have bias and, as a result, discriminate against certain students or communities.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: From the Technology Committee's perspective, safeguards against bias in AI, algorithms, and protecting student privacy are fundamental issues that must be addressed.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Today, we wish to discuss both how AI should fit into the curriculum and how the Department of Education

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: uses AI in its operations.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We need to make sure that AI supports students and teachers rather than undermining the quality of education.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We must ensure that we are preparing the next generation to thrive in this technological era while also protecting the rights and future opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Like Chair Dinowitz, I also joined my colleagues in signing on to a letter from our school communities, and we've heard from our CECs calling on a two-year moratorium.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We look forward to hearing testimonies from the Department of Education and OTI to gain insight and clarity around the many questions about AI in the classroom and the use of AI in the education system.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We also look forward to hearing from members of the public, students, families, educators, and advocates.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Unfortunately, neither the Chief Privacy Officer nor the representatives of the newly established Office of Algorithmic Accountability are here today.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: However, we look forward to receiving their testimony, which we've received, and responses in writing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: As the school year is ending, I also wish to congratulate every student and teacher on completing another academic year.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa thanks Technology Committee staff including Legislative Counsel Irene Bivolsky and policy analyst Eric Brown, and recognizes council members Schulman, Hanif, Gutierrez, Suong, and Wong in attendance.
chapters.title: De La Rosa acknowledges committee staff and council members present

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Now I'd like to thank Technology Committee staff, Legislative Council, Irene Bivolsky.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm learning.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm going to get it right.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I promise.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And policy analyst Eric Brown for putting together this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'd also like to recognize members of the council who are here.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: of the Technology Committee, Councilmember Shulman, Hanif, and Gutierrez, and we have Suong and Wong here as well.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Am I missing anybody?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And now we'll turn it back to Chair.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz introduces Intro 684 requiring a study on language access and geography affecting gifted and talented program access, and Intro 853 on language access, income, and geography affecting special education access, both sponsored by Councilmember Zhuang.
chapters.title: Dinowitz introduces Zhuang's bills on education access studies

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Additionally today, the committee will also consider the following legislation.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Two bills sponsored by Councilmember Susan Zhuang.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: These include Introduction 684 in relation to requiring a study on how language access needs and geographic location affect access to early elementary gifted and talented programs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And Introduction 853 in relation to requiring a study on how language access needs, income, and geographic location affect access to special education programs and services.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'd like to invite Councilmember Zhuang to make opening remarks about these bills.

chapters.summary: Zhuang argues that children in outer boroughs, lower-income families, and English language learners have less access to specialized programs. She shares examples of students traveling over two hours to special education schools and calls for expanding gifted and talented programs rather than eliminating them.
chapters.title: Councilmember Zhuang on education access for all bill package

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I'm here today asking for your support for my education access for all bill package, Intro 684 and Intro 853.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I'm proud to put forward this pair of bills that together we would go a long way towards expanding education access for all students.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Every child deserves the education that meets them where they are and help them grow.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: But like everything else in this city, children in outer boroughs have less access to specialized programs than others.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Children from lower income families have less access.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And the children for whom English is a second language have less access too.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I believe that all students deserve access to education programs that meet their needs.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And no student should be limited due to where they live,

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: What's their parents'income or language they speak at home?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Both of these bills ask the city to do a study how language access needs, income, and the geographic location affect access to education.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Intro 684 is focused on access to gifted and talented program, while Intro...

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: 853 is about access to special education program.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Gaps in access affects both type of specialized education.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: For example, it's sadly well known not enough black and brown students in specialized high schools.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: But how can we, how can brilliant

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Black and brown students have the same chance to get in when their elementary and junior high school do not have gifted and talented program that other students have.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: We are denying black and brown students opportunities and that must be end.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: We should not

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: be thinking about eliminating those programs.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: We should be expanding them so every child who can benefit from them can get into one.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: We need to create more opportunity, not less opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: As another example, it's also sadly well known, some children in outer boroughs need to travel a long bus ride to get to the school, offer the special education they need.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: It's extremely burden for both students and their parents, especially in the bad weather.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I still remember their parents call my office in the snow day.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: The school is open.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: They have to travel.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: The kids have to travel more than two hours to get into school and spend one hour, two hours later, and the school have to end early and travel another two hours to get home.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Parents, those first language is not English also struggle to learn about opportunities available to their children and how to get their children into those programs.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: These are only a few examples.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: We ask you to join us in supporting both of these bills and those issues and other gaps.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And I also want to thank all of you who have already co-sponsored and advocates came to testify and rallied with me today.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz introduces his pre-considered bills: Intro 2129 requiring annual reporting on algorithmic tools approved for schools, and Intro 2130 requiring quarterly reporting on data breaches of algorithmic tools. He emphasizes transparency as critical for turning AI oversight from trust to accountability.
chapters.title: Dinowitz on pre-considered bills 2129 and 2130 for AI reporting

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Finally, the committee will hear two bills sponsored by myself, pre-considered intro 2129

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: in relation to annual reporting on algorithmic tools approved for use in schools, and pre-considered introduction 2130 in relation to quarterly reporting on data breaches of algorithmic tools in schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: As we will discuss today, AI is already entering schools in ways that are meaningfully and perhaps detrimentally shaping the educational experiences of students,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: as well as the professional experiences of teachers, counselors, administrators, and everyone in between.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That makes transparency critical.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Families and educators should know what tools are being used, what data they collect, whether they have been tested for bias and accuracy, and what happens when sensitive student data is exposed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: By requiring regular public reporting on approved AI tools and related data breaches, these bills will help turn AI oversight from a matter of trust into a matter of accountability.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing testimony from the administration about these pieces of legislation.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you to all the members who are here that have been named by Chair De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I would also like to thank my Chief of Staff, Jenna Klaus, and my Legislative and Budget Director, Theo Salter, the Education Committee staff, including Councilor Alejandra.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz reminds witnesses to fill out witness slips, notes no votes will be taken, limits testimony to two minutes and council questions to five minutes, and acknowledges Councilmembers Linda Lee, Kevin Riley, and Farah Louis have joined.
chapters.title: Dinowitz announces testimony rules and recognizes arriving members

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Joe Carvajal, Senior Policy Analyst Chloe Rivera, Policy Analyst Katie Selena, Principal Financial Analyst Andrew Lane Lawless, and Financial Analyst Grace Amato for their work on today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I would like to remind everyone who wishes to testify in person today that you must fill out a witness slip, which you can find on the desk of the Sergeant at Arms near the entrance of this room.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Please fill out the slip even if you have already registered in advance that you will be testifying in person today.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I also want to point out that we will not be voting on any legislation today.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: To allow as many people as possible to testify, testimony will be limited to two minutes per person, whether you are testifying in person or on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I am also going to ask my colleagues to limit their questions and comments to five minutes.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Please note that generally, witnesses who are here in person will testify before those who are signed into the Zoom webinar.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I would also like to note that we have been joined by Council Members Linda Lee and Kevin Riley.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: As well as Councilmember Farah Lewis.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: As a reminder to all of our witnesses, please state your name prior to your testimony for the record.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: If anyone here today...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: If anyone here today requires an accessible version of the presentation given at today's hearing, please email testimony at council.nyc.gov.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz administers the truth affirmation to the panel of DOE and administration witnesses, calling on each individually including Alicia Osborne-Reinhardt, Jeff Klein, Veronica Queller, Dennis Doyle, Tara Carrozza, Scott Strickland, Alan Chang, and others.
chapters.title: Administering affirmation to mayoral administration witnesses

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Now in accordance with the rules of the council, I will administer the affirmation to the witnesses from the mayoral administration.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I will call on each of you individually for a response.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Please raise your right hand.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There's a lot more papers than you're up here.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Please raise your right hands.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before these committees and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Alicia Osborne-Reinhardt?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Jeff Klein?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Veronica Queller.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Dennis Doyle.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Tara Carrozza.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Carrozza.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Got it, okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Lara Lai.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Brenda Garcia.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Suzanne Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Liana Wright.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Scott Strickland.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Alan Chang.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Maribel Huller.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: FTC Danielle Ginter.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Mia Teresa Pate?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Pate?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: If you're here, is Tweet empty?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Like you're all here.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You may begin your testimony.

chapters.summary: Danielle Junta introduces herself as First Deputy Chancellor, describes her oversight of superintendents and key offices, and notes she is joined by colleagues from data privacy, DIIT, early childhood, and inclusive learning. She describes regular community conversations about AI and screen time.
chapters.title: DOE First Deputy Chancellor Junta introduces team and AI engagement context

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Dinowitz, Chair De La Rosa, and members of the Committees of Education and Technology.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Good morning to our council members.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: My name is Danielle Junta.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I am the first deputy chancellor of New York City Public Schools, serving under the chancellor of Kamar Samuels.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Prior to this role, I was a teacher, network leader, superintendent, and most recently, chief of school leadership development.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: But my favorite role in New York City Public Schools was as a student.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I am a graduate of New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I now oversee superintendents and key offices, including academics and instruction, district planning, enrollment, new school development and design, policy and evaluation, and student pathways.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I'm also joined today by supervising superintendents, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Alan Chang and Dr.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Maribel Huller, who work directly with our superintendents and their school communities across the city.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And because AI spans multiple divisions and areas of expertise, I am also joined by colleagues from data privacy, the Division of Instructional and Information Technology, early childhood education, and the Division of Inclusive and Accessible Learning.

chapters.summary: Junta explains that when Chancellor Samuels assumed his role in January, NYC Public Schools published AI guidance on March 25, launched an AI landing page, opened a 45-day feedback window, and began collecting input from educators, families, students, and community members across the city.
chapters.title: Junta on March AI guidance and 45-day feedback process

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Underscoring our commitment to a coordinated and responsible

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: approach to this work.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Over the past few months, the Chancellor and I have been speaking regularly to our communities about artificial intelligence and screen time in classrooms.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Those conversations have made clear that families, educators, and students are thoughtfully considering how these developments are shaping teaching and learning for our students.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I appreciate the opportunity to engage with you about artificial intelligence, student data privacy, and the role of technology in our schools.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We know this conversation is urgent.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We recognize the rapid evolution of AI requires careful judgment, clear safeguards, and a meaningful dialogue with communities we serve.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Families, advocates, labor partners, school communities, and members of this council have pushed us to think more deeply about the role of AI and screen-based technology in our schools.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We are grateful for the engagement, including the letter sent to the mayor and chancellor by 29 members of this council.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: The concern raised by the council members and school communities have helped shape our approach and reinforce the need for clear guardrails, transparency, and ongoing engagement.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: When Chancellor Samuels assumed his role in January, New York City Public Schools was completing AI guidance that had been initiated under the prior administration.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: On March 25, New York City

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: public schools published that guidance, launching an AI landing page on our website, opened a 45-day feedback window,

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: and began collecting input from educators, families, students, and community members across the city.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: This process was designed to be iterative from the start, reflecting our commitment to establish initial guardrails while continuing to listen, learn, and work with the stakeholders most affected by these decisions.

chapters.summary: Junta details the March guidance requirements: AI tools using student data must pass the IRMA privacy review, student data cannot train AI models, human review of AI outputs is required, and AI cannot replace educator judgment in high-stakes decisions. Student use requires educator guidance and critical evaluation.
chapters.title: Junta on March guidance protections and guardrails

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: The March guidance laid an important foundation for our protections.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: This guidance requires AI tools that use student data to go through New York City Public Schools'privacy and security review process,

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: prohibits student data from being used to train AI models, requires human review of AI outputs, and makes clear that AI cannot replace student teaching, I'm sorry, student teaching,

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: educator judgment, or qualified human decision-making in high-stakes decisions about students.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: It also makes clear that any student use of AI requires educator guidance, critical evaluation of outputs, and age-appropriate context.

chapters.summary: Junta reports 6,491 survey responses during the 45-day feedback period. Key takeaways include recognition of AI benefits with safeguards, student interest in AI literacy alongside over-reliance concerns, and educator distinction between teacher-facing and student-facing uses. Top concerns were cognitive development, mental health, and data privacy.
chapters.title: Junta on feedback results from 6,491 survey responses

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: During the 45-day period, which ended on May 8th, we received 6,491 survey responses from educators, families, students, and community members across our city.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We also gathered feedback through engagement with students.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: families, parent leaders, community, and citywide education councils, as well as the Chancellor's Advisory Councils, the Panel for Education, and other stakeholders.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: There were many key takeaways from the feedback.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Many respondents said they clearly or mostly understood the framework, and we heard recognition that AI and technology may have benefits when used thoughtfully and with appropriate safeguards.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Student in particular reflected both interest in AI literacy and concern about over-reliance.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Educators and staff also drew an important distinction between teacher-facing uses and student-facing uses.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: But the feedback also made clear that our next set of policies must be stronger, practical, and developmentally specific.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: The top concerns that were surfaced was the impact of AI on children's cognitive development, mental health and social-emotional well-being, and data privacy and security.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Families and educators shared that they want stronger guardrails, clearer expectations, and greater transparency.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Families and educators have also raised questions about screen-based technology more broadly, how technology is being used, and whether it is supporting active, meaningful engagement.

chapters.summary: Junta outlines values driving next policies: centering developmental appropriateness across grade levels, distinguishing between unstructured student-facing AI and carefully reviewed instructional tools, and recognizing different considerations for assistive technology required by IEPs versus general AI tools.
chapters.title: Junta on developmental appropriateness and distinguishing tool types

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: From this feedback, we understand that our current guidance does need to be strengthened to better protect students and respond to community input.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: While we're still shaping our next set of policies,

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Let me speak to the values of what is driving what comes next.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: First, we need to center developmental appropriateness.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Across all grades, students need opportunities to read, write, problem solve, discuss ideas, and work through challenging tasks with teachers and peers.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We are mindful of cognitive offloading when technology does the work that students need to do for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: AI should not short circuit those learning experiences or allow students to bypass the work that learning requires.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We also recognize that the developmental and instructional needs of students vary greatly from pre-K through high school, and our policies should reflect those distinctions.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: As we build on the March guidance, we are looking carefully at how expectations should differ by age and grade level,

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: how to prevent unstructured technology use, and how to prepare older students for a world in which AI is already present, without allowing AI to replace their own thinking.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Second, we are focused on distinguishing between different kinds of tools and uses.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Not all technology use is the same.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: The clearest risk is unstructured student-facing AI that allows students to bypass the thinking, writing, reasoning, and problem-solving they need to do for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: That is very different from a carefully reviewed tool designed for a specific instructional purpose, used under educator supervision,

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: With clear limits and a clear role for the teacher.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: It is also different from assistive technology or software like text-to-speech required by a student's IEP or 504 plan, a language access support for a multilingual learner, or a teacher-facing planning tool.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Those uses raise different legal, instructional, and equity considerations, ones that we are factoring into our next set of policies.

chapters.summary: Junta emphasizes student safety as essential, describes the IRMA privacy review process and December 2024 AI-specific standards added requiring vendor disclosure. She acknowledges families want broader questions answered about instructional purpose, accessibility, and bias, and commits to community engagement in technology governance.
chapters.title: Junta on student safety, IRMA review, and community voice

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Third, and probably most important, the safety of our students is essential.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Privacy, security, and instructional review must all work together as we evaluate technological tools.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: New York City Public Schools has an existing privacy and security review process through the Enterprise Request Management Application, or IRMA.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Software that has not been reviewed through IRMA is not approved for use with student or staff data.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: In December 2024, New York City Public Schools added AI-specific standards requiring vendors to disclose AI capabilities, prohibiting the use of student data to train AI models, and setting transparency requirements.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: This review is essential, but families and educators are also asking broader questions about instructional purpose, accessibility, bias, and how tools affect the student experience.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: As we enter the next phase of our work, we're working to establish a more comprehensive framework.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: One that responds to ongoing evolution of this rapidly changing technology and factors in students'protections, instructional quality, and more.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Finally, community voice must be part of the New York City Public Schools technology governance structure.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Families want more transparency.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Educators need clearer guidance.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Students need a voice in decisions about tools shaping their learning.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We're committed to engaging with each of these groups and others to ensure our approach to AI and technology reflects their continued input.

chapters.summary: Junta summarizes that March guidance established a foundation but feedback shows need for more detail and specificity. She states DOE is finalizing a stronger approach for the upcoming school year so educators and communities have clarity before the new year begins.
chapters.title: Junta summarizes next steps for stronger AI approach

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: In summary, our March guidance established the foundation.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: The feedback we've received since then from our surveys, from parent groups, and from our students has made clear that we need to build more detail and specificity on that foundation.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Any technology use must be developmentally appropriate, carefully reviewed, transparent to families, and in service of learning, not a substitute for it.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We are finalizing a stronger approach for the school year ahead so that educators and school communities will have clarity before the new year begins and will continue engagement around our updated guidance.

chapters.summary: Junta addresses the proposed bills: DOE supports goals of 2129 on algorithmic tool reporting and notes existing Local Law 35 reporting; supports 2130 on data breach reporting and notes existing state law compliance; and on Intro 412 regarding admissions disclosure, notes much of the information is already publicly available.
chapters.title: Junta on proposed legislation: Intros 2129, 2130, and 412

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I would like to now turn to the proposed legislation.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Pre-considered Intro 2026-2129.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Requires New York City public schools to report on algorithmic tools approved for use.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: in schools.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We support the goal of proposed legislation and pursuant to Local 35, New York City Public Schools annually reports information on algorithmic tools in use at schools.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We look forward to working with the Council and New York City Office of Technology and Innovation to ensure that any new reporting requirements align with existing legislation and our reporting systems.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Pre-considered intro 2026-2130 relates to quarterly reporting on data breaches involving algorithmic tools in schools.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Protecting student and staff data is of the utmost importance and is required by law.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We take any data breach seriously and follow all required procedures in the event of an incident.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We support the overall goals of the proposed legislation.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: With that said, New York City Public Schools already maintains policies regarding privacy audits of third-party tools that process student data, including algorithmic tools.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: These policies are publicly available on our websites.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: The links are in the testimony, and on the website, I would draw your attention to our schools.nyc.gov forward slash AI, forward slash in our schools.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We also have data privacy and security compliance process as the place to search for them.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Moreover, New York City Public Schools reports all

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: data breaches to the state in accordance with New York State Education Law 2D.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We also share information about recent data security incidents on our website and that you would search that way with the forward slash at the end data security incidents.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We welcome continued dialogue with the council regarding the proposed legislation.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: For intro number 412, this requires New York City public schools to disclose information regarding school admissions policies and procedures.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We support the underlying intent of the proposed legislation to ensure that school admissions processes are accessible, transparent, and well understood by our families and public.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: As written, we believe the bill references information that is already publicly available, including

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: each school's My Schools Directory page, which conveys priority structures, selection criteria, and prior year application and offers rates by priority group.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: The same directory data is also published in spreadsheet form on the New York City Public Schools Info Hub for multiple years.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Comprehensive admissions process guides on the New York City Public Schools website explaining evaluation methods, including auditions and other assessments.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And reporting required under Local Law 72 providing detailed admissions outcomes for each school.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We welcome the opportunity to work with the Council to explore how this existing information may further be clarified and made more

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: accessible for our families.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you and I welcome the conversation ahead.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks if DOE has determined a specific age when students should not use large language models. Dr. Pate and Dr. Holla confirm there is no developmental framework for AI age bands, though early childhood has screen time policies of 30 minutes per week for 2K through pre-K.
chapters.title: Dinowitz questions DOE on age restrictions for student-facing AI

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to start with the student-facing AI.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think it's one of the things that troubles me as a parent and a teacher the most.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Is there a specific age that the DOE has determined students should not be using things like large language models and an age where it's a

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: appropriate to begin such use in the classroom or outside the classroom?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Right now, we do not have a developmental framework for that, for age bands.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We have some clearer screen time policies in place in our early childhood.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: grade, you know, 2K through pre-K now, but I'm going to invite Dr.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Pate up to speak a little bit more to the specific AI.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And while she's coming up, I'd like to acknowledge that we've been joined by Council Members Maloney and Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: As our first deputy chancellor said, we don't currently have a policy, but we are learning from insight across the nation.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: We do know that we are paying very close attention to what it means to engage in developmentally appropriate use of AI technology, inclusive of screen time.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Pate.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You have approved, oh, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Holla?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Holla
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Holla
utterances.text: Just to add, specifically in the early childhoods, we just want to include that we have, it's 30 minutes per week and 15 minutes per day.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Holla
utterances.text: However, it's not used for students to be on it alone.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Holla
utterances.text: That's not the intent.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Holla
utterances.text: The intent of it is more for a policy, with the exceptions of like assistive technology for our students with IEPs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And what age is that for?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Holla
utterances.text: And that would be for any early childhood.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Holla
utterances.text: So 2K, 3K, 4K.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So there's no policy for kindergarten through fifth grade?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Holla
utterances.text: No, that's the consistent policy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Holla
utterances.text: Okay, there's no policy.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz questions why DOE approved AI vendors without empirical evidence of benefit to cognitive development, noting Gemini, Claude, and ChatGPT come pre-installed on student devices. He challenges DOE to provide peer-reviewed studies supporting these tools.
chapters.title: Dinowitz presses on approved AI vendors and empirical evidence

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There are seven tools that have, according to Local Law 35, that utilize AI, although it seems that there are many more.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: According to the vendor website.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I'm going to ask about that later, but the simple fact is that you have approved the use of AI tools, or you have approved vendors with AI tools and schools to purchase whatever tools there are.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And of course, you know, Gemini, the large language models come pre-installed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: On the devices that we give our kids, Gemini, Claude, and ChatGPT.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What evidence has the DOE used, peer-reviewed journals, empirical evidence, to demonstrate that these AI tools that you've approved as vendors are beneficial to our students and their cognitive development?

chapters.summary: Scott Strickland clarifies that ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are approved for staff use only, with Gemini available to about 35 high schools by principal request. Dinowitz challenges this, noting teachers and principals report students accessing these tools in classrooms, and asks about VPN circumvention.
chapters.title: Dinowitz and Strickland on ChatGPT and Gemini student access

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So when we're talking about ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini, we have not opened these up for student facing,

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: only under some very specific circumstances.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Alan, would you like to jump in and perhaps Scott?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So this is a great opportunity to clarify that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And just make sure you state your name for the record before you speak.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Scott Strickland, I'm the Deputy CIO in the Division of Instructional Information Technology at the New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: In relation to technology that's been approved for schools, we do have Microsoft products and Google products that have been longstanding approved.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: As Copilot and Gemini came out, we evaluated them under our new standard and also approved them, again, for staff use.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: I want to be very clear on that.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: For students, there is only the Gemini system within Google that we open up to high schools if a principal requests that.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: And there's about 35 high schools that have requested that this spring.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz presses on whether students can access AI tools through approved vendor subscriptions like Canva, and raises VPNs as a way students bypass DOE filters. Strickland acknowledges VPNs may be used but insists students are supposed to use the DOE network. Dinowitz notes multiple principals report students using ChatGPT in class.
chapters.title: Dinowitz on Canva, VPNs, and filter circumvention in schools

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There are a number of tools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I mean, I'm looking at this one right here, which says...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There are a number of tools on your web.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Canva is one of them.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There's another one, Observe AI.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It must be hundreds of vendors that use these AI tools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And when you say open up to students, are you telling me that if you give a fifth grader a Chromebook, they don't go on to the school's subscription for Canva and use those AI tools?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: I'll be clear on the devices.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: So a Chromebook is a user device.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: It comes with the Chrome browser.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: When the student logs into the DUE network, we filter a lot of websites so that they do not have access to them.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: In those cases, that's how we can control access to something like ChatGPT.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: I don't know if Canva is open to students.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: That's really just a presentation piece of software.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Our teachers use that extensively.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There are a number of vendors on your website that are approved vendors that have AI tools that are available for schools to buy.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But I just want to make sure I understand your testimony that schools buy these products, these subscriptions with AI tools, but students don't or are unable to use these specific components.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: That is correct.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, I have a number of teachers and principals who would disagree with you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I would also add, I'm not sure if you're aware,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Are you aware of VPNs?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Have you heard of this magic tool?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Are you aware that they are used in schools?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: In some cases, they may be, yes, but the students are supposed to log into the DUE network and use the DUE network for their work.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think the challenge we're facing already is that you've approved vendors that have AI tools, student-facing AI tools, and we've had

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: reports from teachers that these are being used in the classroom, where students use them, where students use ChatGPT or Gemini in the classroom, and they're somehow getting around your filters.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But let's pretend for a second that no student is able to access these student-facing AI tools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You have still approved vendors that have AI tools, including, according to your testimony, Google, Google Gemini, correct?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And what empirical evidence or peer-reviewed journals has the DOE looked at to determine that this is for the betterment of our students'education and their cognitive development?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: I'll turn it to the pedagogics.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: See here, red means go.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So make sure the red light's on.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz reads from Local Law 35 about an OpenGen AI teaching assistant tool that generates responses to student prompts, contradicting testimony that no student-facing AI is deployed. Strickland confirms it is piloted in District 28 with parental consent. Dinowitz calls students 'guinea pigs' and demands evidence of efficacy.
chapters.title: Dinowitz confronts DOE on OpenGen AI tool piloted in District 28

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Council.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: My name is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: Alan Chang.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: I'm the supervising superintendent for high schools in District 79.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: Where I want to start is,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: As both Council Chairs Dinowitz and De La Rosa have said, we deeply, deeply are committed to our public schools as places of learning.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: And we know that oftentimes in public discourse, uses of AI tools, particularly unstructured large language models like Claude and ChatGPT, have a way of shortcutting and short-circuiting that thinking.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: And by design, we have limited and prohibited that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: Your question is around the research that is behind this work.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Well, my question is that you are approving these tools, and I do want to read one to you from Local Law 35.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What it says and how it conflicts with the testimony that was just given.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And the question is why we're approving tools when there doesn't seem to be any empirical evidence to demonstrate their efficacy in student learning.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So this is OpenGen AI and Teaching Assistant Tool.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And it says the tool purpose

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The tool is your...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: used to generate responses to prompts entered by a student or a teacher.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Requesting the generative value tool to compose text response to a text input.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I just wanted to read that to you because that was...

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: That is correct.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: That Gen AI tool has been deployed to a very limited number of schools, and any student that interacts with that tool has gotten parental consent.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Mostly in District 28 is where that's been deployed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm sorry, we have to, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: For everyone in the audience, we have to listen, and if you have something to say, you can, what do we do, what's this little?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This means yes, and this means no.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But the verbal assents or dissents cannot continue, okay?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So in just one school district you're using this tool, District 28?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: That's the one where it's been piloted and is most heavily used by a small number of teachers and a small number of students.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And with what guardrails or assurances in place, this isn't...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Part of cognitive offloading that so many of us are concerned about.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: I'll make some general statements around that.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: It's used with...

chapters.summary: Dinowitz continues demanding peer-reviewed evidence for AI tools. Dr. Alan Chang cites meta-analyses and randomized control trials from journals including NPJ Science of Learning and PNAS, arguing structured AI tools with teacher guidance can support learning. Dinowitz counters that teacher guidance applies to any educational tool.
chapters.title: Dinowitz presses for empirical evidence; Dr. Chang cites studies

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I mean, my general question still stands.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I just want to make sure you understand my question.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It seems like even if it were just this one school that we're using this generative AI text, our children are being guinea pigs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And it just seems to me that I'm not hearing any studies or data that the DOE has done or that a third party has done or that peer-reviewed journals to demonstrate that these are tools that are beneficial to our students.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It seems to me that vendors have been approved and vendors are telling you that these are beneficial tools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I'm still not getting an answer to my question, which is what evidence is there that these tools are beneficial to our students?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: So, Chair, I'd love to be able to respond to that question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: Please, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: Because I'm...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: In our review of the evidence, there are multiple meta-analysis, randomized control trials, in peer-reviewed scientific articles that state that

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: when these tools are being used with educators that are intelligent tutoring systems, that are structured systems, that are able to be in place,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: What they're able to offer is really an ability to be able to provide careful supports around instructional design, feedback, and adaptability.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: We want to be able to cite one such article that is in the journal PNJ, Science of Learning, that had 28 studies.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: That was published in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: There's another research article in PNAS that is a peer-reviewed randomized control trial.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: Each of these pulled together many, many hundreds of studies and analyzed this research.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: And this research ultimately tells us one thing, that it is really important that students are not having

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: unfiltered, uncontrolled, and unsupervised AI use.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: But when it is being used together with teachers, under guidance and supported,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: that challenges and asks students to be able to show their thinking and learning, that learning is helpful and does persist.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks whether teachers are actually guiding AI use and for what educational purpose. Junta responds about tutoring programs and adaptive online tools, and acknowledges implementation challenges, saying DOE needs to understand how guidance is landing in schools and escalate policy violations.
chapters.title: Dinowitz on teacher guidance and AI implementation gaps

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Well, that's any educational tool, show your learning, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And any educational tool is teacher guidance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's the purpose of a teacher, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Otherwise, what the hell do I need a master's degree for in teaching, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And is it your understanding that that's actually happening in schools, that teachers are guiding the use of AI?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And for what purpose is this AI used?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What's the educational benefit?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Well, I think that there's so many different versions of how it might be used, the most important of which is exactly what Dr.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Chang just talked about with adult oversight.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So we know we have some tutoring.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Programs that, and online programs that are adaptive, whether it, that we're using more in supplemental and intervention spaces.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I also want to go back to the conversation about like the chat GPT.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: You know, we have these policies in our schools and as, and actually is,

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: how come we felt it very prudent to put out this guidance with guardrails, to be very clear about the IRMA process, what should and should not be student-facing, what can and shouldn't be teacher-facing.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And now, as you know, I've spent the vast majority of my career focused on implementation.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And so that's where we are now.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: How is this landing?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: schools, how is the guidance and our policies actually coming to life?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So when we hear these examples about the misuse against our policies, like we want to engage with our superintendents and principals to understand, like, where is the misunderstanding in the policy?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We should not be having, you know, this particular

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: you know, the chat GPT example, freely for children to be using in classrooms.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We should make sure that the Google Gemini is, we understand it to be the 35 schools, and if it's far exceeding that, those are great examples that should be escalated to us for further explanation.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And that's part of our implementation process, the checks and balances of it.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think we hear all the time about escalating.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz shares concerns from multiple principals who feel they are 'building the plane as they're flying it' on AI. He draws parallels to credit recovery programs where students sit at computers with no teacher interaction, warning AI could worsen this without proper guardrails.
chapters.title: Dinowitz raises credit recovery and principal concerns about AI

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Issues and I will tell you when it's almost 1600 schools escalating these issues to me I've had multiple principals tell me you know when it comes to this AI stuff they're they're frightened and they feel like there isn't enough guidance and they feel like they're building the plane it was an

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: as they're flying it that was an actual phrase multiple principals have said they feel like they're flying the plane I want to share a reality with you

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: that I experienced in high school about credit recovery.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You know, all of the principals are really incentivized to make sure kids graduate at any expense.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And so what they would often do is what's called credit recovery.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Does this sound familiar, credit recovery?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And they'll plop a kid in front of a computer.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think in one of my schools it was called Atlas.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And they would sit for 45 minutes doing like online test stuff.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And that was bad enough.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: No teacher interaction with 30 kids in a credit recovery class.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And now we didn't even get to the biases, the hallucinations of generative AI.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And we do not have the guardrails right now to ensure that our yearning to make sure kids get these credits doesn't conflict with what good education looks like.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And it's frightening to so many of the students and families and honestly, a lot of principals who just don't feel like they have the guidance yet.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yet these tools, these approved vendors are still permeating their schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'll ask two more questions and I'll turn it over to my co-chair, my neighbor from the south, Chair Diller.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: For those of you that don't know, my district is like here and her district is right to the south of mine.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: A little geography lesson for all of you.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks about DOE's position on AI detection tools given documented false positive rates disproportionately flagging multilingual learners and students with disabilities. Strickland confirms DOE has no AI detection tools approved. Dinowitz notes DOE does have plagiarism detection tools but no AI-specific detectors for disciplinary hearings.
chapters.title: Dinowitz questions DOE on AI detection tools and false positives

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What is the DOE's position on AI detection tools given documented false positive rates that disproportionately flag multilingual learners and students with disabilities?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Can you repeat your question?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Does the DOE have, what is the DOE's position on the AI detection tools given documented false positive rates that disproportionately flag multilingual learners and students with disabilities?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Does the DOE have an approved AI detection tool or policy regarding AI detection tools?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: AI detection tools, Scott?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: You could just stay.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Just stay, Scott.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We should pull up 13 more chairs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Fair enough.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: We do not have any AI detection tools.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: That I would be aware of that we have?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: That are detecting if a child...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Uses AI to write questions, answer questions, write papers.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I think I'm getting tripped up on your question around students with disabilities and multilingual learners.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The detection tools creating false positives saying particularly students with disabilities or multilingual learners are being flagged disproportionately as using AI with false positives, meaning

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: accusing a student with an IEP or multilingual learner of using AI when it has not been detected properly.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But it sounds like the DOE does not, according to your testimony, doesn't utilize AI detection software or have any AI detection software vendors approved.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But you do have plagiarism detection tools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Those have been around for many years, where students upload their papers, and it detects for plagiarism, correct?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Yes, I am aware of plagiarism detection tools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So you don't have any such detectors approved for use in disciplinary or academic integrity hearings?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I mean, you know, even before AI, we have very, you know, high standards for academic integrity.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We are not using a detection tool, but we want to make sure that our

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: teachers are very closely connected to their students and they understand that this is their product or not their product and how to make sure that the students are, you know, responding in a way that is theirs and not, but in other words.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I mean, it sounds, I'm sorry to interrupt.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It just sounds to me like you're talking as if it's 2015.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You're talking in terms of like, you know, good teaching.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But we are in a completely different world with the advent of AI.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And tomorrow it's going to be completely different.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And in two months, it's going to be astoundingly different.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks what training and certification DOE provides teachers for AI tool use and detection of AI-generated content. Junta describes training tied to curriculum programs like HMH, Amira, and Zurn. Dinowitz presses on whether HMH includes required AI-specific training, which Junta admits it does not.
chapters.title: Dinowitz questions teacher training and certification for AI tools

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And so I just want to ask one more question.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'll turn it over to my co-chair.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You spoke about teacher intervention, teachers looking at the work, knowing their students.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What's your...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What training does the DOE provide?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What certification does the DOE provide?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: How are you actually measuring whether teachers are trained in any use of these AI tools that they are then being asked to use with students or in detection of any AI-generated content that their students are turning in?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: For training, I will invite Dr.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Pate and or Tara to join in.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: But what I would say is some of the programs that we rely on most are connected to our Tier 1 curriculum or supplements to it.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And so the training for all aspects of the program is part of like the New York City Reads and New York City Solves professional learning plan.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
