utterances.text: So there's centrally designed professional learning.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: There is district level.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And then there's school-based.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So if a principal or a superintendent or centrally we're recommending a program that would be good as a supplement or an intervention program, it's going to come with training, as does

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: all the aspects of that program.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So, for example.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: You know, one of the programs that we know and we've gotten, you know, a lot of questions about and feedback is within HMH, there's writable.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We also know lots of our schools adopted Amira for some supports in literacy and Zurn in math, just some examples.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And so...

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Nobody would be asking teachers to engage in those programs without training.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: But that's also our non-computer-based assessment.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So when it's part of the instructional strategy and either tier one intervention or supplemental, that is all part of the professional learning package that is coming into schools.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz expresses confusion over conflicting testimony about whether student-facing AI is in classrooms. Dr. Pate acknowledges AI is new, describes a central AI guidance training course completed by over 8,300 staff, and mentions partnerships with UFT for teacher-facing professional development. Dinowitz counters that tools are in classrooms without full training or AI literacy curriculum in place.
chapters.title: Dinowitz and Dr. Pate on AI training gaps and digital literacy

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: HMH includes required training for use of AI in the classroom?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: It's not required, but there are some aspects of it that, some aspects of the program that different schools or districts are leading into.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You're asking individual schools to choose and figure out whether or not they should

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Provide this PD to teachers?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: No, but I mean, if any program that a principal, a superintendent, or us centrally brings in would come along with professional learning.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Related to generative AI?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: It depends on the, if that is part of the instructional strategy.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: What is the question behind your question?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm just, your testimony seems to be a lot about like what, you know, what teachers are doing in the classroom, what schools are doing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And it just, I'm just a little confused.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Like AI is not in the class, student facing AI is not in the classroom, but also HMH has student facing AI and they get the training for these AI.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I just, I'm just hearing a couple of different things and I'm just trying to get clarity on it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: FTC, if I could.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Chime in just for a moment.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: FTC, that's a great.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I had initials like that's great.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Chair, thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: We want to recognize very publicly that AI is new.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: And in the largest school system in the country, we are taking it all in and we are navigating this evolving landscape.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: To answer your question more targeted and more specifically, we have created a central-based AI guidance training for staff.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: And to date, we have more than 8,300 staff members who have engaged and completed this course.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: One of the positive things about this particular course on our system, we learn, is that as new information develops and evolves, we get to go in and to update that system and that training,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: and it actually pings educators to...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Tap into the course again that a new module has been developed to further educate them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: I would acknowledge that there are more opportunities that need to be taken advantage of, which is why we are partnering extremely closely with our labor partners, UFT, who are doing cutting-edge

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: teacher-facing professional development that is deeply building their capacity around appropriate pedagogical uses of AI in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So from my standpoint as chief academic officer, it is that we are engaging in a wraparound, rapidly evolving, capacity building

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: approach to what it means to educate teachers around appropriate use of AI in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: And also we are starting to deeply look at what AI literacy should look like, be like for students as they navigate what it means to be career ready,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: technology facing, and also utilize AI technology.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: day-to-day in the work that they do from teachers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Is it perfect?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: It is not.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: But are we aggressively acknowledging that we need to continue to develop and to do that work?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: We are.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The tools, I mean, you said we're taking it all in now, that it's rapidly evolving.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's true, but while we're taking it all in, we're putting it out into the classroom without all of the trainings in place and without this digital literacy or AI literacy curriculum with any sort of evaluative tools to know whether our students are

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: AI literate before throwing the tools at them.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to my co-chair, Chair De La Rosa, and also acknowledge that we've been joined by Council Member Farias, as well as Aviles on Zoom.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa asks if the 6,491 feedback responses will be made public; Junta commits to sharing them. De La Rosa then asks about GoGuardian monitoring software. Strickland confirms some schools purchase GoGuardian to monitor real-time student device activity in class, but a separate Beacon module is not approved. Monitoring does not extend to take-home devices.
chapters.title: De La Rosa questions feedback survey availability and GoGuardian monitoring

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you for the line of questioning.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I think that, you know, what Chair Dinos was just mentioning and the responses is why we see that the

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: parent community has come together across the city and in CECs to ask for a moratorium.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Not necessarily because, you know, because there are concerns, but also because there are huge gaps in our understanding of how the technology is being deployed and when.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And I think we do need the time to be able to wrap our arms around what is happening in our classrooms.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I wanted to ask specifically about, you said that in your testimony that 6,491 folks submitted feedback to the AI guidance, correct?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Are any of those comments made publicly available for us to see?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: They are not currently made publicly available, but we will get them.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: It is our intention to share them.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: To make them publicly available.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Does the DOE monitor student computer use in classrooms through programs like GoGuardian or any other monitoring tools?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Or additional apps.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You take that.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: I got it.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Some schools can purchase and use GoGuardian.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: They use the feature that monitors that the students are on task with whatever ed tech program is happening in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: There is a, and just to be clear, there's a separate module of GoGuardian called Beacon that is not approved for use and is not used in any of our schools.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And the devices, if the devices are take-home devices, it's monitoring them while they're at home too?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: No, only in schools.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Only in schools.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And who has access to that information and what information is available through GoGuardian?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: The purpose of it is to monitor the real-time status of the device as they're in the classroom for that session.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: When it's turned off, all that information is not stored anywhere and used for any other purpose.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So can you just walk me through it?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So my kid is sitting in class, she has her computer open, and she goes to YouTube.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: What do they see?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: She's not supposed to be on YouTube, let's just say.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: If the school has purchased GoGuardian and is active for that session, the teacher can see in the GoGuardian monitor that 22 students in the class are on the HMH curriculum, for example.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Another student has opened another window, and they really should be only on the EdTech tool.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And they can detect which student is on another window.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: They can see that information live.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: I believe the answer to that is correct, yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, and so they can turn the students by attention.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: They can walk by, follow up, and say, please come back to your assigned task.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Are there any other monitoring tools like GoGuardian that are being deployed in our schools?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Any other tools like GoGuardian?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: That's the one that's most prominently used.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: I'm not aware of any others that are used across the system in a large way.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: And again, they're purchased school by school at this point.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I do think that like, you know, that response that I'm aware of causes concern.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We want to know.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And we need to know if there are other monitoring tools.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So it would be helpful if we could get back to the committee.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: If there are others, it would be helpful in us understanding.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa raises concerns that DOE has declined to provide parents comprehensive lists of approved AI products. Junta says DOE is working with superintendents to gather information on what tools are in schools and encourages parents to ask their schools. De La Rosa asks about contract registration through Checkbook NYC.
chapters.title: De La Rosa on approved AI products, parent FOIL requests, and vendor transparency

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So parents have asked about approved AI products that are currently being used in the schools.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It is my understanding that the DOE has declined to provide some of that information.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And that some parents were asking, have parents done FOIL requests for that information?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And why is there hesitancy around sort of providing comprehensive lists?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We are currently working as part of our next phase.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We're currently working with our superintendents and principals to have more of exactly what you just said, which is we need to understand everything that's in our schools,

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: how they're being used, especially what the top ones are, which ones might be leading to some impact that's interesting, because it is also new.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And we do want to encourage schools to innovate and then scale, you know, what's working.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: With that said, we're currently engaging in that process.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: But I would say as a former principal that any tool that I'm using, any curriculum I'm using in my school, a parent has a right to ask and deserves to be answered.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So in cases where parents might have a question about what, you know, my child uses X, you know, I didn't mean Twitter X, like insert any example, you know, can you talk to me a little bit?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: little bit about how it's used, how long they use it, you know, are they using it alongside a teacher?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Those are amazing questions for our parents to be asking.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We encourage them to do so and we expect our teachers, our leaders, our superintendents, and ourselves to be able to answer those questions, especially if we've adopted them so locally.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks about nondisclosure agreements with vendors. Dennis Doyle clarifies DOE has data processing agreements with confidentiality provisions but no NDAs preventing disclosure of contract existence. Junta confirms all contracts are registered in Checkbook NYC and with the Comptroller.
chapters.title: De La Rosa and Dinowitz on NDAs, Checkbook, and contract transparency

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's good to hear.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Does the DOE register all contracts through checkbook?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I will have to get back to you to make sure.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I believe the answer is yes, but let me find out and come back to that question either in this hearing or shortly after.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Does the DOE have nondisclosure agreements with vendors?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: If so, why?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And are those contracts also registered with checkbook or passport?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: At this point, I'm going to turn us over to our Chief Privacy Officer, Dennis Doyle, to jump in.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: I'm not sure exactly what you mean by non-disclosure agreements.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: I mean, we have data processing agreements with all vendors that process student data.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: If that's what you're referring to, then the answer is yes.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: If there's something else.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I mean, I think nondisclosure agreements are pretty standard.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So is it a nondisclosure agreement or not?

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: Well, it depends what we're talking about when we talk about nondisclosure.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So can you describe what you're describing?

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: So for a data processing agreement, which we have with all of our third-party vendors, which we have with all of our third-party vendors,

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: third-party vendors.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: the process student data, there's non-disclosure aspects to that agreement.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: They have to agree to keep our data, our information confidential.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: So that is, in some ways, it is a non-disclosure agreement.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We do not have any non-disclosure agreements with vendors that would prevent us from

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: disclosing the existence of a contract with that vendor, for example, if that's what you were referring to.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, or like what you're procuring the technology for.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: Correct, yeah, we would not have NDAs for something like that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, and again, the question around checkbook and passport, we'll get back to.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I can answer.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Yes, all of them are registered in checkbook and with the controller.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks about the AI playbook planned for June 2026 per DOE's guidance. Junta explains the original plan has shifted due to feedback and the evolving national conversation, saying DOE is not racing into a playbook but carefully building out stronger guidance. Dr. Pate adds the delay is about being responsive to feedback.
chapters.title: Dinowitz and De La Rosa on AI playbook timeline and shifting plans

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The AI guidance states that on page six that a playbook is planned for June 2026.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What is that playbook and is it currently available?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: As part of our original

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: plan.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We were releasing the guidance going through 45-day feedback, and it would result in the next step of a playbook.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: That is how we started this process.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Given feedback, given some additional educational and professional reviews, and given the shifting national conversation, which has really

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: escalated over just the last couple of weeks alone, we are not necessarily committed to

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: making sure that playbook comes out in June because what we are committed to and was part of our testimony is going back to what that like baseline guidance is and flushing that out to be more responsive to the feedback,

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: to take in some of the shifts in national conversation and research that's coming out.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And so right now, the playbook as it was originally sort of part of the original plan is shifting.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I could, you know, invite Dr.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Pate if you want to add to that, or if that's just the answer, we can move on.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: FTC, I think you did a wonderful job at responding to that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: What I would add is that this is not about a rush to get a playbook out for the sake of playbook.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: What we are committed to is galvanizing the voices of community, which FTC talked about, connected to the 6,500 plus feedback that we received.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: And some of that feedback really shared with us a bit of a slowdown.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: In order to really be thoughtful and strategic about what it looks like to ensure privacy, safety, and to ensure that what is instructionally sound for students

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: is incorporated into what our playbook would be.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So the delay is not about getting the job done.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: It really is about being responsive to what the feedback has told us.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: over this past 45 days and to just acknowledge that this is really an iterative process that you can expect lots of loops, lots of changes, lots of shifts as we become better informed,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: as research continues to evolve, and as we have these hearings, which also give us some really good insight about the direction that we need to go from some of your constituents as well.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm going to ask two more questions and then I'll pass it back so we can open.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But I wanted to ask, I do have more questions for later.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to give other people a chance.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks about YouTube access on DOE devices; Strickland says YouTube is generally blocked unless a principal requests it for educational purposes. Dinowitz then asks about Play Prodigy, an app that prompts students to pay for features. Strickland confirms the free version is approved but the pay features are not encouraged.
chapters.title: Dinowitz questions YouTube policy and Play Prodigy app

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Do you all have a specific policy around YouTube?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Scott, would you like to talk about YouTube?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Yeah, the policy on YouTube is it is not available for use.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Generally, it's blocked unless a particular school asks for it to be opened up for educational purposes at the principal level, and we do that when that's requested.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We have heard concerns from parents that there is instruction that includes, you know, go watch this YouTube video on X, Y, and Z.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So we want to have clarity around those type of instructions.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I don't know if you have any feedback on that, but we have heard that from parents.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I mean, I am aware and have seen where YouTube or some video link, you know, is helping bring a lesson to life, tapping into children's like immediate engagement in that.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: That's part of the work that we're thinking about even on screens and screen time that might lead there.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: But I have not seen an example of just, you know, independently go watch a YouTube video.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: That would be under those cases where we're saying that's the kind of work that is in either a whole class or a small group and certainly under the direction of a teacher in the space.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The other thing I wanted to ask, we heard from some of the parents about this app, and I'm forgetting the name, that basically tells students that if they pay,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Like if you enter a credit card and you pay, you gain some powers.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You know the name?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I don't know the name.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Play Prodigy.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Can you discuss if that is an approved application or program that the DOE has approved?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I believe Play Prodigy is not an approved app.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Am I remembering that correctly?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Well, it actually is.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Oh, it is?

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: It is.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: The free version is approved.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: It's been through our process.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: There are other applications that have been approved that sometimes have that feature of pay for more things.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: That's not approved, nor is it encouraged.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: This particular app is only used in a few, very few schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It would be interesting to figure out which schools and for us to see that information.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Obviously, there's concerns there, given the breaches and everything else that has occurred, you know, putting credit card information and all of that stuff at risk, but also making sure that that is not something that we're encouraging.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Can I just clarify one thing and correct me if I misunderstood you?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So there is the version that's free, but we would never require our families to move into the pay.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: That would be a choice that they made, and I would not want to see our schools requiring that or encouraging that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: More on that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it back and then...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We'll come back for round two.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz notes that 6,491 responses represents less than 1% of parents, calling it a very low percentage compared to the parent survey which has significantly higher response rates. Junta agrees and says DOE supplemented with targeted conversations with advocates, labor, parent leadership, and elected officials.
chapters.title: Dinowitz critiques low feedback response rate from parents

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I would just add your 6,000.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Some of the responses is like less than 1% of the parents.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And you have, that's not a question, I'm just sharing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's, I would say, a very low percentage.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm sure many council members will have a lot to say about the people who helped develop the guidance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And that's compared to the parent survey, which has significantly higher, and you do a lot of work, a lot of good work, to make sure parents fill out that survey.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I would think a response rate of less than 1% isn't, I don't know, ideal.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Parents have a lot to say, rightfully so.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is the future of our children, the future of our city.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And I couldn't agree with you more.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I think you were, given the amount of conversation that we're having about it, we thought, I certainly would have thought we had more, but we did intentionally then make sure that part of our feedback process is included.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Specific conversations around this with advocates, with labor, with parent leadership structures, with our elected.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And so, you know, and really making sure we're listening, even the PEP comments became feedback for us.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to recognize Councilmember Zhuang for questions first.

chapters.summary: Zhuang expresses disappointment that DOE was unprepared to answer questions about her bills on G&T and special education access. She asks about the effect of G&T participation on specialized high school admission and cites her own research showing 12% higher admission chances. DOE officials say they do not have the data readily available.
chapters.title: Zhuang questions DOE on gifted and talented program data

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Actually, I'm very disappointed.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: The early childhood education did not show up.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: No, they're here.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: They're here?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yes, ma'am.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Very good.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: On the statement said early child division don't have any purview of gifted and talented program, right?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: But how come you guys have purview here about early child education program, which also run by DOE?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: You guys showed up all mayor's announcement about pre-K, 3K, which are also DOE programs.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And now you are choosing, come to different talent program.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: You guys don't have any purview over?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Is that Terry picking?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Or I don't know how, who gonna answer my question.

utterances.speaker: Veronica Cuellar
utterances.text: Veronica Cuellar, I believe you're referring to the Mayor's Office of Early Childhood Education in the letter.

utterances.speaker: Veronica Cuellar
utterances.text: The Division of Early Childhood Education is involved with gifted and talented, however.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: So no one come from administration side to testify about my bill?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We have members from the Division of Early Childhood from New York City Public Schools with us today.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay, they are here today?

chapters.summary: Zhuang continues pressing for data on G&T program effects on later life outcomes and current program distribution in black and brown communities. DOE officials acknowledge they did not bring G&T-specific data. Suzanne Sanchez introduces herself as Chief of Special Education. Dinowitz instructs DOE to provide answers before leaving the hearing.
chapters.title: Zhuang presses DOE on G&T and special education data gaps

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay, I have questions.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: So who can answer the questions?

utterances.speaker: Veronica Cuellar
utterances.text: Myself and my colleague Liana will support with that.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Only 12.5% of children with disability in New York City schools are white.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: What percentage of tuition payment for students with disability are...

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Oh, this is not for you guys.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: What effect does participation in elementary GNT program have on getting into specialized high school?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Do you guys have information?

utterances.speaker: Veronica Cuellar
utterances.text: We don't have that information at this moment.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: But I did the research.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I have the information.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: They have 12% more chance kids getting to a specialized high school.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And also, that program really benefited low-income students in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And next question, what effect does being a gifted and talented program have on those children later in their life?

utterances.speaker: Veronica Cuellar
utterances.text: We don't have that information at this moment.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Have being in different talent program increased expected wage by 9%?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Do you know, do you have information about what current different talent program in

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: black and the brown community.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Do you guys have any data or you can help me to get some data?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Council members Wang, do you have data for us to share?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I don't want to, I was just going to say we did not bring the team prepared with data specific to gifted and talented today.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We brought the team to speak to AI and screen time more so we can accept your questions and note them and also follow up for the spaces that we're not specifically ready to answer you.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: But it is in the hearings agenda, right?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: About two bills I have today.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: But you guys did not prepare to answer my questions.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: A lot of family depend on the answers.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: They want to know the answers.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: This is how, this shows again why those special needs family get ignored.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And the gifted and talented program get ignored, especially in black and the brown community.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And now even doesn't have people come here to testify.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: This is how we're going to respect those families.

utterances.speaker: Leanna Wright
utterances.text: We do have data publicly available on our website that shows the demographics of students enrolled in gifted and talented programs.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And by neighborhood, too?

utterances.speaker: Leanna Wright
utterances.text: By district, we have the number of students from every district who apply to gifted and talented and receive offers every year.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Can you tell me for the record what's the ratio in Manhattan, what's the ratio in Brooklyn?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm sorry, as you answer, please state your name for the record.

utterances.speaker: Leanna Wright
utterances.text: Yeah, my name is Leanna Wright from the Office of Student Enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Leanna Wright
utterances.text: I don't have all of the numbers in front of me, but we can share the links to where they're posted on the website.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: If no one comes here to prepare, then why come?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I think some of the bill that you were asking about also is from the mayor's office who provided some written testimony in response to your bill as well.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: The response is...

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: That's my understanding.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: It said there's no purview.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Over the gifted and talented.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Said the no purview.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: That's it.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: didn't say why, where, who has the purview.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: If they don't have the purview, we don't know which department can answer my question.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Well, we run the gifted and talented screening and admissions process.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: It is housed within the Division of Early Childhood, and then it is for our children entering kindergarten currently.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: If we don't have the data to speak specifically to that's not necessarily publicly available, we will follow up and get that.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And that's the reason I asked on my bill.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: We need to have that data available.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And everyone says it's not in their purview, no one in charge of this data.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I don't know which department is going to work on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And also, for the disability students,

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Special needs students, no one come to testify.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Just said, we agree on your purpose, but that's no information at all.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: You got support or not support?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Who gonna answer the question?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Council Member Joann, I'm just gonna ask the DOE, there's a lot of you here.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: All with tons of data.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I think before you leave today, I think you can testify to what the data

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: and answer her questions.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I understand you don't have it in front of you now, but before you leave, you should have that and testify to that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And then this will be the last, and then we'll move on to Councilmember Aviles.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: Councilmember Suzanne Sanchez, Chief of Special Education, and I am here to answer any questions around students with disabilities that you may have.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We're going to go on to the next member, but any of the questions that she had previously asked, before you leave today, we'll testify to.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think you could find it between now and then.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Councilmember Aviles on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: I will come back.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Avilés cites the May 2026 state comptroller audit finding DOE lacks written data classification policies, risk assessment, and backup/recovery procedures required by the NIST framework under EdLaw 2D. Dennis Doyle responds that DOE complies with privacy laws, accepted audit recommendations, and is implementing improvements including a formal data classification policy.
chapters.title: Avilés questions DOE on state comptroller audit findings

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chairs, for this important hearing.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I guess I'd like to ask the administration around issues of privacy.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I certainly was one of those council members who is deeply concerned about, you know, kind of this...

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: What we don't know what's happening in our schools related to AI and student use, direct usage of AI.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: So I'm deeply concerned about privacy.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: As you all know, on May 4th, the state comptroller's office released a really scathing audit, finding that the New York City Public Schools maintains no written policies for data classification, risk assessment.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Or backup and recovery as required by the NIST data security framework specified by EdLaw 2D.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: So given that that law was passed in 2014, why has the DOE failed to develop these policies that's making, obviously, these data breaches, which are profoundly concerning, more likely and harder to respond to?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you for that question and I just want to let you know we have our chief data privacy officer with us today who's going to respond.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: I just want to start out by saying obviously student privacy is of the utmost importance to New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: And we did work with the New York State Comptroller on this audit over the last few years.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We submitted a formal response to that audit that sort of explains that we comply with all federal, state, and local privacy laws.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: In some cases, we go beyond what the law requires.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: But that we're always working continuously to try to improve our data privacy practices.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: And while we disagree with some of the findings in the New York State Comptroller's audit report, we did accept and agree the recommendations that were made in that report.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: To some extent, we assert that we already comply with the recommendations in that report.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: And one example is actually, as you're referring to, is the requirement that we align with the NIST cybersecurity framework.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: This is something that we...

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We use, as you've noted,

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: Were required by New York State Education Law Section 2D to align with the NIST Cybersecurity Framework.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: And just a point on that, the NIST Cybersecurity Framework is not necessarily a checklist of requirements for organizations to follow.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: It's a framework for assessing an organization's cybersecurity posture and to continuously evaluate it and to try to continuously improve it.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: And so we have been implementing the NIST cybersecurity framework.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: With that said, we recognize that there are improvements that we plan on making.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: One is to update our data privacy and security policies page, which we will be doing in the coming months.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We will also be issuing a formal written data classification policy.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We do have a data classification policy.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: It's documented in a few different places, but we want to make sure that we have a very clear, formal data classification policy for that.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We're also, as has been mentioned earlier in this hearing, working with the schools to make sure we have a better understanding of the tools that they're using to make sure that it's in compliance with their data privacy and security policies.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: Another thing noted in the audit report was the timeliness of notifications.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We're also improving on that front.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: I think if you read the report itself, in 2020,

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: 24 and 2025, there were five, the state controller found that there were only five of 102 reported data breaches that were not made in accordance with the timeline requirements.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: But with that said,

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We are striving to improve that.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: Part of the difficulty with notification timeliness is making sure that we're having our staff be fully aware of what their obligations are and that they're reporting it up to us in a timely fashion.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We also can encounter difficulties with working with third-party vendors and trying to get the pertinent information from them in time in order to issue these notifications.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We also will have translation requirements when it comes to sending out notifications.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: So there are a lot of hurdles when it comes to issuing these notifications in a timely way.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: But with that said, we're still working to improve that.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: And also on the training point, we've also improved in terms of staff completion rate for our training.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: In 2023-24, it was 117,000 staff members completed our data privacy and security training.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: In 25-26, that number went up to 138,000 of approximately 150,000 or 160,000 staff.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: And this is also an area where we actually exceed the law, where education law 2D only requires training for those who have access to student data.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We require it for all of our employees, which we think is a good policy.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: Unfortunately, that also creates separate challenges in sort of ensuring that everyone actually is completing the training that has access to data so that we're complying with the law.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: So in short,

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: You know, we are working continuously to improve our data privacy and compliance policies and practices.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We've accepted the recommendations of the controller, and we look forward to, you know, issuing these new improvements in the coming months.

chapters.summary: Avilés asks when updated security protocols will be released. Doyle says data privacy and security policy updates will come in coming months, and notes DOE updated Chancellor's Regulation A20 on student privacy for the first time since 2009, incorporating feedback from parents, advocates, and school leaders.
chapters.title: Avilés follows up on security protocol update timeline

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: So just to circle back, I mean, your updates on security protocols are going to be coming out in a few months.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Is that what you're suggesting?

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: On our data privacy and security policies, yes.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: That's one of the requirements under New York State Education Law 2D is that we have that posted on the DOE website.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: It is publicly posted there right now.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We want to improve that.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We want to improve it to make it clearer, more accessible, so everyone has a better understanding of what our data privacy and compliance policies are.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: And I also just want to add, too, along these same lines, we've also, in the last year or just over a year ago, updated our chancellor's regulation, which is Chancellor's Regulation A20 on student privacy.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: That regulation had not been updated since 2009.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: So we worked very hard on that to try and bring our own DOE regulation up.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: to speed with the current landscape of privacy law.

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: We worked with a lot of parents, community leaders, advocates, school leaders, incorporated their feedback,

utterances.speaker: Dennis Doyle
utterances.text: and we feel came up with a regulation that actually strengthens our data privacy practices, but obviously this is a continuous process and we're going to be doing more.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Aviles.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And if you'd like, I'll put you down for round two.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, I'll put you down for round two.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, followed by...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: All by Gutierrez.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Wong asks where parent feedback on AI guidance is publicly available; Junta says it will be made public. Wong then asks about the June 2026 playbook timeline. Junta reiterates the playbook is shifting due to feedback and the national conversation, and DOE is building out stronger guidance instead.
chapters.title: Wong questions parent feedback access and AI playbook

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I went to public school, one to learn facts and also to learn how to think critically.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I sent my daughters to public schools, also expecting them to think critically, not to just type something into an AI engine and then take the results for granted.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I've been playing with this consensus, perplexity, chat, GBT, and I was asking about myself, like, what do you know about me?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And then a lot of times they're wrong.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yes, and I'm concerned because one, the technology is already here.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Kids are already using it, whether you see it or not.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And my other concern

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: is that teachers are using it, whether they're using it to prepare the lessons or whether they're using it to grade student papers.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Those are very concerning.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But I would like to start with one thing.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: When I see students, everybody has AI.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: It's in between the ears.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: This is actual intelligence.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And my message is that they have to use it, rather than relying on some AI engine telling them things and they take it for granted or they believe it's true.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I claim that the given technology, a lot of results you get from AI engines are false.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Now, with that, here are my questions.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I read from the website, DOE website, there's an AI guidance, and then there were parents could submit feedback until May 8th.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And there's feedback somewhere.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I would like to read it.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I want to learn about what parents are thinking.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Where is all this feedback?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can I see it?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: It is not currently public, but we are going to make it public.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you please do that?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Or send me the link.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You have my email?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My next question is, at the same AI guidance, page six, it says, a playbook is planned for June 2026.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: In one week, we're going to be in July.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: What is this playbook?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Where is it?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And is it available?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Yes, that goes back to when asked earlier about the playbook.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: The original intent and timeline, we thought after we launched the initial guidance that by the end of the year, we'd be able to really use the feedback to get us to this playbook.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: But the feedback actually is taking us in new directions, asking us to think about a developmental sequence, what can and can't be used, when it can.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Also, the conversation around screen time started coming up.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We're trying to pay attention to all the feedback that we got through survey and our public engagements.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And so right now, we are not racing into a playbook.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We are carefully considering how to build out the original guidance that puts us on stronger footing with clarity, guardrails, and specificity.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I have some time left.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about policies for teachers using AI to plan lessons or grade papers, comparing it to HR departments using AI to screen resumes. Dr. Pate states assessments and grading are prohibited AI uses, and DOE does not recommend teachers use AI to generate lessons, emphasizing educator input must be culturally appropriate and accurate.
chapters.title: Wong questions teacher AI policy for lesson planning and grading

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, do you have a policy for telling teachers what to do with AI when it comes to planning lessons or grading papers?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because I know, like for even right now, HR departments, they screen the resumes and reject resumes if they don't see a certain term or they don't see a certain experience.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And then applicants get rejected without having anyone looking at the resume, just rely on some AI engine, right?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So what is your policy on...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You have a policy for teachers on how to use AI.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I am planning lessons or grading papers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: I want to make it very clear that in the guidance that was released in March, it really clearly states that assessments and grading are prohibited uses of AI in our New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, what you say is, what about planning lessons?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: What stops a teacher from telling AI, generate a lesson about Revolutionary War in Saratoga?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: I hope they get great lessons.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: I think what we have to do is to make sure that ultimately the lessons are content aligned, they're factually accurate, and they are deeply engaging with the learning that we want students to be doing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: And so we do not recommend nor encourage teachers to be using AI tools to be generating those lessons.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: We want to make sure that that educator input and guidance is culturally appropriate and accurate for those learning purposes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: And to one of the things that you said earlier, we really truly want to make sure that the intelligence is something that we're developing with our students through deep interaction, educator guidance and support, and that is what we strongly believe in.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman Wong.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'll put you down for round two.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Squeeze in one more question, I'll go to round two.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Let's do round two.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Councilmember Hanif.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Hanif asks if any biometric recognition technologies are deployed in schools. Strickland confirms no biometric detection tools are used and that such tools are prohibited by law. Hanif then asks how AI tool necessity is evaluated before introduction.
chapters.title: Hanif questions DOE on biometric recognition technologies

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs, and thank you all for joining us.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I do want to just at the top share that I'm supportive of AI in our schools and, of course, urge intentionality before our schools and students administer these tools.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And I also want to add that I'm one of the signatories of the letter calling for a more

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I believe that the way in which AI curriculum is built should be guided by students and whatever is put in place should be piloted or observed, followed by an assessment

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: and feedback.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: A feedback loop so that we're not just saying, we're not just dropping the curriculum, the plan, and then just moving on.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: We can't avoid AI.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: We need to be on top of it.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Could you share if any biometric recognition technologies are currently being deployed in our schools for educational or security purposes?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I'm going to let Scott jump in on the different applications.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: He just has that.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Yeah, I'll have Dennis back me up.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: We do not have any biometric detection tools.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: And that's prohibited by law.

chapters.summary: Dr. Pate describes a tool scorecard being developed to assess educational value and student outcomes, and beta testing with 50 teachers across grades 6-12 in core content areas. Tara Carozza adds that DOE is currently only evaluating teacher-facing tools, not direct student AI use, due to concerns from the feedback window.
chapters.title: Hanif questions AI tool evaluation and beta testing process

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And then can you share, how is the necessity of an AI tool going to be evaluated before introduction?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Oh, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So as these new tools are coming, the question is how do we, you're asking how we're going to evaluate them.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I'm going to ask Tara or Mia to jump in on some of the work that's underway.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: So both saying yes to specific tool or company and then evaluating them year one, year two?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So what we are working to develop is a tool scorecard that will give us a more in-depth assessment of not only the educational value add, but also impact, meaning outcomes

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: for students.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: We are also currently doing some beta testing.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Could you share just in more kind of clearer language what the testing includes, how that is being conducted?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: It's being conducted through professional learning experiences where

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: some teachers and school leaders, 50 teachers actually, and school leaders are engaged in programs that lend itself to AI-empowered tools.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: And we are assessing whether or not these tools have instructional value add

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: and then collecting surveys, talking to teachers, figuring out the different creative ways that they are utilizing those programs so that we can start to better determine how we can more effectively assess

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: the value add.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: of a tool that may be utilized within New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And these 50 teachers, what's the representation?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So they span from sixth to 12th grade.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: In addition to that, they span our core content areas, so math, science,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: ELA as well.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And will there be another set of teachers?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: The goal is that we, as we develop this scorecard, we want to enlist the partnership of our educators.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: It is really about like how we trust them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: To inform us more about the value add of a particular tool.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: It is not about taking the word of the tool creator to say, hey, this is great and go use it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: No, what are our educators saying?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: How are they utilizing the tool?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: What are their thoughts around the value add of the tool?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: And what are the academic impact outcomes that inform us that this is, in fact,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: a tool that we should invest more time and energy into as a whole, not leaning towards what the vendor is saying is good.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: I would imagine they would all say that they're great and good.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So we can't take their word for law as it relates to the impact.

chapters.summary: Hanif asks if a similar scorecard exists for students. Carozza says DOE is creating evidence-based scorecards for all stakeholders including families, aiming for transparency in evaluation criteria. Hanif recommends including students in the evaluation process.
chapters.title: Hanif asks about student evaluation scorecard and family tools

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Because I have a few more seconds, could you share for a similar scorecard is being a

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: with students?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So I'm going to jump in Tara to do this work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So Tara.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And I'll come back for second round, but ending here.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Tara Carozza.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: I'm the Director of Digital Learning and Innovation, former District 75 teacher.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: Currently, we are not evaluating tools that are direct student AI use.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: We're starting with teachers.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: And the reason for that is because we have the same concerns that you as an advocate and many others have shared with us, particularly from the 45-day feedback window and the listening sessions that we engaged in.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: And so we're starting with teachers and leaders who are already using the tools for the purpose of supporting their productivity, for supporting their lesson differentiation, scaffolding.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: We already have high-quality instructional materials and grade-level expectations within all of our New York City Reads and Solves curriculum.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: And so we're looking at those standards that we've set.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: We've vetted those curriculums.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: We know they're research-based and evidence-based, and we're taking a similar approach to vetting our tools beyond IRMA.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I would recommend our students seeing some of this, being able to work with a tool and for us to

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: to see how they're responding so that they're also part of this process.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: Agreed.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: And we are creating the same evidence-based tool scorecard for all of our stakeholders.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: We're in development of those, so I did want to add that.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: One for families as well, so we can empower families to go through the same process.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: So we're all looking at the same evaluation criteria.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: We're not saying, okay, the DOE is doing one thing and not sharing that out.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: We really want to be transparent in our next steps forward with all.

utterances.speaker: Tara Carozza
utterances.text: And of course involving students and empowering them with that same agency and critical evaluation and critical thinking as Councilmember Wong shared.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Hanif.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I particularly love the questions around evaluations.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: FTC, give her a highly effective in 3D, please.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Very niche joke.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Gutierrez.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Gutiérrez, a moratorium signatory, criticizes DOE's process as backwards—releasing guidance before collecting feedback—and says principals feel forced into using AI tools without preparation. She demands a digital literacy vision and desired outcomes from DOE before guidance or playbooks are issued.
chapters.title: Gutiérrez critiques backwards AI guidance process and lack of vision

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I have a few questions, and I was one of the signers on the letter.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: At first, I wanted to go through the guidance thoughtfully and was able to meet with some parents, I think some who are here to testify and really listen to their concerns.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think the first thing about the guidance was that even the process seems backwards, where you put out a guidance, you're collecting feedback, and now you're going to use that feedback for a playbook.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It just feels like...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: The process doesn't make sense.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: If you wanted to actively use feedback, you would have

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: called for feedback first and then put together a guidance.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: A playbook, I don't know if a playbook equates to the same as guidance.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I just, I want to make sure that like, I don't know, I feel like we're creating something that we don't necessarily need to do at this point.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So I just want to make sure that I raise my concern with the process.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It really feels backwards.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think in speaking with a lot of parents from my district, from my community, especially for those that are younger, that are in elementary school, there's concern for the fact that

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: They are kind of being brought to the table months after some of these tools are already being introduced.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: The top concern being with these tools being introduced into the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I'm sure you'll hear from folks that are testifying that today, but bigger picture, I think that we deserve a vision, like a digital literacy vision from the DOE.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I feel that we deserve to know what are the expected outcomes that you all as a governing body of all our public schools expect to have before you start throwing around a guidance or a playbook or mandating vendors.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And I will say from just the day-to-day school.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: feedback, I think a lot of principals feel like they're being forced into interacting with these tools.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think a lot of schools feel like they're not prepared and they're being told from the top, from Tweet, that they have to utilize this.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So a lot of this has been very fragmented.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It has been fragmented from when you put

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: the guidance out.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It is being fragmented from the way that you're implementing it.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So I would just love for you all to share if there is a bigger picture, what is the desired outcome so that we can tell people here publicly because there are concerns.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So yes, we want a moratorium.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: We want to slow it way down.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: But I think a lot of that is your fault.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think a lot of that is

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: because of how you have laid this out.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So what can you tell me now that's gonna help us feel like we can trust and feel calm about the next four years under this administration and what you guys have laid out since March?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: That's really important feedback.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And I think

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: What I would want to mostly offer here is I don't think we disagree.
