utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: You know, the prior administration, you know, hit the gas on go with AI and technology, and we were coming out of the gate.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: This was very shortly after Chancellor Samuels came in.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: You know, AI has been a moving train.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And so one of his...

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: First priorities, in fact, I think it was a week after he established his new cabinet.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We started on the 5th.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I think we put out the guidance on the 24th-ish.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And that is because we recognize that this guidance, this train was moving.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And we wanted to put in guardrails.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And back to Councilmember Wong's question, some of the...

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Answers to your question are what is in red, what is in yellow?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: What is in green?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: That answers some of the specifics.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We need to get more specifics, so I want to acknowledge that.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And I think that's why we are also saying today and sharing with others, like, we're not moving fast into this playbook.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We want, you know, Chancellor Samuels.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I'm sorry to interrupt.

chapters.summary: Gutiérrez asks whether the original March AI guidance could be tossed after the playbook process. Junta says DOE stands by the March guidance but acknowledges the need to build more detail and specificity. Gutiérrez expresses frustration with the fragmented implementation approach.
chapters.title: Gutiérrez asks if March guidance will be discarded

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: If you could answer this in your continued answering is what is the role that the playbook is going to play that the guidance that you all put out did not

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: where we hoped i think the original intent and this was you know uh back to the original plan dr pate if you want to jump in on the original intent of the playbook was to take this feedback and make it very much like answering

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: um to like the questions that were coming in and feedback and realignment but i think we need to go further than what the playbook's intent was going to be and kind of go back to the guidance and ensure that we're doing that engagement

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: that you're talking about.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: This is a core value of Chancellor Samuels, of our mayor, of this administration.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We believe that we do need to reset using the feedback and also the whole conversation around AI and has shifted nationally since our guidance went out.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I'm not saying it was our guidance, but it is important for us to take all of that in so that when we get to a vision for the role that technology, screens, and AI, because they're all intersecting but different, that we're able to offer you that.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: And I can share with you now.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: That is the current process that we are engaging in as a team.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, can I just ask one last question?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Did you want...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think it's a yes or no answer, actually.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Is there a world where after this playbook is done, the AI original guidance that you release in March is tossed, is garbage?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I don't think so.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think we stand by our March no.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thanks, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Gutierrez.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: While I can appreciate you're not moving fast into a playbook, you want to be thoughtful.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think the evaluative tools and the feedback from parents, students, educators are important.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: These tools are in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So it is, it's astounding that both of those things are true at the same time, that we've already released these tools into the classroom without the playbook, without the guidance for the students and the teachers that are really required

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: before engaging with these types of tools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I will now recognize Councilmember Joseph, followed by Maloney.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Joseph asks where parents are in the AI conversation, whether they are made aware of AI in classrooms, and if they can opt in or opt out. She also asks about language access for non-English-speaking parents and who the two leads are on AI digital learning. Dr. Pate describes superintendent-level parent engagement.
chapters.title: Joseph questions parent engagement and AI awareness

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Great, thank you, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: As we're having this important conversation around AI, we've been in this space before.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Where are the parents in this conversation?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I heard nothing about parents.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I heard teachers, students, educators, but I have not heard

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Where is parents in this conversation?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Are they made aware of AI in the classroom?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Do they have the option to opt in, opt out?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And another part of this conversation, I'm not hearing language access, and we know a lot of our parents in the city, English is not their first language.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And I have a question for the Deputy Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Who are your two leads in this AI digital learning space right now?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Who are your two leads in your office?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Within the, thank you for your questions.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You're welcome.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: You want me to lead off with that one?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Is that Mia and Tara?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Within the division of the first deputy chancellor in academics and instruction, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Pate and Tara are leading that work.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: But that work hinges upon the partnership with DIIT, the partnership with data privacy.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So these gentlemen also are very integral in this work.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: These things cannot...

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Happen without each other.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: That I know.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You're preaching to the converted.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So I've asked my question.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So tell me, how are you taking into consideration when you deploy AI tools, how are parents made aware?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How are we using parents' voices?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Parent engagement.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Education cannot happen without parents.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Where are the parents in this conversation?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: In addition to the AI guidance and the feedback loop, superintendents were provided with on-the-ground resources, and superintendents did their own engagement unique to their communities

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: where they utilized the different governance structures within their district to engage families, to collect insight, to collect their feedback, and then they provided us.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Those trends, the themes, and that was incorporated in addition to the 6,400 responses that we had.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Which we still consider as a low number as having the highest, the largest school system in the country, 6,400 is very little.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So here's my question.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: When you took that feedback, what were the three top things that stood out for you?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So the top three concerns that stood out from the cumulative information that was gathered was around cognitive offloading, was around safety, and around privacy.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And that's been our theme, privacy, cognitive, and development.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: It's a national theme, so yes.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So we're going to put that there with the parents.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So you know when you do your playbook, council members, Gutierrez said you should throw it out.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: But parents have to be center of this conversation.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And what about our educators?

chapters.summary: Joseph asks for a timeline to ensure every educator receives AI training, noting some students are 'running circles around their teachers' with AI. Dr. Pate acknowledges no timeline is set yet and agrees technology moves faster than training development, describing it as a challenge of chasing capacity building.
chapters.title: Joseph on teacher training timeline and AI literacy gaps

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What PD is being provided for them?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Because I also know.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: that some of these curriculums also come in a hybrid model where you have a technology component to it.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How is that being used and how are teachers being trained?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So we spoke a little bit earlier about the two-tiered approach that we're taking now.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: We have developed AI guidance at the central level, and our educators are engaged in that training.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So to date, more than...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: More than 8,300 staff members have taken that information and that course.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: In addition to that, on the other side of it is that we are partnering with our labor partners who are doing some really, really great work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Even myself, I've attended their capacity building training.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Tara has attended the capacity building training that the UFT is offering.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So that is another way in which educators are being trained very skillfully around that work.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What's the timeline to make sure that every educator somehow went through at least one session of these classes that they're not navigating?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Because I know some kids, I could tell you, are running circles around their teacher with the use of AI.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And they can find other detectors that can make sure that the product that they produce is AI.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You cannot detect if it was used through AI.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And I know quite a few.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Yes, so I have an 11th grader at home who is currently providing professional development to me connected to...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: All that AI has to offer and then some.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So I do agree with you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: around the need to continue to build capacity.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: We have not set a timeline as of yet that I can offer you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: But the technology is moving faster than you're working.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: By the time you go to sleep, somebody has created a new one.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: And you are absolutely correct.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: And there lies the challenge in it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So I don't disagree.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: I am agreeing with you that because of the rapid evolution of technology, it sometimes feels like we are chasing the development of professional learning and capacity building opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: But we are actively creating and developing that.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks how DOE addresses biases in existing AI tools. Dr. Pate acknowledges there is no endorsed tool that tracks bias, calling it a 'mega problem,' and says DOE is looking for resources. She emphasizes AI literacy development and awareness that AI can be wrong as the primary approach.
chapters.title: Joseph questions DOE on addressing AI bias

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And one thing we want to address with AI, how are you addressing biases that do exist in existing AI tools now?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So currently there isn't a solid tool that we have endorsed that tracks or trace or keeps account of bias, but we do know that it is, again, a mega problem that we are looking for.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Resources that help us do that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: But we don't have a solid tool that we are currently utilizing to engage in that work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: I think where we can really kind of dig our heels in is around AI literacy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Development and awareness.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: I think people have to understand that it can be wrong.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: It is not all

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And it says that too.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: If you're depending on the prompt that you type in, it will say this answer may not be accurate.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You should do further research on it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So the development really comes through awareness to your point.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So the more we get that message out to educators, we get that message out to school leaders, we get that message out to students.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: By spreading that message, we start to build an awareness that you shouldn't put everything and trust it all as is.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: You still must continue to critically think.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: You must engage with AI technology, not as a consumer, but as a producer, meaning that you are asking it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Questions that will probe your thinking, not to deliver and to give you blanket answers that may or may not be true.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So it really is about building awareness around a rapidly evolving, as you said, every day.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: It's a monster at this point.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Yes, it is.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about supporting English language learners and students with disabilities. Suzanne Sanchez, Chief of Special Education, describes the irrefutable evidence base for assistive technology for students with disabilities, noting it follows careful IEP evaluation processes and goes through IRMA vetting. Dinowitz clarifies this is distinct from unvetted AI tools in classrooms.
chapters.title: Joseph and Sanchez on supporting ELL and students with disabilities

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And how do we support our English language learners and students with disabilities?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And I have the rap.

utterances.speaker: Scott Strickland
utterances.text: You can answer that last question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: So I do want to invite our friends from Dial and Multilingual Languages to just kind of add a value-add piece to that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: Thank you, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Pate
utterances.text: P.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: Pate, Suzanne Sanchez, Chief of Special Education, and thank you, Councilmember Joseph, for the question on students with disabilities always at the forefront.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: We know what you're doing and hope you're going to visit PS6 Seed this summer, but that's another conversation.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: Students with disabilities benefit significantly from the use of assistive technology, and that is something that New York City Public Schools is committed to, ensuring those students have the right technology in the right cadence, in the right dosage, at the right time.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: Chair, you brought up earlier around research and efficacy studies and the evidence, the body of evidence on technology for students with disabilities is irrefutable.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: And it's important to note that technology for students with disabilities, what typically referred to as assistive technology recommended on a student's IEP,

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: follows a really careful and thorough evaluation process by an expert in that field who then recommends the use of a specific AT tool.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: It could be a device, it could be a software, it could be an app, all of which go through the IRMA vetting process.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: Research is very clear that children with disabilities who utilize AT not only see growth and improvement in academic outcomes, but mental health, social, emotional as well.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: as we remove barriers to learning, students start to feel better about themselves, and that is certainly seen and felt in our students with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, but I just want to clarify for everyone listening, the specific nature of what you're talking about, AI, you're talking about an individualized...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Education, well I know what it stands for, I wanted to have like a little gravitas and you know emotional cadence to the word individualized.

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: Get your gravitas in.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Individualized education program.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Was the dramatic pause there, was that good?

utterances.speaker: Suzanne Sanchez
utterances.text: It was great.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Because what you're talking about is something akin to providing extra time on the test and what we're concerned with is providing tools that give the kids the answer on the test and the cognitive offloading.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I just want to clarify that for anyone listening today that absolutely if there's a specific tool for a specific student

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: to ensure that they have access to the same educational opportunities as everyone else, that is not the same thing

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: as throwing AI tools into the classroom with no vetting or little vetting, relying on vendors and corporations to provide guidelines instead of listening to parents, students, and educators.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks why DOE does not have a required digital literacy curriculum given decades of social media and media bias concerns. He argues DOE should not wait for state guidance and should ensure digital literacy and civics education happen in all schools, not just provide opportunities.
chapters.title: Dinowitz questions lack of digital literacy curriculum

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I do want to get clarity on something because I keep hearing this reference to is this digital literacy.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Is there not a DOE-approved digital literacy curriculum?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I mean, we've had issues with digital literacy.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We've had for well over a decade social media.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We've had bias in the media.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We've had, when I was in high school, in the 1900s, we had...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It makes me sound right.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Oh, I see some other people who are also old as hell like I am.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That we had lessons in curriculum just like this because the internet was coming of age and anyone could put anything on.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So how is it that we don't have a digital literacy curriculum?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: How is it that the DOE isn't requiring this?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And yes, update it as necessary, but how is this not part of the general curriculum in our schools?

chapters.summary: Dr. Chang responds that state law requires media literacy and that NY State Ed is updating guidance to include AI. He describes Future Ready NYC partnerships with industry and higher education, and emphasizes civic readiness as essential. Dinowitz pushes back that DOE should not wait for the state and should set expectations for all schools.
chapters.title: Dr. Chang on media literacy, civic readiness, and Future Ready NYC

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: So, Chair, I think this particular point, the dramatic pause around individualized, is extremely important, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: At no point in time do we ever want for there to be any tool that shortcuts thinking that our students are doing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: We know that they have to do that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: I know you don't want there to be.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm not asking what you want.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm asking what the policies are in place because we want the same thing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I don't think you don't.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I just want to be clear on that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: And I want to answer your question about digital literacy because you are correct.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: State law requires that we are thinking about media literacy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: We're teaching for accuracy, biases, and facts.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: Back in whether that's the Internet or Wikipedia, as students are writing their research papers,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: it is a part of the curriculum that teachers are really thoughtful about engaging students in details about how we write research papers, how we're defending our facts and our citing where our work is coming from.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: And so New York State Department of Education is actively updating that guidance to specifically require information around use of artificial intelligence.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: And we are working with our higher education partners to learn together with them how we're addressing this particular issue about teaching not only, I would say, artificial intelligence literacy,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: but I would also argue that there's a real civic readiness that we have to be prepared for.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: Because as AI really infiltrates and our work is going to be more and more

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: works into our entire society, it is dramatically shaping how we are confronting

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: facts, how we're engaging in discourse, and it is extremely important that when we want our students to become leaders in our society, that they're working closely

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: with each other, with researchers, with our teachers, to be able to learn about this information and to be able to use it appropriately that is enhancing what it is that they're learning inside the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I love everything you said about what our students need to learn, what our teachers need to do, except

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: that you don't need to wait for state guidance to do it.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You are empowered as the Department of Education to determine what goes into our classrooms.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There are limitations and requirements, yes, but you have programs all the time.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You have civics for all week, and you spend just a week on it.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Just a week on civics to highlight what's going on perhaps through the year, but you have no...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: In no way are we saying that we are waiting for the state to do that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: I said that we were collaborating with state ed to make sure that that guidance is updated.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: We have tremendous examples of what is happening across our schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You have examples.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I know you do.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I know you do, and I've seen it, and I've spoken to it.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to thank you for help setting up those meetings about discussing the seal of civic readiness.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There are incredible things going on in our schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And it seems time and time again.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You sometimes provide support for that and you celebrate it, but there doesn't seem to be.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: an expectation that all of these wonderful things you're talking about happen in our schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And it does not seem to be a clear evaluative tool to ensure that even if you do expect it in our schools, that our teachers and our students are engaging in this type of learning, the very type of learning.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Trust me, I know you know, and I know that we need

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: in a society so now that seems so like at each other's throats that we absolutely need civics education, absolutely need digital literacy, but it is your job to ensure that this is happening in our school, not just provide the opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: As a supervising superintendent that works with our high schools, that is helping to ensure successful implementation of Future Ready NYC, as one example where

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: we are working with industry partners, with our teachers, with college professors to make sure that is a key component.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alan Chang
utterances.text: And it is data that we are monitoring as part of our instructional work to make sure that students are prepared for this.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to Councilmember Maloney for questions.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Maloney asks what changes are expected in the revised AI framework and when it will be released, noting distinctions needed between teacher and student use cases and age appropriateness. Junta says DOE is working furiously but cannot commit to a date, and agrees agile governance with feedback loops is needed.
chapters.title: Maloney questions revised framework timeline and age-appropriate AI

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: First, I want to reiterate the urgency of this conversation, because as has been said, AI is already in the classrooms, it's already in our lives, and that includes the parents, the teachers, the students, the principals.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And we all agree on the need for AI guidelines and frameworks and age-appropriate use of AI in schools and in life, while preserving critical thinking, accuracy, student privacy.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And I want to flag that we need to be moving a lot faster.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Because while we are slowing down, AI development is not slowing down, workforce is shifting, and people are going to be using this in everyday life.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So your testimony notes that there's changes that need to be made to the guidance, and some of them include

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: distinctions between teacher use cases and student use cases, more rigor around the age appropriateness for use of the tools, how you introduce AI, at what age.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So what changes are expected and when will we get that revised framework?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: So those are all of the things that we are exploring and hope to be able to include in upcoming guidance.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: We are working furiously through that.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I cannot commit to a date, but we will be offering clarity on those exact items.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks what system exists for continuously revisiting AI guidelines given the technology's rapid evolution. Junta agrees the iterative process with feedback loops must be baked into the next guidance evolution, and DOE officials emphasize ongoing engagement with educators, parents, and students.
chapters.title: Maloney on agile governance for rapidly changing AI technology

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And a follow-up question to that is this is a very agile technology that is going to require agile governance.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So what is the system for continuously revisiting these guidelines and the protocols?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Because this is rapidly changing and we can't have a line in the sand.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: It's going to have to keep moving.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So what's your thought on the process for that?

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: I mean, I couldn't agree more.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Junta
utterances.text: The way it's moving, we have to bake into what our next evolution of our guidance is, this iterative process that includes feedback loops and coming back to it in some sort of time frame.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Those are part of the conversations we're having right now.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Krishnan asks how DOE teaches students to use AI responsibly, critically, and ethically—not as a replacement but as a tool to interrogate. DOE officials describe AI literacy as essential, and Tara Carozza details a 15-lesson high school module on AI concepts and ethics, with K-8 support planned for the coming year.
chapters.title: Krishnan questions AI civics and responsible use education

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And as that, oh, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I want to be able to add that also, you know, you and many others have spoken about here is that we're doing this together with our educators, with our parents, and really with our students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And that that is going to be a really important part of this and ensuring that we have processes that are regularly helping them understand, but also give input and insight into this work so that we can update and continue to be able to revise this work.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I would expect that the next set of guidance will have details on how we'll continue to iterate.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And on the point of AI civics, I completely agree with the points that were made earlier.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I'm going to make assumptions, but most, if not all, students are getting access to smartphones.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Their parents and teachers are getting access to these tools, and they're readily available outside of the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So how are we teaching students to use AI responsibly, critically, ethically, not becoming dependent on it, viewing AI as a tool, not a replacement, and critically evaluating that distinction

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: to interrogate AI, not just operate AI?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I know you mentioned this before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yeah, and I think that that is truly a conversation and some of the most important things we could be focused on in education today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And, you know, in an age in which it is

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: so readily available for us to be able to get access information, but we can't understand whether or not that is officially true, or to be able to participate in our democracy requires that we're educating and working with each other on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We have a number of teachers that have been very thoughtful about thinking about what this, it's not just literacy, but really working deeply on evaluating the information that they are getting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And we look forward to sort of working with our labor partners, with our school principals, and really being able to sort of showcase and demonstrate what that can look like so that we can help support that happening across more and more of our schools.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I would echo the, oh, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: I wanted to jump in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: As Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: Chain, Future Ready NYC is within him, and Future Ready NYC and the Office of Student Pathways this year did create a high school instructional module for students, which was 38 teachers delivered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: It's a 15-lesson module covering AI concepts, ethics, societal impact, and connection to Future Ready Pathways.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: That's happening at the high school level.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: And then for this coming school year, our computer science education team, which really historically has done a lot of the capacity building and developmental work around computational thinking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: They helped co-create our state standards on the computer science and digital fluency standards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: They will be doing a K-8.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: support with teachers as well as instructional modules that aren't just use AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: It's really looking at the conceptual concepts across age and developmental grade bands.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: So they will be implementing that within this school year.

chapters.summary: Krishnan emphasizes that high school graduates will be expected to know how to interact with AI tools in the workforce, and urges the next guidance to include specifics on age-appropriate AI civics. He acknowledges the challenge of building the plane while flying it but stresses the need to move faster.
chapters.title: Krishnan on workforce development and age-appropriate AI introduction

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Just to close out, Chair, in the next iteration, I would love to see those specifics about age developmental AI civics and how we're introducing and probing students as their experience with AI evolves, too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And similarly, workforce development, where college graduates certainly and high school graduates are going to be expected to know how to interact with these tools.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So I'm in the camp of we need to be introducing students at an age-appropriate level with a lot more details to what we're in the guidance.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: But we are going to be building this plane as we fly it.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Into the future.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So it's a problem that we're far behind on and will continue to be because of just how fast this is evolving.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Maloney.

chapters.summary: Avilés asks why a vendor for the MOSL teacher evaluation algorithmic tool is listed as 'vendor not disclosed' in the Local Law 35 report. DOE officials explain it is an algorithmic tool predating generative AI and say they will follow up on whether it was developed internally or by a third party.
chapters.title: Avilés questions undisclosed vendor in Local Law 35 report

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I have a few more questions.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I wanted to ask, so local law 35 speaks to vendor disclosure, and it says that all agencies need to report the use of AI tools.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And the latest report identified seven tools used by New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I won't go through all seven.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: But there was one that says annual professional performance review measures of teaching leadership.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Calculation.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Vendor not disclosed.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Is there a reason why this vendor is not being disclosed?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of what?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yeah, I'll start and maybe you guys can continue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So that's the Mosul process that's been in place a long time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I think the date on that has started maybe 2008, 2013.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's not really AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We put that into the report because it's an algorithmic tool.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It does.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Make observations and then there's scoring that happens in the background.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So that's what the reason it's in there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's an algorithmic tool that came way before generative AI hit the marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: MOSL is the measure of student learning, which is part of the teacher evaluation process and principal evaluation process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Well, not MOSL is in the teacher space.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: But regardless, why can't that vendor be specifically disclosed?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yeah, we'll get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's possible it was developed internally, but we'll get back to you if there was a third-party vendor or if it was developed internally.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Okay, that would be great.

chapters.summary: Avilés asks about the Chief Privacy Officer's role at OTI, whether DOE uses master service agreements, and whether OTI's citywide AI guidance binds DOE. Doyle explains DOE works closely with OTI on IRMA and cloud reviews but is subject to separate laws under FERPA and EdLaw 2D rather than the city's identifying information law.
chapters.title: Avilés questions OTI relationship and procurement process

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I wanted to also ask, I know OTI is not here, but in your perspective, what role does the chief privacy officer in OTI play in the operational process?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And does DOE utilize a master service agreement procurement process for NYC schools?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm going to bring in our data privacy to speak on his role and his work with OTI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, I can speak a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Thank you for the question about our relationship with OTI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We work closely with OTI when it comes to the IRMA process and the data privacy and compliance process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: One of the parts of that process is to have third-party vendor products undergo a cloud review conducted by the Office of Technology and Innovation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And we will also work closely with OTI and the Chief Privacy Officer's office at OTI in responding to any cybersecurity incidents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We'll work closely together in sharing information about the incidents and make sure we're responding accordingly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I defer to somebody else on the contracting aspect of that question.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Anybody else?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Master service agreement procurement process?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm going to have to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I don't want to misspeak on that.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: OTI has a citywide AI governance, the AI action plan, and the generative AI use guidance.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Does OTI's overall citywide guidance bind the DOE, or does the DOE believe itself to be outside of OTI's authority?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I don't believe we see ourselves as outside of OTI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Would you have any more insights to share?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I think we generally comply with OTI's guidance when it comes to issues like cybersecurity and data privacy.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And if there's a conflict with OTI's suggestions or guidance, who governs?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think it would probably depend on the subject matter area.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I mean, for example, there's the city's identifying information law, which is administered by OTI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: That's something that the DOE is actually not subject to because we're subject to separate laws under FERPA, New York State Education Law 2D.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: So I think it would depend on the subject.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Depends on subject.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you for clarifying.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yeah, I would just add a bit to this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We, to the extent possible, love to be consistent with what OTI wants, both technically, legally, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Every once in a while, as Dennis mentioned, there's things that are over and above on the educational setting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Also, there's the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Situation where the DOE is really an agency of the state and the mayoral control, it can be removed at any point and therefore we need to be able to operate independently.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But we do like to follow what they're doing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We work with their cyber command a lot on general

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: security, and then specific incidents, we work cooperatively together.

chapters.summary: Avilés asks how many privacy complaints DOE receives annually and typical outcomes. Doyle says he does not have specific numbers but describes the investigation and notification process. Avilés then asks about parental consent rights under the Parents' Bill of Rights for Data Privacy and Security.
chapters.title: Avilés questions parent complaint process for data breaches

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I wanted to just ask a little bit about the parents'rights.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Under the Parents'Bill of Rights for Data Privacy and Security, parents have the right to file complaints about possible breaches and unauthorized disclosures of their child's personal identifying information.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: With New York City schools and with the New York State Department of Education.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Can you, how many complaints do you all receive per year?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: What is a typical outcome of those complaints?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: How are they sort of followed along?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I don't have the specific number in terms of complaints that we receive from parents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: If we were to receive a complaint from a parent about an unauthorized disclosure, we would investigate it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And if we determine that personally identifiable information had been subject to an unauthorized disclosure,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: we would follow our normal protocols where we notify the state of the disclosure and we then make sure that any impacted student is going to receive a direct notification of that disclosure.

chapters.summary: Avilés asks how parents can exercise the right to require written consent before education records are disclosed. Doyle explains DOE has ready-made consent forms on its website, and describes FERPA exceptions allowing disclosure without consent, such as to school officials with legitimate educational interest or in health emergencies.
chapters.title: Avilés on parental consent forms and FERPA exceptions

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: The DOE's data privacy and security policy states that parents have the right to require written consent before personal identifying information in your child's education record is disclosed.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: The policy also states that in some cases, the law allows disclosure without parental consent.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I wanted to ask if you could clarify how a parent can exercise the right to require written consent before their child's education record is disclosed.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And is there a specific form that parents must submit to require this consent?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: So we have forms on our website that are designed for parents to provide consent for disclosure of education records.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: They're not required to use those forms as long as we have the consent in writing from the parent and it meets all the requirements under the requirements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: FERPA, meaning that it identifies the information to be disclosed, who it's going to be disclosed to, the purpose of disclosure, and it's signed in data, we'll accept that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: But we do have a ready-made form available for parents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And then the first part of your question was, oh, for just requiring consent in general.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: The general rule is that consent is required before any student information is disclosed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: There are, however, a number of exceptions to that general rule under FERPA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: For example, if you're disclosing student information to other school officials, it could be other employees within the school.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: That does not require consent as long as that disclosure is being made to another school official who has legitimate educational interest in receiving that information.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: That's just one of the many examples.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I mean, there's a health and safety emergency exception, for example, if a student has a medical emergency, needs to be taken to the hospital.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We don't need to get consent from the parent before we start disclosing that information to, you know, medical technicians who are going to be treating the student.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa questions Doyle about the state comptroller audit finding that only 73% of employees completed required privacy training in 2024, and that DOE could not verify which employees with access to student data had completed training. Doyle reports 138,000 of approximately 150,000-160,000 employees completed training in the most recent year.
chapters.title: De La Rosa questions privacy training completion rates

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you, co-chair.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to talk about this state controller audit that was raised previously by Councilmember Aviles.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You familiar with it?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, good.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That was an easy one.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You spoke about the training previously, about the required training.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Part 121 requires annual privacy and security training for employees with access to personally identifiable information, correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: That's correct.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And the DOE requires employees to complete annual privacy trainings, correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So the report, the controller found that in 2024, only 73% of employees completed that training.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The audit found the DOE did not review the completion list to verify that employees with access to personally identifying information had completed the training, correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: That was the finding in the audit, yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That was, so it is correct.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So the DOE...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Could not prove that every employee with access to student personally identifying information completed the required annual training.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: True?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So that was from 2024.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Does the DOE know today whether employees that are not completing annual trainings have access to personally identifying information?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: No, I mean the difficulty with this and this is

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: is an area where we actually, as I said before, we actually exceed what's required by Ed Law 2D.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Ed Law 2D requires training for anyone with access.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We require it for all employees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: The difficulty that we have with this is we do not have a list of all of our employees that identifies in that list whether they have access to personally identifiable information.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But as part of your policy, you require all employees to...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: To take it regardless, yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And what percent of employees have taken this training?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Our most recent numbers from the most recent school year, we had 138,000 employees complete the training.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: What percent is that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I would have to get back to you on the exact percent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think it's out of roughly 150,000 or 160,000 employees.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So that's not 100.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: It's not 100%.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But I mean, if your policy is all employees and you can't identify which employees have access to personally identifying information, I guess it makes sense to just train all employees.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And that was in a report issued two years ago.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So even with your own numbers, and your own requirements, not every employee

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: gets this training, correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Every employee receives the training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Whether they actually complete it is a different question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I mean, we strive to have 100% completion, and we're working towards finding ways to make sure that we get closer and closer to that number.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: It's extremely difficult.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Given the number of employees we have, we have employees who are going out on leave, employees who are coming on to the table.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You said roughly 130,000 out of 160,000 completed?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Was that what you tested?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: 138,000 roughly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Ballpark, yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, so you have 22,000 teachers going on leave, not completing it?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Not necessarily teachers, just DUE employees.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Sorry, DUE employees, you have 22,000 teachers a year going on leave?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Well, there's some flux in that number, but again, not all of those employees necessarily have access to personal identifiable information.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Right, but...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You don't know which employees have access.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So you made a smart decision to require all employees to complete this training, but it doesn't sound like a requirement.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It sounds like a desire.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So what steps are being taken to ensure that the adults, the employees that have access to this information, get the training that you require?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: It is a requirement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Whether or not we can actually make sure that everybody 100% completion is the challenge.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We send out the annual training every fall, the beginning of every school year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We give a deadline to complete the training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We send out multiple reminders to staff who have not completed the training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We'll send out reminders even after the deadline passes, continuing to send reminders along those lines.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We part of the updates to the Chancellor's regulations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: A20 was to require supervisors to make sure that their supervised employees are completing the training.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And when were those updates to the Chancellor's regulations made?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Those were made in May of last year.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So the only change between 2024 when this report was released and they said that this was an issue and now is that you told supervisors to tell their employees to complete the training?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: That was a change in the regulation, but we're continuing to try to increase our training completion percentage through updating awareness, guidance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We send out principals digest messages, superintendent guidance, superintendent digest messages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: So we're constantly trying to make sure that our staff are aware of their obligations and that they're meeting those obligations.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It sounds like a huge, it just sounds like a huge gap.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And this is a report from two years ago.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yes, please, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Chang.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Chair, let me jump in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Please.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And I want to say that, you know, as somebody who supervises our superintendents and therefore our school-based staff, we take full responsibility to ensure that those adults who are working with personal identifiable information,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: we are doing everything in our power to make sure they're getting that training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And that is the priority staff, and we will work with our team internally to make sure that that gets done.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, do you have goals for yourself?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I mean, you can call them smart goals, whatever acronym you want to use, but you're at like 80-something percent now.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It was 73%.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: two years ago, do you have goals that you're setting for yourself in terms of ensuring that this, as we know, AI is changing rapidly, it's getting into the classrooms, more and more vendors, I mean, this is important.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So what are the goals that the DOA has set for themselves to ensure that it is actually a requirement

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And to me, that means 100% compliance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, I think the goal is 100% completion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I mean, that's what we're striving for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And we recognize the difficulty in getting to that number, but we're constantly trying to get to that number.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa asks about confidential weaknesses in system monitoring, unsupported systems, and firewalls identified in the comptroller's audit. DOE officials confirm changes have been made or are planned but decline to disclose specifics for security reasons. De La Rosa presses for status updates on each recommendation.
chapters.title: De La Rosa questions audit findings on technical control weaknesses

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, I want to move on to my other questions, but I am interested in hearing what steps since 2024, what changes you've made besides saying something different in the Chancellor's regulations and sending out more emails in Principles Digest is being done to do this

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: critical training that was identified in the Comptroller's report, the audit.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The audit also identified weaknesses in DOE's technical controls that were not disclosed publicly because they were confidential, correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yes, that's right.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So is the DOE in a position today to share more information about those weaknesses and what it's done to remediate them?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I don't believe we're in the position to disclose.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think part of the reason why that portion of the audit report was kept confidential is because there's information in there that could potentially jeopardize our information technology assets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Security posture.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Like your mom's made a name last four of your social and first pet's name?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Something like that.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Something like that.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But what that means to me also,

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'll say the audit states the weaknesses involved system monitoring, unsupported systems, and firewalls, correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yes, that's right.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And have any changes been made to your systems to improve system monitoring and firewalls and other unsupported systems?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I could defer to DIT on that, as far as I know that work is underway.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The answer is yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The changes have been made or we're talking about doing the work?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Both.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Tell me more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I don't really want to go too far past what has already been disclosed with the areas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And this is...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This is common practice in a security, the security side of an audit, that some of the information is not disclosed, disclosed publicly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But it was disclosed to us, and to the extent that we said, yes, we agree, and could make the technical changes to improve security, we've either planned to do that or have done it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So this, again, this is a report, I just...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: From 2024, and they're in a confidential report, you just shared that there were specific recommendations made in the confidential report.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: In those two years, in addition to whatever changes that you are implementing,

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: outside of the recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: In those two years, have you implemented the recommendations made by the controller's report?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Sorry, can I just interject?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Please.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: What is the 2024 date that you're referring to?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I have a report.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The data was from 2024 in the report.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So how many of those?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah, so, Chair, please, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'll try to parse this because I would like to be able to answer the question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But let's say there were five or six recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Without disclosing specifically what they were, I think it would be appropriate for us to look at those and provide back as much information as we can about the status of each one of those.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And I think, I mean, this is all part of a pattern, right?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I mean, everyone from the DOE.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And the council, I think we care a lot about the same things, but what we're interested in is the implementation of a policy that keeps our kids safe and keeps our kids learning.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So I want to turn over second round to...

chapters.summary: Wong asks specifically which AI tools principals, teachers, and administrators are using. DOE confirms ChatGPT, Gemini, and Copilot are used by staff, while Perplexity, Claude, and Consensus are not widely used. Wong asks about licensing and whether anyone has evaluated the quality and accuracy of different AI engines.
chapters.title: Wong questions which AI tools DOE staff are using

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: To Councilmember Wong.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Based upon what I hear today, AI tools are already being used inside DOE, either by administrators or like principals and teachers.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you tell me which tools are being used right now?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Was there a vetting process on the right, like how did you choose these AI tools?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please answer that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I want to answer it in a few ways.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: All of the tools, the digital tools that we are...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That we have a long list on our website of a lot of digital tools that have been through the IRMA process, which Scott could probably speak to where to find it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: In terms of what's actually being used in schools, we are working currently with our principals and superintendents to gather that information.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Our current guidance is where we struck the fine line that this must have been approved through our IRMA process and adhere to all of our privacy trainings and regulations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And so at the adult level, there's tools that adults in our charge have adopted, are using for teacher-facing, leader-facing uses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And then we are also gathering what are the top, all of the different applications that are being used in our schools that the student facing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We're doing that now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But as we're collecting that, I just want to be clear, there is a bottom line expectation that these, whatever is in use, is through the IRMA process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And if not...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Wait, let me be really specific.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Are you using like, with teachers, principals, administrators, are you using ChatGBT?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm sure some are.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: What about perplexity?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Perplexity.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is it AI engine?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Not widely used.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How about Claude?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: C-L-A-U-D, no?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Not widely used.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How about Gemini?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How about consensus?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How about co-pilot?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Do you have to negotiate licenses for them, or you just open it up and start using?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because in my understanding that you may need commercial licenses for the site use of these AI engines.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you talk about that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Maybe some larger providers like Microsoft and Google have free versions of their Copilot and Gemini and also have paid versions that have expanded capabilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: In both cases, those products have been approved, and central offices and schools can purchase the advanced features, and staff can use the free features.

chapters.summary: Wong presses on whether DOE has compared quality of results across different AI engines and whether commercial licenses are needed.
