DOE officials say Microsoft and Google products have free and paid versions, both approved, and emphasize human judgment and fact-checking are expected with all AI use.
chapters.title: Wong on AI licensing and quality comparison across tools

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That is based on existing licenses you have with Microsoft and Google?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But no one really looked into the engine.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is this giving me the correct results?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Or, I mean, anyone ever looked at that?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because the quality of these AI engines vary.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Anybody look at that and say, Gemini may give us better results than chat GBT?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: It's like comparing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I mean, our full expectation is that these are very limited use cases and that human judgment always has to be a core part of this, particularly when it comes to instruction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We want to make sure that it is always aligned to the curriculum and the standards that we expect and that there are much more trusted sources that educators and teachers should be using for those purposes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We do know that there are some cases where administratively, for efficiency sakes, where they can be useful, but even in those cases, it is absolutely critical.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That all the adults that are using those tools are fact-checking and ensuring that everything that is being produced is accurate.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Council Member Hanif.

chapters.summary: Hanif raises concerns that Amira, a reading support tool, collects biometric voice data from students. Tara Carozza explains Amira uses student voice interactively for reading fluency practice, and under the data processing agreement, all data must be deleted after one year, hosted in the U.S., and not used to train other models.
chapters.title: Hanif questions Amira's collection of student biometric voice data

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I had asked about whether public schools were collecting biometric data.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And while the schools may not be retaining that information, it's my understanding that Amira

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: which is being used for reading support, is collecting biometric data.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And is that something you all know about?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: What was the conversation with Amira around the storage of data specifically?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to Tara, but I really appreciate you giving us also the opportunity to share how that is used.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I mean, I think that, you know, reading is one place where this technology is, I think, going to be a lifeline.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: But in the same vein, if student voices are being collected and we're not sure where that

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: might be going to, who it is being used by aside from Amira, that is a problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: Thank you for your question, Tara Carosa.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: So Amira is using student voice in an interactive way with their AI agent in order to provide reading fluency practice, feedback targeted towards that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: that individual students needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: In terms of our data processing agreement with Amira and with all of our vendors, there's a one year data

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: And I'm going to pass it over to Dennis, but I'll share, you know, as I understand it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: After one year, all data has to be deleted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: It has to be hosted within the U.S.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: And there is no use of student PII or biometric data that goes out to any other large language model used to train any other models used to be monetized.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: So using any form of student data is not allowed to go out based on our data processing agreement.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And that agreement, can that be shared?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tara Carozza
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it to Dennis as the chief data privacy officer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, we generally don't share our data processing agreements publicly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Why is that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: It's just like any other internal DEWE document.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We don't share it publicly.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I mean, it's really hard to then trust.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: There is a portion of the agreement that is posted publicly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: It's the supplemental questionnaire.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Does it include this piece about the deletion after one year and commitment?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yes, it does speak to how the vendor will...

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I would love to just be pointed to where that is.

chapters.summary: Hanif asks whether reading comprehension is improving with Amira and requests evaluation data. Dr. Pate says longitudinal data is not yet available but shares that students using Amira up to three times weekly for 10 minutes showed about 4-5 months acceleration in reading comprehension compared to non-users, based on last school year's K-2 data.
chapters.title: Hanif on Amira's reading comprehension evaluation data

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I also want to know on the reading comprehension piece, has there been a sort of evaluation to understand if reading comprehension is improving?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I mean, I know that if I was using this in the fourth and fifth grades, I would be a much stronger reader.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I struggled with reading, and I think voices...

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: like hearing someone else speak to me and it would be so helpful.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: So I'm also curious, how are you evaluating how this technology

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Is delivering?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So currently, due to the new nature of Gen AI Empowered Tools like Amira, we don't have that longitudinal data.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: As of right now, we have it in lower grades that we can share the current impact in NYC public schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But part of this tool evaluation framework and part of our research approach moving forward and understanding how tools are being used within our local communities, within our urban school district and the rich cultural

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: diversity of New York City public schools, we need to do that work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Again, we need to see what the vendor says.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This particular vendor does have a very large research base.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: They've done their own RCTs.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And what are they?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: what's their kind of, like, what do they say that a student can learn reading by?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You mean like a grade level?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Yeah, like for anybody who's, for the students who are using this tool to strengthen their reading comprehension, like by what stage of the school year

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: are they meeting that threshold?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I can follow up with the exact data, but I'm going to tell you what I remember off the top of my head.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: In terms of students, it was students using it up to three times a week for 10 minutes a day, showed about a four to five month acceleration in reading comprehension improvement compared to students who are not using it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And that's for last school year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So we can share that exact.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That would be great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So you can see that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Again, that's in the earlier, that's K-2, where, you know, we're looking at screen time, we're looking at what use, but Amir is not a student sitting in front of a computer and just interacting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's within the instructional program of a class.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's within the instructional program and high-quality instructional materials at grade level of our New York City Reads program, and it's a supplemental tool.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That's not the core instruction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The teacher is the core instruction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: When I've visited schools that are using it, it's in small group.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's extremely limited, and it's not just one-to-one for an entire class period.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So I do want to clarify that.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I think that's fantastic.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And if there are other data sets that you all have, which I hope you're keeping track of in terms of curriculum

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: and information retention or improvement of a specific skill, I would love to get a hold of that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Okay, we'll follow up with what we have.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa follows up on Hanif's questions, asking why data processing agreements are not shared publicly. Doyle says it is DOE policy not to post internal records. De La Rosa argues it is a choice and says the Council expects to receive the agreements, noting no NDAs prevent disclosure per earlier testimony.
chapters.title: De La Rosa demands data processing agreements be shared with Council

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: One quick question for Mr.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Doyle.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I just wanted to clarify.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You said you don't, follow up on Councilmember Hanif's questions, you don't typically share the data processing agreement?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yes, that's right.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Is that a...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You don't typically do it, you can't, or the DUE won't?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think it's, as I understand it, DUE policy, we don't make publicly available or publicly post internal DUE records or DUE contracts.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But that's a policy that's a choice.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And so it would be a choice to share that information with the council, correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I would have to defer probably to our records access unit on that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: They handle the disclosure of records.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I imagine it's okay because you didn't sign an NDA.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: According to testimony, so I'm sure it's fine.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So I think we can expect at least the council to have those data processing agreements because this also came up, you know,

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: a lot of concerns about this particular program using CLAW, using other platforms to process its data, which leads to a whole other mess of issues.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So we look forward to seeing those agreements.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The council looks forward to seeing those agreements.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Councilmember Zhuang?

chapters.summary: Zhuang asks DOE for data on special needs students by zip code, borough, race, and language access. Suzanne Sanchez says much of this data is publicly reported in City Council special education reports and offers to review it outside the hearing. Zhuang notes 75% of students with disabilities are black or Latino.
chapters.title: Zhuang questions special education data by zip code and demographics

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I have some questions about my bill for disability.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Thank you for coming to testify.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I just want to know, do you have the number of kids in different zip code and the different borough needs, special needs kids?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: How many numbers, Maggie, by zip code, by borough, by race, and by language access?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So we do have, I believe, most, if not all of that data that's publicly reported in City Council's special education reports.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And we can, I don't have them at the top of my head, but we can certainly go over with you that data at a time outside of this hearing if that's helpful.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Zhuang cites data showing 71% of private school tuition reimbursement for special education went to white families, while only 12.5% of students with disabilities are white. Sanchez acknowledges the equity concern and describes efforts including inclusive districts in underserved neighborhoods, 900 intervention teachers, and expanded assistive technology access.
chapters.title: Zhuang on racial disparities in private school tuition reimbursement

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And also, by race, I look at the data.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: It's 75% students with disability in public school system are black and brown, black or Latino.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And also, for the private school tuition reimbursed went to black and Latino students.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Only 24%.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And most of tuition reimbursed, 71% going to white students.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Is this data correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So in terms of the students who are by demographic ethnicity in public school settings, I think you said 75% are black?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Black or Latino.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: A little bit closer to 50%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: A little bit closer to that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: However, I do want to acknowledge your question and your comment and be very clear

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: that the Division of Inclusive Accessible Learning, which is a division I work in, but New York City Public Schools at large and certainly this mayoral administration are very focused on equity and integration of public schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Our chancellor has said very clearly about true integration, and that includes students with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So this is something that is very, very paramount and something that we are working on across the board as well.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: So what's for the last year, city has been reimbursed for the special needs kids education more than $72 million.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: 71% went to white family.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: But in New York City system is 12.5 of students with disability in New York City schools are white.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: What is city doing to ensure students with disability who are not white get?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: The help they need.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you for asking that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That is a focus of our work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And just a few of the things that we have done as a city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: One, I want to share that we have opened fully inclusive districts for autistic children in neighborhoods and communities that were historically under-resourced and unserved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And in those districts, every student who's coming into kindergarten receives a seat at a high-quality program close to their home, one of our specialized programs called AIMS or Horizon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And those were intentionally opened in districts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: 5, 12, and 14 just to start because those districts often don't get, they're sometimes districts that don't get resources needed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We have also hired and trained over 900 intervention teachers who focus on literacy

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: and reading for children, and those teachers work primarily with students who have historically been marginalized, black and brown students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That is a primary focus in their work as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Another thing that we have done is built out our assistive technology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There used to be certain neighborhoods where you saw a lot of assistive technology requests and then other neighborhoods where you saw very few.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: few and we've intentionally done training and outreach to families and teachers and school staff in those districts and we've seen those numbers really rise so leveling the playing field around literacy

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: assistive technology for our students and building programs in communities that historically have been under resourced all of that said council member we certainly have work to do

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: and I'm excited about our work with First Deputy Chancellor Janta and her team on this kind of equity and inclusion for all students moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We know that we have what's called an over-referral problem of black and brown, specifically boys, being over-referred for special education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We're really clear on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And that is something that is paramount, training our school psychologists, training our school leaders, and working through that so that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: the right children get the right services the right way, where and how they need them, not based on race, ethnicity, or family income.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So lastly, I just want to share one other thing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We opened 80 called sensory gyms, seed sites across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: In the past, before we opened these four years ago, we continued to make more and more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We started with 10.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This year we ended with 79.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Before that, only families who had the money to pay privately or had the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: opportunity to sue New York City public schools got that kind of service and support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: What we did is we built 79 of these that are free of charge in neighborhoods so families can access that for free and not have to sue or pay privately.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So we are working at this very important issue from a lot of different ways.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I have one more question.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I have one more question.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And also, I represent a large immigrant community.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: A lot of time, parents come to my office saying, hey, I have to travel one and a half hour to bring my kids to Queens to go to a special needs school.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And do you have data to show how many kind of kids in New York City have this type of situation?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yes, we do have data on students who are enrolled in District 75, which is the citywide district for children with some intensive disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And very often, those children are going to school far from home because the program is not located in their neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And what we are building and the three districts that I mentioned, and we are expanding our specialized programs to be close to home so children don't have to take the bus, get up at 7 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And take the bus for an hour.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But we do have data on those programs as well as students currently enrolled in District 75.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Do you also have the data for the family?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Because I represent large immigrant family.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: A lot of family doesn't speak English at all.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: From the data I see, research data, not DOE data, saying one of five kids'family speak English as a second language, special needs kids do not have service.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That is not the data that I have, and around one in five families who are not English speakers not receiving services.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Data said never receive services.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Never.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: If those are children who've been evaluated for service and never receiving it, I would very much want to look into that because that's not something that we see in the data set that we have.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And because a lot of times the family doesn't speak English, they don't know how to access the data.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Even they do not know where to report this situation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yeah, and we have, there's a lot of resources that we have for families who do not speak English,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: or even families who are new to New York, how they can get services quickly for their child without even any documentation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And we would love to have conversation with you, any other council members, on what we should do more, better, to get that word out to families, because those services do exist, and we want families to have

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: feel that they can ask.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And sometimes we hear that families are fearful to ask based on sometimes immigration status or if they are not comfortable with the English language.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And we want to do everything possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And if we have to do, we certainly would be willing and happy to do more to reach more families.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: One thing I do want to say since we have the first deputy chancellor and her team here is that the first stop for any family is their school.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And if a family is not receiving service support that they need, the first place that they should go and be supported is in their child's school as well.

chapters.summary: Zhuang shares constituent stories of immigrant families traveling 1.5 hours to Queens for special needs schools, and asks about data on students facing long commutes. Sanchez confirms DOE has District 75 data and is expanding specialized programs closer to home to reduce busing burdens. Zhuang also raises language access barriers for immigrant families.
chapters.title: Zhuang on busing challenges for special needs students

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And in 2024, September, on the hearing, we already said out-of-borrow bus routes for students with disability usually two hours away.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Two hour one way, four hour every day.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: When this type of situation happens, city permits bus routes up to two hours each day.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: In borough routes without IEP, mandated travel time is one and a half hour.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Is there any way we can fix this issue?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So in terms of the busing issue, and I know you mentioned children with and without IEP, I will just say that as a system, the best way for us to fix that issue is to continue to open

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: more specialized programs for students with disabilities in their home and local communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That's something we've been advocating for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The chair, Dinowitz, has heard this very often from us, and we know these programs work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And that is our best way to resolve part of the busing issue is not to put children with disabilities on buses to travel an hour from their home.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: If we have all the service in the neighborhoods, then it will be much easier.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: If we can have the data, it's easier too.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We will share that District 75 data with you.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa reads from DOE's website stating IRMA does not evaluate algorithmic bias, then reads another section stating the legal team reviews bias. Doyle confirms the legal team does not review bias, calling it a possible website error. De La Rosa instructs DOE to correct the website, which has been public since March.
chapters.title: De La Rosa questions IRMA bias review and website discrepancies

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to personally thank you because I think you know how important it is to support our students with disabilities, and I just appreciate your legislation on this.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I will remind everyone, according to testimony during our executive budget hearings, that I think we're all in agreement that we do need to open up more specialized programs for students with IEPs, and there's a little roadblock called OMB.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That was the testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: From the last hearing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to go back to the audit, but before that I just want to go to the tool review, the AI tool review.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm a little confused by the guidance that currently exists.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It's on your website, so if you could walk us through, that would be very helpful.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The current guidance says the Enterprise Request Management Application, IRMA, is DOE's privacy and security review process.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The guidance also says that AI tools can't be used with any student or staff data unless they've passed the IRMA process, correct?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: For the record, they nodded their heads yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Oh, sorry, yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: No, it's...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That just means you're good listeners.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That's all it means.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But the guidance says IRMA does not evaluate algorithmic bias, equity impact, or instructional effectiveness, correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But you also say on another part of the website that the legal team reviews bias.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Does the legal team review bias?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: That may be a misprint on the website.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I'm not sure what part of the website.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You should have had AI fact check your website.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Possibly.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm just kidding.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Relax, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I just want to read it to you so we're on the same page.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Legal and compliance review.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: New York City public school teams review legal terms, privacy protection, security measures, instructional value, and AI-specific issues like bias and transparency.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So you're testifying that that's a typo?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Well, I would say transparency is something that gets conducted as part of a legal review, but that's not...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: The same as bias.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I mean, transparency.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The word bias is there.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So you're testing that that is a typo.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: The legal team does not review for bias.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, so it's on the website, so I'm sure your webmaster will go and delete that right now.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But this is the guidance, by the way, that's been up for however long, for weeks or since, was it March it was published?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It's been up for months, so please correct that.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And I also want to add, so IRMA does not evaluate algorithmic bias.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We've determined the legal team does not review bias either, and you'll fix the website.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And New York City Public School is actively building the capacity to review for algorithmic bias.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That is correct that that is something the public school team is working on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Say the question one more time.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The question is, is the website accurate that New York City public schools are actively building the capacity to review for algorithmic bias, which, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Go ahead, Scott.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott
utterances.text: I guess I'll start.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott
utterances.text: For tools that otherwise look like they have a great educational purpose or maybe even operational support purpose,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott
utterances.text: I think we want to extend our review of that to bias.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott
utterances.text: We can't do all tools all the time, but for the ones that we want to use and encourage our people to use, we want to make sure that the bias is also evaluated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott
utterances.text: There's a variety of ways to do that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott
utterances.text: One of the best ways is to make sure the tool uses a curated data set to do its work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott
utterances.text: And we have several tools that follow that kind of processing pattern.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But you don't have a policy that all tools, you don't have a requirement that all tools use a curated data set or just the data that you allow it to use, correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott
utterances.text: There are tools that will use a large language model in their operation, yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: With a large language model, you know, takes from anywhere.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm saying that.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa asks if DOE can commit to ensuring bias and equity impact are part of tool evaluation. Tara Carozza describes an equity and ethics fellowship and collaboration with OTI on bias evaluation, but acknowledges no solid tool currently exists for algorithmic bias assessment. De La Rosa calls the minimum standards 'shocking.'
chapters.title: De La Rosa presses on algorithmic bias evaluation commitment

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Is the New York City Public School currently building the capacity to review for algorithmic bias in all of their approved tools and all the tools they plan to approve?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I think I just located the section that you're talking about in the guidance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And I just wanted to ask Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Pete if she can come up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I actually just quickly sent, I think, the thing that you see.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And I just wanted to know if Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Pete can speak to it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah, I'll read it to you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Bias and equity review.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Irma currently evaluates data privacy.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: New York City public school system is actively building the capacity to also review for algorithmic bias, equity impact, and instructional effectiveness.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But I'm focused on the bias and equity impact piece.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So we started with the instructional effectiveness with the tool evaluation framework.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The equity impact that we're looking at, particularly in...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Particularly in tools, we started with exploring that through our equity and ethics and equity fellowship.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I want to make sure I get that naming right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: A teacher training fellowship over the past year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: In terms of bias and equity impact in tools,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: given the amount of tools and even across the private sector, there are not bias evaluation of tools readily available.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And so as the New York City public school system, we're looking at how we can actually work with our existing big partners on the enterprise level.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And I'm looking at our partners at DIT.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: As well with the directionality of OTI and what the city is going to do with bias evaluation and equity impact because it's a new area, just like the research within AI.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But algorithmic bias is nowhere near a new area.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I mean, it's in AI now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You're asking.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But is...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It sounds like you're going to be having conversations.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Your website says you're building the capacity, but it sounds like you're kind of talking about going to be working with enterprise partners to figure out what maybe, how to figure out what bias looks like.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We're not.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That's kind of what I'm hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I understand that's what you're hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I respect that as well, and I hear that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So let me try and clarify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: In terms of tool evaluation at our size and scale, algorithmic bias is something that we can evaluate for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Doing it at the scale and across the number of tools we have in New York City public schools is something that we have to do in development.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's not something that we have for you right at this moment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It is something that we are thinking through, we're working through, and looking at what's the availability of us in terms of what tools are available to actually evaluate that, the algorithmic bias within all of our tools at the various levels.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We have enterprise tools, we have smaller ed tech tools, we have curriculum supplemental tools, so it's across a wide number of tools.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So whose decision in the DOE is it to approve tools in the

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: first place without discussing or figuring out what the bias or the equity impact is?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The first step of any tool approval is for a principal, a superintendent, or a central leader to demonstrate its educational value and impact.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That's step number one within the IRMA process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So it has to be an educationally purpose-built tool.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That's number one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it on the bias part, not because I'm passing the question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm passing it to Dennis in terms of what our current expectations and our current...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The current IRMA process has in terms of what vendors can share to us, it's not a requirement within our process at the moment, but it is something that we're looking to build into it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So I'm going to pass that to Dennis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks, Tara.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Right now, again, the legal team at least does not evaluate for bias.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I'm not exactly sure which part of the website you're referring to, but if you could get it to us, we could try to correct that because I know there's other parts of the guidance and website that...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Are clear that the IRMA process does not yet evaluate for bias.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And I think when we're talking about tool approval also, we're talking about approval just from a data privacy and security compliance standpoint, which is really just

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: the minimum floor for a tool to be used with student data.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think in order for an AI tool to be approved,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Like it has to go through additional layers of approval beyond that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: IRMA is just evaluating for the data privacy and security portion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But given the explosion of AI and the challenges we face, can the DEA commit to ensuring that bias and equity impact are part of the tools that you use to evaluate these tools, which

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: you've already released into the classroom and already released to our schools, but can that be one of the things you commit to doing?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So let me get to the answer first.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So I just, I have a printout of the guidance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Me too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So the section that you're referring to is under gaining clarity together, concerns and considerations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Earlier in the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Document there is a section three how tools are evaluated and we clearly say there that it uh the irma process currently reviews tools for data privacy and security it does not yet evaluate for algorithmic

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: today's not my day with this word uh bias it is that one word it is it really is

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: um it's a terrible word i just wanted to to show the nuance of this is how our tools are evaluated in section three and that that is under the considerations and sort of next steps for that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And in terms of like bias and tools, you know,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You know, bias lives well beyond our tools as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's part of our commitment around ensuring a culturally responsive and sustaining curriculum.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's been part of our...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: of our investment even in the hidden voices, the work behind the integration in our curriculum, because we also know that just our curriculum, you know, we're always checking, is it representative, is it biased, what other,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: you know, primary source documents can we bring into a curriculum?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So in terms of the ask on the AI tools, I'm just gonna take that as under consideration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I don't, I,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Can I commit to even the capability to do that as part of what we hope to consider?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I just don't want to lock in on the feasibility of how we would be doing that yet.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, I mean, I think you should.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I mean, I think there are certain baselines, and I think what was testified to earlier, it seems as if there's a very minimum standard for approving tools that...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You know, that can pose a danger cognitively, emotionally, academically to our students, and that there aren't certain, that we're not taking this a little more seriously in terms of like, you know,

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: the minimum standards is a little shocking, honestly.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You know, that we are putting the car before the horse by releasing these tools with the, I don't know, the same guidance, it seems, that we're using for any other vendor.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But this is a new world, and we have to really take seriously all of the elements that have been brought up today, including bias and equity.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And I do want to quote, because Mr.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Doyle asked where it was in the

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Armor process, step six.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: According to the website, and I understand it's a typo, but I did want to cite it for you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Step six, legal and compliance review.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The teams review legal terms, privacy protection, security measures, instructional values, and AI-specific issues like bias and transparency.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Number six.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa cites the comptroller's finding that DOE reported 67 of 141 breaches late, nearly half. Doyle explains difficulties with vendor reporting timeliness, staff awareness, and forensic investigation delays. De La Rosa asks what steps beyond training have been taken to fix lapses in notifying families within the required 60-day timeframe.
chapters.title: De La Rosa questions data breach reporting failures

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to end with just more about the state controller's report.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: About data breaches.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I think very concerning to all of us, correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yes, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Good answer.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Oh, we're nodding again.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That's called saying yes out loud.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It's called implementing feedback.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We love to see that.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And, of course, the DOE thinks it's important to report data breaches in a timely manner when they occur.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Reporting the breaches is required by Part 121 of the State Education Department's regulations.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It requires the DOE to notify the State Education Department's Chief Privacy Officer within 10 calendar days of discovering a breach.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The Comptroller's audit reviewed 141 breaches and found that the DOE reported 67 of those breaches, which is almost half of them, late.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Explain why the DOE failed to comply with the law in almost half of the instances that the Comptroller examined.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think as far as reporting to the state goes, when we have a breach,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I alluded to this before in another answer, but part of our difficulty is that we rely on either our vendors to come to us and report this information to us in a timely fashion, which unfortunately does not always happen, or

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: we rely on our staff to come to us and report the breach or unauthorized disclosure to us as soon as possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And I think one of the ways that we've improved on that front, and I think the figures in the controller's audit show that at least we're trending in the right direction, is by improving our awareness campaigns, improving our training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I understand that we are not at 100% completion for the training, but even that number is improving.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We're finding that we're getting more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: More staff that are aware of their privacy obligations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: They're in contact with our office more and reporting more to us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: So I think we're improving on that front.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And it's a tight timeline, I think 10 days to report to the state.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: So those are some of the factors that go into it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: But, yeah, we're moving in the right direction on that front.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So you've spoken about how you ensure or are attempting to ensure that professionals get the training so that they report.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But what about the vendors?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Our vendors are required under our data processing agreements to notify us as soon as they have confirmed an unauthorized.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: authorized disclosure or a data breach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: There are sometimes difficulties with getting that information from vendors in a timely fashion, notwithstanding what they're required to do under the DPA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Sometimes vendors don't contact us at the right point of contact.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: They will, for example, contact a school and not contact the student privacy office so we can affect the notification right away.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But that's not, I'm sorry, but that's not in their contract that in the instance of a data breach, contact.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: It is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: It is, but we still have difficulty with vendors, you know, complying with that part of the contract, and we're looking into, you know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Different ways that we can strengthen our DPAs to make sure that vendors are going to be held accountable to this to make sure that they're reporting it in a timely fashion.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So you drop them as a vendor?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Say that again?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You drop them as a vendor if they don't comply?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I didn't, no, I didn't say that.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I know, I'm asking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Would we?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I mean, we have...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: No, have you?

chapters.summary: De La Rosa details the Illuminate breach where 380,000 families were not informed for over two years. Doyle explains the timeline of forensic investigations and notifications. De La Rosa asks if DOE drops vendors who fail to report breaches; Doyle confirms DOE stopped doing business with Illuminate Education after the breach.
chapters.title: De La Rosa on Illuminate Education breach and vendor accountability

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Have we before?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah, have you, would you, did you?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, in the case of Illuminate Education, for example, that was a data breach that happened about four years ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: There was a pretty wide-scale breach that happened, and New York City public schools decided that we're no longer going to be doing business with that vendor.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So they failed to report for two years.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And then once you found out about the breach, how long did it take?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Take the DOE to report to the state the data breach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I'm sorry, but what was the...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You just said that...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: The two years?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: How long did it take for Illuminate to report to you after the data breach that there was a data breach?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think what happened with Illuminate, and this precedes my tenure and my current role, but

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Illuminate informed us of an incident, but they didn't completely categorize it as a data breach until several weeks afterwards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think it was a few months before they officially deemed it a data breach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And so it was a matter of a few months from the time of the incident to the time that we were officially notified of a data breach.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Well, in the Illuminate incident, 380,000 families were not informed.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: This is what the contract you brought up, 380,000 families were not informed until May of 2024, more than two years after the incident.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And seven months after Illuminate had informed the DOE.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Of the additional students involved, according to the DOE website.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Delays in informing families can have devastating consequences because children are a top target for identity theft since they don't have credit ratings.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: What's the explanation?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I mean, these are inordinate delays.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So what I'm asking about is the delays with the DOE.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: What explains the delays between the DOE and the reporting of this information according to the controller's report?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Well, for the Illuminate case, there was actually, so there was the initial incident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We eventually received the information necessary to make the notifications, which is often part of the difficulty and the delay in getting the notifications out as we rely on the vendors to get us that data as soon as possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: That can often take months.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Forensic investigations have to be completed before we get that information.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: As soon as we got that information, which was about four or five months after the initial incident, we sent out the notifications.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think it was about a year and a half later, Illuminate came back to us and informed us that they had actually discovered that additional students had been impacted

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: and that they had not initially informed us of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Again, we had to go through that process of obtaining the data from them, the whole forensic investigation, and go through our process of matching that with our own records so we can send out notifications, translate letters, all of that, get that done as soon as we could.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The controller found the DOE failed to report breaches to families within the required 60-day time frame.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Obviously, parents kind of act to protect their children's information or work to mitigate damage from a breach if they're not told in a timely fashion that their child's information was disclosed.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So what steps has the DOE taken to fix these lapses?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, that's a good question, too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I mean, I think, again, a lot of this goes back to our training and awareness programs, making sure that our staff are completing the training is very important, and we're pushing to get that, like I said before, to a much higher number.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: No, no, I know.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm talking about when the DOE, not when like an individual teacher or school, I'm talking about when DOE Central gets this sort of information.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Unless it's the policy that an individual teacher or principal submits this information to the state.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We would consider the date of discovery to be the date that the principal at the school learns of an incident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And then sometimes there can be a gap between them reporting that back up to the student privacy office so that we can then turn around and send the notifications.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And when we do send the notifications, when it is a cybersecurity incident like in the case of Illuminate, we also do extend free credit monitoring and identity theft protections to students, which is...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Sorry, you do that or the vendor does?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We do that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Why do you...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Well, the vendor reimburses us for it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa asks if DOE has included penalties in vendor contracts for data breach reporting failures. Doyle says DOE is looking into strengthening data processing agreements to include penalties for not notifying in a timely fashion. De La Rosa calls penalties 'incredibly appropriate' given the scale of student data involved with AI.
chapters.title: De La Rosa pushes for contractual penalties for vendor data breaches

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: At minimum, by the way, at minimum, I feel like there should be a penalty, but...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We do get reimbursed for it, but that's sort of another area where we try to go beyond what's required by Ed Law 2D to make sure that once we do send those notifications that they have these resources.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So in a...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: In addition to the increased training or trying to get that number up to 100%, which is the requirement slash the goal, what is the DOE doing to fix the lapses?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So you've mentioned the training.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: What other steps have they taken?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think we're relying on timely reporting from all of our offices and all of our schools so that we can get this information out as soon as possible.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And there's no, so you're saying there's no issue between once you get the data or information, there is no hurdle, no speed bump, no hiccup for the DOE to then report it to the state?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Well, reporting to the state is one thing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: I think we're talking about reporting.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And to families, yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: If we're talking about notifying families, that's a little bit more complicated because we have to get the impacted data.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We have to understand who is impacted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: We have to then get, if it's a vendor that's involved, we have to get the data from the vendor, then match it against our own records so we have contact information, translate the notices.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: 60 days.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You have a 60-day time frame, right?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm simply asking if there are any additional steps based on the finding of the audit that you were doing to improve this to ensure that you are notifying families in the 60-day time frame.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, and I think one of the things that we're looking into is, and we're always looking into, is ways to strengthen our data processing agreements if we're talking about a data breach that's associated with a vendor to make sure that they are held accountable, that there could be...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Penalties, for example, for not notifying us in a timely fashion.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Well, is that going to be in your contracts then?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Because I think the penalties are completely appropriate for a company.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Besides reimbursing you for paying for families'data monitoring,

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Is that going to be included in your contracts, that there will be a penalty when there are data breaches?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: It's something that we are looking into for including into our data processing agreements, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, I mean, I think that's incredibly appropriate given the scale that we're talking about and the amount of, especially with AI, the amount of student data that we're talking about.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Because we're not just talking about personally identifying information that you guys carry.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We're talking about all the stuff that they're inputting into whatever chatbots or systems that they're using, which goes a lot deeper.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Knowing that, especially since kids use, I hope you're teaching them, they shouldn't, but using these chatbots as mental health counselors and using them as friends and as buddies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Yeah, and one other thing that I should point out too is when we're talking about data breaches and when the controllers report first at data breaches, we're not only talking about data breaches that are derived from third-party vendors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Under Ed Law 2D, a data breach is any unauthorized disclosure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: So if we have a DUE staff member that's sending an email to the wrong recipient and includes one student's personally identifiable information in that, technically that's going to be considered a data breach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: It's going to trigger all the same obligations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: under Ed Law 2D.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: And I think a lot of the delays that we see are sometimes, again, the staff not reporting that to us in a timely fashion so that we can get the notifications out.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it back over to Chair Dena Rosas.

chapters.summary: Gutiérrez asks what compliance standards are referenced in the IRMA security review and who sets them. DOE officials explain the three-step IRMA process: data processing review, DIIT technology review, and OTI cloud review, with standards set by DOE.
chapters.title: Gutiérrez questions security compliance standards in IRMA process

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: When talking about, it says here, paragraph four states that vendors complete a security questionnaire covering encryption, access control, and compliance standards.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: New York City Public Schools security team reviews and approves it.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Can you clarify what compliance standards are referenced in this paragraph?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And where are these standards issued?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Who are these standards issued by?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Are you referring to the data security review process?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Yes, I'm referring to the security check part of it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Okay, I'll defer to DIT for that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Sure, so the review process that goes on within the IRMA process is three steps.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The data processing review, which is legal and working with the vendor, getting the proper documentation, and then DIT,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: does a review of the technology related to the system, and we get into those areas that you just described.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We have security professionals that know how to do that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We have some tools that we also use to support that process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And the third step is a review from OTI Cloud, which also looks at the technology and its security and where things are hosted and make sure that it's secure from their perspective.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: But who sets the standards?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Is it, you know, OTI?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Is it DOE?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's DOE.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, and then I had another question.

chapters.summary: Gutiérrez asks about the prohibition on behavioral monitoring, noting the guidance says it's prohibited but also says DOE is reviewing how policies apply. Strickland confirms behavioral monitoring is in the red/prohibited area and DOE is ensuring it stays closed, not opening it up. Gutiérrez confirms the prohibition extends to device monitoring, emotional recognition, facial recognition, and profiling.
chapters.title: Gutiérrez on behavioral monitoring and biometric surveillance prohibitions

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Let me find it in here.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, here it is.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think Councilmember Hanif asked about biometrics, but we wanted to know a little bit more around behavioral monitoring, which it states that it's prohibited at this moment.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Behavioral monitoring is prohibited in the guidance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That's in the red area in terms of a feature of a system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: But the guidance also says that New York City Public Schools is reviewing how existing policies apply to AI-related collection and use of biometric and behavioral data.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So is it prohibited or are you all...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Exploring or studying or considering biometric use for behavioral monitoring.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So the biometric we talked about is illegal to use.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Behavioral monitoring, again, is red area.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So I think in both cases,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: we might be continuing to look at that, almost to be sure that if any tool comes that begins to encroach on those areas, we point out that we can't allow that to happen.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, so you're not considering it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yeah, we're not opening it up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We're making sure it stays closed.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Got that.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And then, let's see.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Does the prohibition also apply to device monitoring, emotional recognition, facial recognition, profiling, or any other forms of surveillance?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Broadly, yes.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa thanks the administration, summarizes key findings including the need for vendor penalties, tightened reporting transparency, and her two bills on algorithmic tool reporting and data breach reporting. She emphasizes the opportunity to embed digital literacy and civics through Portrait of the Graduate without waiting for state guidance.
chapters.title: De La Rosa closing remarks to administration panel

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We have a number of follow-up questions that we'll submit to you in writing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to thank the administration for coming today.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
