utterances.text: This is obviously a very huge issue that I think everyone here is really concerned about.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We uncovered a few things, I think.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Penalties for these companies that are involved in data breaches is perfectly appropriate, and we look forward to seeing those contracts as was discussed in the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: There's a lot of work to do around tightening up reporting and transparency around these tools, and those are my two pieces of legislation that we look forward to discussing with you further.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And of course, as I started out with, what it means for our students in the cognitive offloading, and Dr.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Chang mentioned civics and digital literacy.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And we at this point in the city's history have an incredible opportunity with Portrait of the Graduate to make sure that is embedded.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You can decide, and we can decide together, to make that a requirement, to evaluate it, to make sure it's happening, and not wait for guidance from the state in order to require us to do it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: This is our city.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: This is our school system.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And we have an obligation to give our kids the best future possible, and that includes all

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: All of the things that were mentioned today includes protecting our children so that they have every opportunity that they deserve.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So I want to thank you once again for coming.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We'll get started on public testimony in just a moment.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa opens the public testimony portion, reminding attendees of decorum rules, two-minute time limits, no video recording from the witness table, and procedures for submitting written testimony to testimony@council.nyc.gov within 72 hours.
chapters.title: De La Rosa announces public testimony procedures

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We have one panel that is self-explanatory.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We'll begin public testimony in a moment.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: If you'd all take your seats and your conversations outside.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I now open the hearing for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: My co-chair and I remind members of the public that this is a government proceeding and that decorum shall be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: As such, members of the public shall remain silent at all times.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: No video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but may submit transcripts of such recordings to the Sergeant-at-Arms for inclusion in the hearing record.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: If you wish to speak at today's hearing, please fill out an appearance slip with the Sergeant at Arms and wait to be recognized.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: When recognized, you'll have two minutes to speak on today's oversight topic.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: If you have a written statement or additional written testimony you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of that testimony to the Sergeant at Arms.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may also email written testimony to testimony at council.nyc.gov within 72 hours of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Audio and video recordings will not be accepted.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And I may remind you throughout the hearing, adults, your testimony again is limited to two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And if you go over, I'll...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We'll just ask you to please submit the rest of your testimony in writing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Our first panel, I always like to call students.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Our first panel is a student panel, Selena Wu, Ariana Ahmed Misha, Yelani Joseph, and Odin Adler.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin when ready and please state your name for the record.

chapters.summary: Yolani Joseph, a 16-year-old sophomore and NYCLU Teen Activist Project organizer, shares an experience where a teacher's AI-generated reproductive health slideshow was full of errors. She calls for AI literacy beyond filling classrooms with products, demanding accountability, clear standards, and human judgment at the center of education.
chapters.title: Student Yolani Joseph on AI normalization without accountability

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: Hello, good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: My name is Yolani Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: I'm 16 years old, a sophomore from Brooklyn, New York, and an organizer with the NYCLU's Teen Activist Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to speak today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: When people talk about artificial intelligence in schools, I often hear one assumption.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: That students either fully support it or don't care about its impact.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: But many of us do care because we're the ones experiencing these changes in real time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: My concern is how quickly AI is being normalized in schools without enough accountability, transparency, or guidance on how it should support student learning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: Recently, my friend and I were reviewing a reproductive health slideshow for class and came across a sentence full of scrambled words, errors, and unclear information.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: Our impression was that the teacher created the classroom content with Notebook LM, an AI tool, and we were shocked.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: Even though we were eventually able to make sense of it, we shouldn't have had to spend time guessing what the curriculum was trying to say.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: Students deserve educational materials that are clear, accurate, and understandable, especially when learning about subjects as important and personal as our own health.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: And that experience reflects a larger issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: So much that it's something that I've also heard echoed when talking to my peers about AI in education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: I've had one of my peers recently tell me, quote, although AI is helpful in some ways, students often use it to cheat instead of studying.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: And when exams come around, students can feel lost because they don't have that sense of security that they did before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: And another student, my friend, shared with me that, quote, her school is shutting down and they're opening up a school for AI-focused in its place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: I love my school because everyone was able to get the attention they needed, and taking that away feels like a step in the wrong direction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: Even though that plan for an AI school was paused for now, what stood out to me wasn't the opposition to technology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: It was a pattern.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: AI is being normalized faster than students are being taught how to engage with it responsibly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: AI literacy has to mean something other than filling classrooms with AI products.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: It has to mean understanding how and when to question these products and protect against the threat that they pose to teaching and learning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: Students are asking for accountability, clear standards that protect student learning, and standards that ensure human judgment stays at the center of education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yolani Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you, Yolani.

chapters.summary: Odin Adeller, an 18-year-old recent graduate, describes how his school's restriction on AI helped him develop writing confidence and critical thinking skills. He opposes DOE guidance encouraging AI for research and creative projects, and raises concerns about students being forced to sign away digital rights to big tech companies.
chapters.title: Student Odin Adeller on writing without AI and data privacy concerns

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Timer be reset.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You start.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You should worry about you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Go ahead and testify.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: They'll figure it out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Hello, City Council, and thank you for spending time out of your busy day to listen to what I'm here to say.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: My name is Odin Adeller, and I'm a leader at the New York Civil Liberties Union's Teen Activist Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: I'm 18 years old and have just graduated Urban Academy Laboratory High School this week.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: I'm here to talk about my views on AI and tech products in schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: At my high school, we did a lot of writing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: And before I went to Urban Academy, I didn't feel confident about my writing skills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: We were also not allowed to use AI to write our papers at all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: That was not a problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Without AI, I could do something else.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Ask my teachers for help.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: And now I feel much more prepared for college.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: In college, which I'll be starting this summer, I'm sure there'll be much to write, and it'll be writing that I'm sure I'll be expected to come up with ideas for myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Fortunately, I believe I can now put some of the many ideas I think about on the paper quite well without AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: That also helped prepare for my high school's frequent class discussions where subject knowledge and critical thinking is essential.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Knowing how to write without AI also helps creatively.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: I'm not sure I would have had as much fun or learned as much in my playwriting class where I wrote up a play that got acted out to my school, but I just used AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: My classes felt much richer due to me and others avoiding using AI to replace thinking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Unfortunately, I've heard that official guidance on artificial intelligence is being sent to schools,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: which encourages students to use AI for research, exploration, and creative projects.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: This is unwise, I believe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: I'm concerned that it will guide those in schools how to use software that is not useful in the classrooms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: In schools, I believe not just the final typing up of a paper, but more, including gathering ideas, should all be done without AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: At school, students should be taught to think for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: I'm sure it's possible people knew how to do so for a while before AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: That is why I'm glad my high school is as restrictive of AI as it is for both the writing that students produce and the creation of the material used to properly teach students to be critical thinkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Support that kind of AI restrictive policy throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: I'm also concerned about the risk of student data privacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: It's required by the government for minus or 10 schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Students should not be effectively forced to sign away digital rights to big tech companies to fulfill that requirement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Instead of spending much taxpayer money on AI and data capturing products, which are not what students need, the city should focus resources on actual human education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you, Odin.

chapters.summary: Arunamid Misha, a rising junior, describes student opposition to AI policies from a weekend event with hundreds of students. She argues AI takes away critical thinking skills, that students want human teachers not AI bots for feedback, and that AI bots create unrealistic perfectionist expectations that destroy student confidence.
chapters.title: Student Arunamid Misha on critical thinking and human teachers

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: Hello, Chair Dinowitz and Chair De La Rosa and members and staff of the Committees on Education and Technology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: My name is Arunamid Misha.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: I'm a rising junior at the Institute for Collaborative Education and a member of the Teen Activist Project at Night Blue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: This spring, I helped plan a weekend event with hundreds of students to listen to their concerns about AI guidance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: And there was a very specific session about AI in that entire ceremony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: And that conversation about AI went so far and so much that it went over time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: And even after the session, we could not stop talking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: About how much we absolutely hate that policy idea.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: And critical thinking is the whole purpose of our education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: That's why we get degrees and everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: But AI is completely taking our skill of critical thinking because they're not letting us work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: So what's the purpose of education if AI is helping us to critical think in some ways?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: And also as students, we want real human teachers to teach us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: And we want real human teachers to give us feedbacks about how to improve our work, not AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: And also by reading our hard-worked assignments, because we worked hard for all those assignments every single day, and to get to know how we think and how we do our works as a human student.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: So as a human student, we want human teachers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: However, for many students today, they instead use AI bots, which tells them how to improve their works.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: And these AI bots are so insanely perfectionist, they keep saying the students again and again to fix their work again and again until infinity, and this makes the students lose all the confidence over their own work, which is really very bad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: AI Pulse, at the end of the day, pushes us into a fake and unrealistic world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: We do not want a robotic policy in this already robotic world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: And also for this reason, I have hundreds of students who care would like to request you to push DOE to make a policy dependent on humans rather than AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: Because rearranging a system after AI is going to destroy it is going to be possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: But the lives which is going to be destroyed after these processes cannot ever be fixed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You timed that really well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: That's perfect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dennis
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Selena Wu, a rising senior who has built machine learning models at Princeton and MIT, opposes Gen AI in schools. She describes how an English teacher's AI grading incorrectly deducted points and falsely accused classmates of using AI. She argues AI makes learning more boring and calls for engaging learning environments instead.
chapters.title: Student Selena Wu on AI undermining classroom relationships and learning

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Hello, City Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Thank you for allowing me to speak today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: My name is Selena Wu, and I'm a rising senior from Francis Lewis High School and a member of the Teen Activist Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: The field and market of AI is growing at a rapid rate, which is why it starts getting implemented everywhere.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: First-handedly, I know how strong artificial intelligence can be.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: In fact, I've spent my past summers researching and building machine learning models at schools like Princeton and MIT.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: This is why I would like to oppose the use of AI technology in a school system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: My comments will focus on the use of Gen AI products.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Firstly, the highlights of learning are the interactions and jokes my teachers make when teaching.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: In my AP Lang class, we start off every day spending 10 minutes discussing different things, whether it is political, philosophical, or controversial.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: These interactions not only creates a bond between the class, but makes the classroom more lively, and it sharpens their critical thinking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: This actually makes me look forward to learning and attending class, which is something that not a lot of teachers and students can experience.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Having to think about complicated topics and shaping them into concrete words are a skill needed for the AP Lang curriculum as well as the test.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Offloading tasks to AI jeopardizes these human relationships and means me, my classmates, and my teachers understand one each other less.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Furthermore, when Gen AI is used in schools, it is often incorrect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Last year, I had an English teacher that graded our essays with AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Not only did his AI incorrectly deduct points from us, it also accused multiple of my classmates from using AI to write their essays.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: However, we wrote these essays in class on paper with a prompt that was given to us on the day of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: There was no possible way for any of us to use AI, and we were unfairly given points getting deducted despite following the rubric.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Compared to my AP Lang class,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: this year, there's a huge contrast between my learning and relationship with a teacher that uses AI compared to one that doesn't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Secondly, I have seen that implementing AI in robots and the education system has made learning even more boring for some students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: The rise of AI chatbots like ChatGPT and Claude has encouraged students to cheat, plagiarize, and break numerous honor codes, but this issue is much more complicated than adults can acknowledge.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Rather than implementing AI into schools and allowing students to further rely on such technology, I think that there should be a focus on creating an engaging learning environment that addresses why students use such technology in the first place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: By encouraging curiosity and making learning more interesting, students may feel more confident, less afraid, and find less of a need for AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Selena Wu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa asks if the students' views against AI represent most classmates. Students confirm that peers who care about their future oppose AI, noting that while some use it for convenience, students understand it impacts learning and the environment. Odin notes AI use is uncommon at his consortium school where class discussions demand original thinking.
chapters.title: De La Rosa questions student panel on classmate views about AI

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I have a couple questions.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Any of you can answer this.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But, you know, I often, I think there's sort of like an assumption that, you know, kids will do whatever to, like, make the work as easy as possible, right?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: If there's, like, a chatbot available, they're just asking, right?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Is it your...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Feeling that your views, that you don't want AI in the classroom because you know how important critical thinking is, that's been your general, you want more transparency and accountability.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Do you think that's the view of most of your classmates, even the ones who use AI in the classroom?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: I think like to understand the issue a lot of time as we are not adults a lot of time it's not necessarily I'm not being weird but not necessarily always important to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: do whatever we want to do definitely it's going to make our works easier definitely it's going to maybe

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: make us less think about it but what's the whole purpose of education is the main question so if we do really want to learn or make ourselves smart or want to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: critical think then we would definitely need a process of education where we need to think about something before answering, not necessarily a bot telling us or

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: doing works for us, because at the end of the day, that is definitely not good for us under any circumstances, because first of all, it basically lacks all the opportunities for us to learn anything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: The whole purpose is learning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: So definitely there must be some students, of course, who wants, not a lot of people, because people who genuinely cares about their future or wants to learn would not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: Follows such kind of things or would do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: But most of the students, whenever I talk with my peers, and as I also mentioned that we had a conference about talking about issues like that, people absolutely hate AI, no matter how much they,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: Some people might say that they like it, but they do not like it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arunamid Misha
utterances.text: They understand that how this is also impacting the environment in many different ways.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Yeah, I believe also at my school is very uncommon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: I don't think I notice anyone using AI, especially, which in general, especially for like the writing and the art that's being done.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Because I feel like when we're writing, like for example, my playwriting class, like this is art that people have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: want to be creative about and they want to share to their class, they want to feel proud of it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Some of it could be connected to maybe issues that they feel, you know, that they care a lot about or personal experiences and it would just be, it would feel so hollow to, for example, use AI for that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: Or in my classes, you know, we have these class discussions a lot.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: My school's a consortium school, so that's probably why, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: If you're using AI to write something, I don't think you're going to be having a very compelling, let's say, defense of your opinions or your ideas, and you're not probably going to be able to engage very well in the conversation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: It's going to be boring.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: And I think students like being able to share their views on various issues and back it up with their own ideas and with their own evidence.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I appreciate your testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Are there any other students in the room that want to testify?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And you filled out a slip?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, so this panel is dismissed.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You can go back to your seats.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And you guys can come up.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm just going to need to know what you're doing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Students, come on up.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It almost reminds me of, like the AI technology is pretty tempting.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It's like very tempting.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It almost reminds me of social media.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Like students, they want to be on social media, but I spoke to a lot of students who were so happy when they banned the phones because it takes it away from them because they want to be engaged with their friends.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So are you, are you,

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: 12 to a student.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Are you testifying?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: In the back row?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, just you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You're the only student.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, please state your name for the record and you may begin your testimony.

chapters.summary: Vinny Dahl, a 15-year-old sophomore, testifies about Intro 684 and geographic barriers to education, then demonstrates how AI detection tools fail by showing a ChatGPT paragraph that bypasses detectors and Chancellor Samuels' speech flagged as 40% AI. He argues DOE cannot effectively regulate AI and detection will harm innocent students.
chapters.title: Student Vinny Dahl on AI detection failures and geographic education access

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: My name is Vinny Dahl and I'm 15 and I'm a sophomore at Queenside School for the Sciences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Today I come here to talk about two things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Well, AI is part of it, but first I want to talk about introduction 684-2026 about geographic location and how it affects students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: First of all,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Education and the quality of education is not the only thing that affects students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Depending on how far they live away from schools, it affects on how well or how much education they get.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: If they cannot come to school on time because of the MTA, they cannot receive a quality education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: These are the omni-cards we are provided.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: When these fail, students cannot receive equitable education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Most students, especially with those with disabilities or special education, often live further away from their schools or often receive more struggles in getting to their schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Now, this...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Inequality can lead to a plethora of issues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Now, I want to reserve the rest of my time to talk about AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Artificial intelligence is not as easy or as simple as we are portraying it as.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Part of the issue is how easy or how undetectable AI can be.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: There is no regulation the DOE can put to ban AI in schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: This is a chat GBT paragraph.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: I can't humanize it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: There's softwares that can help humanize it, which turns a GBT version to something that cannot get flagged by detectors, which teachers often use.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: This is the result after I humanized it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Now, it also, detectors historically does not work and nothing can work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: This, quickly, this is a speech from Kamar Samuels.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: This is it getting flagged for 40% AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: There's no way the DOE can regulate AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: And if it does regulate AI, it will eventually affect innocent students who didn't use AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: That'll be all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You have a very famous alum from the school you go to.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: His name is Alejandro Carvajal.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: He's sitting right next to me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: We're getting a new building.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: I heard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Congratulations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That was all him.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I don't know if you noticed.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: He's building it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: With AI.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much for...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: No, he's not.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm kidding.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: On the next panel, and as always, if I mispronounce your name, I apologize in advance.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You will correct me for the record.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Tania Nasser-Frederick, Edgar Trout.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Travis Prox.

chapters.summary: Nasser-Frederick from the Collaborative Research Center for Resilience and the AI Moratorium Coalition warns that most ed tech vendors are designated as 'school officials' under FERPA, allowing data sharing without parental consent. She raises concerns about HMH's ownership by Veritas Capital, which also builds DHS biometric databases, and calls data processing agreements a cause for alarm.
chapters.title: Tanaya Nasser-Frederick on vendor data loopholes and private equity concerns

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: Hi, my name's Tanaya Nasser-Frederick.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: I'm with the Collaborative Research Center for Resilience, which conducts investigative and community-centered research to ensure the government use of technology advances the public good and democracy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: I'm also speaking as a member of the AI Moratorium Coalition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: The data privacy officer mentioned that schools have the right to share student data with school officials, but he neglected to mention that most vendors in New York City public schools are designated as school officials.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: The Department of Education does list its FERPA-exempt vendors on its website.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: This list includes the mandated curriculum HMH, which hosts Amira.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: Amira has taken a major investment from the investor branch of Amazon's Alexa.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: HMH is owned by the private equity firm Veritas Capital, which also currently owns the company Periton that is contracted to build DHS's biometric database.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: Two companies consulted on the DHS.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: OEs preliminary AI guidelines, Accenture and Salesforce also maintain quiet contracts with Customs and Border Patrol.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: So the data privacy officer needs to better explain why data privacy agreements are not available to the public, because it's a great cause of alarm for parents, teachers, and students who are concerned that student data is being used for third-party product development,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: and even contributing to higher resolution databases used by the Department of Homeland Security.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: The public also doesn't have a way of verifying whether a vendor has undergone an IRMA approval process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: We don't know whether HMH's AI add-ons are writable in a mirror.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: IRMA approved or if they are considered approved based on HMH's pre-existing approval.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: And take a company like iReady that's facing multiple class action lawsuits around the country for wrongly sharing student data.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: Even were there an overwhelming community mobilization against a vendor such as iReady, most districts are too dependent on the product to cancel their contract with the vendor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: The real concern around AI is private equity and venture capital-backed consolidations around private solutions for public education beyond siphoning public spending for the profit of these companies and their vendors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: A district's dependence on a vendor is a threat to democracy and to the kind of community engagement that the DOE claims to value.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: Once the district establishes a relationship of dependency with a vendor, the vendor is operating with impunity.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Edgar Trout suggests distinguishing between top-down ed tech rollouts (where a pause is prudent), bottom-up teacher innovations, learning about AI itself through unplugged activities, and teachers using AI for lesson development. He argues beneficial uses should survive a moratorium and stresses partnership with higher education institutions.
chapters.title: Edgar Trout proposes four AI activity categories for moratorium consideration

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Good afternoon, chairs, committee staff, and the wonderful students in the room.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: I would like you to consider four activity categories when debating a moratorium on AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Category one, the top.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Down rollout of ed tech tools by the central administration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: A pause is prudent here, especially given the insightful questions of this council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: The next three categories, though, should live beyond a moratorium.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Category two is the bottom-up learning innovations by classroom educators.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Imagine a lesson where students tackle an issue in their community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: A Gen AI conversation helps them aggregate public data and stakeholder views for them to discuss, analyze, and critically debate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Category three is learning about AI itself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Imagine a Gen AI tool writes a story, print it out on paper, view it screen free.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Students then learn to uncover any biases in that output.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Today's students must experience this as future tech leaders, policy advocates, and consumers who will vote with their dollars on technologies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Category four, teachers using AI tools to support lesson development.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Imagine a teacher who prepares a supportive lesson after conversing with a Gen AI tool about students'common misperceptions on the topic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Students benefit without FERPA-protected information being shared.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Encourage and support teachers to propose innovations and the AI tools they need.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Rigorously review the ideas from compatibility with regulations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Accompany this with AI-specific professional development.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Strengthen it by organizing co-creation communities of teacher innovators, community stakeholders, and pedagogical innovators at higher education partners.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: I can't stress the higher education portion enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: As we think about what activities live outside a moratorium, we should remember that these technologies will continue to be used in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Our students will graduate, they will become students in college where they'll confront AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: We must be responsible in our use of AI, but we must also not do a disservice to our students in pausing the beneficial uses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Travis Proof from Fordham University outlines four pillars for AI in education: understanding AI foundations, discerning ethical versus unethical uses, augmenting rather than replacing work, and recognizing when AI is inappropriate. He describes Fordham's ethical AI hub and calls for a citywide training strategy beyond education.
chapters.title: Travis Proof of Fordham on ethical AI framework and citywide training

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dahl
utterances.text: German, good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: Travis Proof from Fordham University, Chair De La Rosa, proud distinguished alum of Fordham University, good to be here today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to talk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: You know, this hearing is tackling some of the critical operational questions the city is going to be having to grapple with, but our testimony at Fordham is focused on a bit more specific question the number of the students very ably raised,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: which is when AI is in use, how can we ensure it is applied ethically, responsibly, and with end users trained to use it with humanity, discernment, and emotional intelligence?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: We can teach students how to ask better questions, not simply generate better answers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: It is important to note the conversation we're having.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: About the opportunities, challenges, and concerns of AI is one machine technology cannot replicate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: That is both our power and is our blueprint.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: There is a global reckoning happening around AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: And just weeks ago, Pope Leo proclaimed that the dignity of the person must always be present in the technology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: It's not by accident that the dignity of the person has not always been present.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: Recent years, careers in social work, teaching, ministry,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: the arts have frequently been deprioritized amidst a push for the youth and young adults to pursue opportunities in STEM.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: Fields understandably deemed a national priority driven the need for economic competitiveness, but in some corners, the humanities were outright stigmatized.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: The lack of balance is why we need this important conversation now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: And at Fordham, we are following four general pillars of how should we approach AI in education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: One is understanding the foundations of AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: Two, discerning ethical versus unethical content and uses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: Three, how to augment, not replace, work product and workers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: And four, recognizing when the use of AI is not appropriate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: We're operationalizing these values through the construction and launch of an ethical AI hub at our Lincoln Center campus.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: And I've included in my testimony a few of our key initial areas of focus, as well as some of the community engagement we've done over the past year and a half to help guide that work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: And we'd be happy to share that information with you if it's helpful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: But the gist is this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: We need a citywide training strategy that goes beyond not just education, but the entire citywide community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: We want to be a partner.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa asks Dr. Trout about learning AI without computers; he confirms unplugged activities are a computer science education tenet. De La Rosa asks Nasser-Frederick to clarify the FERPA school official loophole, which she explains has allowed vendors to access student data without consent since the 1970s.
chapters.title: De La Rosa questions panel on unplugged AI learning and vendor loopholes

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I do want to highlight step three, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Trout, and your, I guess, your four recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And I think if you could talk a little more, just a little more about it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Because I think there's an assumption that, oh, to learn about AI, you need to put a chatbot in front of a kid.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But you're highlighting that you don't need to use AI.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: itself on a computer to learn about AI.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: One of the tenets of computer science education, which I'm intimately involved in, is what we call unplugged activities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: Just because you are learning about a computer does not mean you need to be sitting in front of the screen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edgar Trout
utterances.text: And I, again, stress that if we work with higher education partners, we can co-design these experiences that teach about AI without doing some of the damage of extra screen time.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We were trying to figure out your name, but I couldn't catch it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: What's your name?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Tania?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Sorry to...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: My question is, you mentioned that the, basically what you're describing to me sounds like a loophole.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: In the vendor system where if they're registered in-house, can you explain that a little bit more?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, that's been going on since the 70s, like ever since FERPA was invented, they never really defined what a school official is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: So, you know, like the construction company who they hired to do the cafeteria stuff, those are all school officials through like No Child Left Behind and the Every Student Succeeds Act.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: It became more formalized that like vendors,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: are designated as school officials and most ed tech vendors and classroom application solutions, all that stuff, they function as school officials.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: And you can see that on the DOE's website.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: But yeah, that's what the data privacy officers didn't

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: mention and I think that's an important detail to how data is shared to third party

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tanaya Nasser-Frederick
utterances.text: how schools can share data to companies without parental consent.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I just wanted to clarify that because I thought I understood it correctly, but you explained it way clearer now.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I just, just at form, I just want to clarify, similar to the question for Dr.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Trout, which is a citywide training strategy.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Is your contention that we should be training kindergarten?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You said K-12 students, teachers, administrators.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Is your contention that we should be training kindergartners using a computer or on AI?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Or is there some other clarification point about what you mean by K-12 students, empower them as creative, critical thinkers?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: For our university, the stance is this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: We are not trying to convince people to use AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: We are trying to convince people how to do that critical thinking that the students were talking about, how to understand ethical versus unethical uses, whether that's on a computer, whether that's other technologies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: But that's really what begins for us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: And I think that's all age groups.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: It's all sectors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: It's outside of education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: It's within the small business, medium-sized enterprise space as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: But that's really our position.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: We're not here to convince people to use AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: We're here to convince people to understand ethical realities, discernment, and to understand when it's...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Proof
utterances.text: Being used on them and if they do use it, how to use it well.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I appreciate your testimony and look forward to continued work together.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Very clear that we need to do a lot of work in putting those guardrails in both academically and for safety purposes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Our next panel, Ruth Lowenchron, Gracie Messier, Matt Henning, Larry Lieberman, and Lizbeth Lucero.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: Ruth isn't here, just so you know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: She's from my organization.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin when ready.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Please state your name for the record.

chapters.summary: Lucero testifies about Urban Assembly's AI-supported instructional feedback tools showing measurable improvements including 14% increase in student talk time and 14% decrease in classroom silence. She emphasizes AI works best when human-guided and student privacy is non-negotiable, noting recognition from the EdSafe AI Alliance.
chapters.title: Lisbeth Lucero of Urban Assembly on human-guided AI in education

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: Thank you so much for the opportunity to submit this public comment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: My name is Lisbeth Lucero.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: I'm the Deputy Director of Advancement and Communications at the Urban Assembly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: And I'm actually here on behalf of our Chief Executive Officer, David Adams, who is delivering a commencement address at the Urban Assembly New York Harbor School.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: And we really appreciate the council's focus on AI, student data, and privacy in our public schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: At the Urban Assembly, we believe that AI has the potential to expand opportunities for students and educators, particularly in areas of college and career advising and instructional support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: At the same time, we agree that AI must be implemented thoughtfully with strong safeguards to protect our students and ensure responsible use.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: From our experience, once...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: Simple lesson is true.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: AI works best when implemented and it is human guided.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: These tools can help educators and students access information more effectively, but they cannot replace the relationships and expertise of our counselors, teachers, and school leaders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: We also believe that student privacy is non-negotiable and tools reflect this, and our tools reflect these philosophies, which is why our schools have been recognized with EdSafe AI Alliance for demonstrating responsible and ethical

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: AI practices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: These safeguards ensure that innovation and accountability go hand in hand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: At the same time, a focus on risk should not prevent us from pursuing opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: Our work with AI-supported instructional feedback has led to measurable improvements in teacher quality, increasing the use of respectful language in the classroom and an increase in lesson previews.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: We've also seen increases in student participation through 14% of increase in student talk time, alongside a 14% decrease in classroom silence, which indicates an engaging and inclusive classroom environment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: Besides these numbers, our impact with students and teachers are real.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: As AI becomes more common, the question is not about whether AI could be used for good and bad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: It's about how we can ensure to solve the biggest problems for our students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: So thank you again for helping us figure out solutions and innovative strategies for our public education system.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Please make sure your microphone's on.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Remember, red means go.

chapters.summary: Lieberman describes MOUSE's 30-year history training NYC public school students and teachers in technology, including an AI League at 40 schools with Carnegie Mellon curriculum. He reports 83 teachers attended a generative AI fluency training in one council district, calling the demand for AI training the largest in the organization's history.
chapters.title: Larry Lieberman of MOUSE on AI training demand from teachers

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: There we go.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: Good afternoon and thank you, council members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: My name is Larry Lieberman and I'm here on behalf of MOUSE, a nonprofit that for 30 years has trained New York City public schools, students and teachers to use technology creatively and responsibly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: We appreciate the council's attention to security and to artificial intelligence in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: The answer, of course, is not avoiding AI education or distancing our educators from positive and helpful technology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: The answer is more training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: Teachers and our young people know that AI is available and need to learn what it is, how it works, where they are using it, and how to use it safely, intentionally, and equitably.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: That is exactly what Mouse's AI League does at 40 New York City public schools across all five boroughs with curriculum created in partnership with Carnegie Mellon University.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: That, to your point earlier, includes an incredible set of generative AI lessons using only whiteboards, markers, and yellow stickies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: In the program, like Mouse's AI League, students in NYC are learning how AI works and knowing this helps protect them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: We urge the council to vigorously move to protect student safety while supporting high quality digital learning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: Together, our jobs include ensuring that New York City public school students are prepared to be the future engineers and innovators at New York City's formidable employees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: Last week, 83 New York City teachers in Council District 35 attended a mouse generative AI fluency training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: Yesterday, 25 more high school teachers in Council District 15 attended mouse generative AI training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: The demand among teachers for AI training is as large as anything we have ever seen in 30 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: Please come visit Mouse in the Classroom.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: Get to know our teachers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Larry Lieberman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Happy to.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thanks.

chapters.summary: Messier from New York Lawyers for the Public Interest supports Intro 853's goals but questions whether the study is the most effective use of resources, noting existing data already shows language, income, and geography affect access. She cites that only 28% of ELL students with IEPs receive services in another language and 47% of Spanish-speaking students receive recommended services versus 95% of English speakers.
chapters.title: Gracie Messier of NYLPI on special education service disparities

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: My name is Gracie Messier, and I'm a legal intern with the Disability Justice Program at New York Lawyers for the Public Interest, or NOPI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: NOPI is here to testify and supports the goals of Intro No.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: 853 and appreciates the Council's commitment to special education services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: However, we encourage the Council to consider whether the study, as currently proposed, is the most effective use of the city's resources.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: Much of the information the study seeks to collect is already available through existing reporting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: We encourage the Council to refocus the study to answer questions that existing data cannot, or devote funds directly towards improved special education services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: According to the city's most recent data, only 28% of English language learners with individualized education plans, or IEPs, receive those services in another language.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: And 95% of English-speaking students were fully receiving their recommended special education services, while only 47% of Spanish-speaking students were fully receiving those same services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: Data also show substantial differences in accessing services based on income.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: Students receiving services disproportionately come from low-income households, and students from higher-income neighborhoods are more likely to be placed in inclusive settings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: Gaps in services fall hardest on low-income families and multilingual households.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: In NOPE's work, these communities have consistently reported the same barriers, delays in receiving related services, shortages of qualified providers, difficulty accessing bilingual services, and limited availability of appropriate placements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: NOPE shares the Council's commitment to improving outcomes for students with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: However, existing data already established that language, income, and geography affect access to services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: Whether through investment, through direct investment, or more targeted study, we urge the Council to instead focus its efforts on ensuring that students receive the services to which they are entitled.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gracie Messier
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Henning expresses TechNYC's support for the reporting bills but offers recommendations: clarify the broad definition of 'algorithmic tool' in 2129, coordinate with existing IRMA and Office of Algorithmic Accountability frameworks to avoid duplication, and work with DOE on the October 31st reporting deadline infrastructure for 2130.
chapters.title: Matt Henning of TechNYC on supporting bills 2129 and 2130 with recommendations

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair De La Rosa and Chair Denowitz.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: My name is Matt Henning, and I'm the director.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: Director of Government Affairs at TechNYC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: TechNYC represents hundreds of companies, from global tech leaders to startups, that are deeply committed to the advancements of the tech sector in New York City and supporting the city's public schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: I'm pleased to testify today on pre-intro 2129 and 2130.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: TechNYC does not believe New York City's children should be treated as test subjects, and new technology should not be deployed without oversight, clear policies, or transparency.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: But we are equally concerned about the risk of failing to prepare the next generation for a world where AI tools are pervasive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: Without thoughtful guidance from trusted institutions, students will engage with AI entirely on their own terms, not unlike what happened with social media.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: The answer is not to choose between adoption and a prohibition, but to build a transparent, accountable framework that earns the confidence of families and educators.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: That is precisely why we support the intent and goal of both of these bills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: However, TechNYC does have a few recommendations to further improve these bills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: First, the definition of algorithmic tool in 2129 is broad, and we'd encourage the council to consider

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: whether additional clarity would help DOE and vendors focus on compliance resources and tools that genuinely implicate the concerns motivating these bills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: Second, these bills layer new requirements on top of existing Office of Algorithmic Accountability and IRMA framework.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: Implementation guidance should make clear how they coordinate to avoid duplication.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: Last, on 2130, the first quarterly brief reporting deadline of October 31st is a fast-moving timeline, and we encourage the Council to work with DOE to ensure the necessary infrastructure is in place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matt Henning
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony, and thank you.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa asks Lucero whether using the EdSafe AI Alliance framework indicates DOE hasn't provided robust enough support. Lucero explains the tool has been recognized by EdSafe and there have been conversations with NYC DOE and OSP, though the exact relationship is unclear. De La Rosa notes it appears to be another evaluative tool outside DOE's framework.
chapters.title: De La Rosa questions Lucero on EdSafe framework and DOE support gaps

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: These are my two bills to increase transparency and accountability, so I thank you for your recommendations to improve the bill.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Elizabeth, I just want to understand something.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You support our schools, and you've chosen to...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Use the EdSafe framework.
