utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So to me, tell me this is true or not, to me that means the DOE has not provided a robust enough support system, so you've chosen to go one step further and use like an outside assessment tool.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Can you talk a little more about this decision?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: Yeah, definitely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: So one of our AI tools is used for instructional quality, and it's been recognized by the Edsef AI Alliance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: I'm not 100% sure the relationship that we currently have with them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: Most of my notes are from our team who builds the tool at the Urban Assembly, and

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: there have been conversations with the NYC DOE and OSP with some of the work that we're doing in our ed tech space.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisbeth Lucero
utterances.text: So hopefully that clarifies some of the questions.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: No, it sounds to me like it's another evaluative tool, but we'll look more into that.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to thank this panel for testifying today.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And we'll move on to our next panel.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Someone with beautiful handwriting.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Michelle Blonde.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Tell me the rest, please.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Blondeville?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Can I say I love it?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It's in beautiful cursive.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I have to.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Blonde Monville.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Beautiful cursive.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I meant it when I said, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You know, they don't teach cursive in school anymore.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Catholic school.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Catholic school.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Maybe I'll send my kids to Catholic school.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: No, that one's cursive.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It'll be good.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Shannon Ritchie and Julie Zhang.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Odin Adeller
utterances.text: You may begin when ready.

chapters.summary: Xu testifies in support of Intro 684, noting G&T programs are unevenly distributed with too few seats—10,000 families apply for 2,500 spots. He criticizes the sole reliance on subjective teacher recommendations for G&T identification, arguing it disproportionately harms immigrant families and ELL students, and calls for universal screening with standardized assessments.
chapters.title: Yatin Xu of Place NYC supports Intro 684 on G&T access

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Dinowitz and Chair De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to give my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: My name is Yatin Xu, and I'm speaking today on behalf of Place NYC, an organization that advocates for high-quality, accelerated learning opportunities for students in New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: Place NYC strongly supports Intro 684, which calls for a study on how students'language status and geographic location affect access to early elementary gifted and talented programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: G&T programs are unevenly distributed across the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: Many districts, particularly in the Bronx, Brooklyn, and parts of Queens, have few G&T seats.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: Geographic location should never determine whether a child receives an education they need.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: Every year, 10,000 families apply hoping for a spot with just 2,500 seats across the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: There are simply too few seats.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: Equally troubling is the sole reliance on a subjective teacher recommendation for G&T identification for the program's lottery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: This process is prone to teacher bias, and we believe it disproportionately harms immigrant families where students'home language

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: is not English.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: The current pre-K teacher assessment takes place just three months after students in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: As a result, many bright English language learner students may not be recommended for gifted and talented programs, even when they are clearly ready for more challenging work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: We urge the council to examine these problems and recommend concrete solutions, specifically the adoption of an objective evaluation measure in early grades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: Universal screening using standardized assessments would give every child a fair opportunity to be recognized, regardless of language background or zip code.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: By including an objective evaluation and early identification, we can ensure that students who are ready for acceleration receives the appropriate education they deserve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yatin Xu
utterances.text: Place NYC urges the council to advance this bill so we can finally gather the data needed to build a fairer and more effective G&T program for New York City students.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Michelle?

chapters.summary: Blommenbill testifies about being an unwitting victim of neurological attacks and technology weaponization, describing organized stalking, electronic assault, and non-consensual human experimentation. She thanks the Council for laws like the POST Act and biometric access restrictions.
chapters.title: Michelle Ann Blommenbill on Havana Syndrome and technology weaponization

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: Yes, my name is Michelle Ann Blommenbill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: I'm a health and fitness educator for 40 years, a former adjunct lecturer at New York University, former instructor at Fashion Institute of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: technology and at the American Red Cross.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: I'm here today to represent those innocent American citizens who are being experimented on for the use of AI and to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: add to the supercomputer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: Thank you for your servitude in these difficult times.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: I'm speaking on behalf of everyday people who are Havana Syndrome or anomalous health incident victims, some knowingly and unknowingly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: With the glaring awareness of the benefits afforded to our diplomat counterparts, helping American

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: victims affected by neurological attacks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: We certainly hold fast to the notion that one day we will be free from torture, pain, invisibility, and the weaponization of technology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: I do thank the New York City Council for all of those

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: laws put into place like the post act on Hanifa's law that she just put into place about

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: businesses accessing our biometrics and so forth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: Every day people

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: Havana syndrome victims as compromise of diagnosed Havana syndrome public citizens who have been unlawfully experimented on and who endure targeting in various infamous matters.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: These heinous crimes include, but are not limited to, organized stalking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: Thank you for those harassment laws also that are put into our workplace.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: Smear campaigns, noise harassment, electronic assault from directed...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: Energy weapons, non-consensual human experimentation socially and technologically as V2K blue-eye technology and AI.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Ritchie, a parent and former teacher, calls for an AI moratorium and reduction of ed tech products. She cites a New York Times article where Google said investing in schools helps onboard kids to its ecosystem, and argues schools are force-feeding tech products that collect and sell children's private information. She calls for nurturing human connection in education.
chapters.title: Shannon Ritchie on AI moratorium and ed tech as data collection

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Shannon Ritchie.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: I am a parent of a third grader in Greenpoint, Brooklyn, and a member of the SLT.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: I am here today to speak out against the use of AI in our schools and to encourage a push from the council to reduce our usage of ed tech products.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: First, I would like to thank the council members who have signed on to our moratorium proposal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: Thank you for listening to your constituents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: To those who haven't yet signed, I hope you are listening today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: The DOE claims to want parent and student input, but they couldn't be bothered to stay here and hear us out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: So much of what they said today is misleading and misrepresenting what we are experiencing on a daily basis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: It's nearly impossible to find real peer-reviewed data supporting the use of AI in education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: So what are we doing here?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: Why aren't we listening to scientists, researchers, or education experts?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: Why are we simply trusting the words of salespeople, of representatives from ed tech and AI companies?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: According to a New York Times article, Google claimed, quote, investing in

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: and schools helps onboard kids into Google's ecosystem, end quote.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: Schools are force-feeding kids tech products, and those products are collecting, storing, and selling private information pulled from our children's brains.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: This is a plot straight from a dystopian sci-fi novel, yet we are living this reality every day when we send our children to school.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: Our kids and our teachers deserve more than being reduced to data collection.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: Speaking as an educator myself, learning is driven by human connection.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: It is not merely pouring information from one source into another, which is essentially how ed tech products work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: If we want a robust public education system, we need to nurture it and curb the over-reliance on screens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: We need an AI moratorium now, and this city needs to stop seeing our children as products being ripened for harvest by Google and predatory ed tech companies.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You're welcome.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I have to ask first.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I have to ask.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You said you're an educator yourself?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shannon Ritchie
utterances.text: I'm not currently teaching, but I was a teacher, yes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Very cool.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may be in.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Zam from Homecrest Community Services testifies in support of Intros 684 and 853, describing how immigrant families in southern Brooklyn face barriers including lack of translated materials, transportation challenges, and uneven program availability. She argues a child's opportunity should not depend on whether a parent speaks English or lives in a resource-rich neighborhood.
chapters.title: Julie Zam on language access barriers for immigrant families

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Go after the new chair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Excuse me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Dino West and the members of the education committees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: My name is Julie Zam.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: I am a program assistant at Home Cross Community Services, Multisocial Services Center.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: I am also a mother of two daughters in New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify in support of Intel 684 and Intel 853, the education for all bills introduced by Council Member Susan Zhuang.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Home class community services serve low-income, immigrant, and namely English professional families across southern Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Many parents in our community care deeply about their children's educations, but they do not always have equal access to clear information.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: They may not know about gifted and talented programs, special education services, eligibility rules, application timelines,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: innovations, and where to go for help.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Intel 680

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: 84 will require the city to study how language needs and location affect access to early elementary gifted and talented programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Intro 853 will require the city to study how language needs, income, and location affect access to special education programs and services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: A child's opportunity should not depend on whether a parent speaks English fully, understands the school system, or lives in a neighborhood with more resources.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: In our borough neighborhoods like Homecraft in Brooklyn, families face many barriers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: This includes namely outreach, lack of translated materials, transportation challenges, and uneven program availability.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: These barriers can affect whether a child gets the same educational opportunities as other children or receive the special education support they need.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And as I said, you can submit the rest in writing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julie Zam
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'd like to thank this panel for their testimony this evening.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And our next panel.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Craig Garrett.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Kaiser, Lainey Hampson.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Kelly Clancy and Liat Olenek.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, so Kaiser and Laney will testify.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: Hi, my name's Lainey Hameson.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: I'm the co-chair of the Parent Coalition for Student Privacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: Thank you for holding these hearings today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: I wanted to start by addressing some of the misinformation that was spread by DOE this morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: Google Gemini is used in hundreds of elementary school classrooms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: Installed on thousands of young children's Chromebooks and inserted into their education every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: So I have no idea what the DOE official meant when he said it was only used in a few high schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: Google Gemini was also cited just this week as one of the most unsafe chatbots.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: On the market when it comes to mental health, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: I also wanted to talk about the PowerSchool and Illuminate breaches, which released the data of many, many thousands of former students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: That data should no longer have been held by the vendor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: The state law requires data minimization and data deletion when that data is no longer needed to carry out services, and yet the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: The DOE does not require this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: The PowerSchool, you talked about using vendors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: PowerSchool has been noted over and over of not complying with the most basic security protections, which led to the breaches of millions of students'personal data a few years ago, and yet the DOE allows

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: schools to still use more than 15 PowerSchool products, including Naviance, which more recently was cited as disclosing data illegally

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: in violation of state laws and federal wiretapping laws, and a $17.25 million settlement was made by the court.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: Which New York City families are eligible for, and yet the DOE has not told any families about this alleged breach or that they're eligible for a piece of the settlement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: I just wanted to go on a little bit more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: My testimony is very lengthy, but the DOE has not been following compliant with the privacy laws.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: That's why we recommended strengthening them, and they did not strengthen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonie Haimson
utterances.text: They did not use the recommendations that we proposed on the AI working group.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Kaiser?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: My name is Kaiser, community organizer with the Alliance for Quality Education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: AQE supports Intro 853.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: We oppose Intro 684.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: All academic programs...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: services should be equitably accessible, but there is no evidence that children eight and under can be reliably identified as gifted or that separating them from their peers is educationally sound.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: AQE encourages Council to strengthen and pass the pre-considered bills to propose annual reporting and quarterly data breach reporting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: There is no data showing that AI learning tools are effective or safe, except claims provided by the profiteers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: Yet the Chancellor has announced plans to, quote, beta test these products on our children, including in District 75.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: The bills should include public disclosure of evidence of efficacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: Algorithmic tools have been proven to have racial and gender bias.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: The bills must require assessments of bias and hallucinations in all AI tools in use.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: Our kids'best interests, not profit, must be centered here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: The bills should include public disclosure of the number and positions of all DOE staff who have existing financial or other relationships with the companies that profit off of these products and those companies'involvement in developing trainings and policy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: Unfortunately, reporting is not enough, especially when we can't trust it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: There were many inconsistencies in DOE testimony today, flagging staff either not having thorough understanding of what their department is doing or misrepresenting it entirely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: AQE is grateful that more than half of the council has signed on to the call for a two-year moratorium for all AI in schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: Right now, kids are losing access to the most essential kinds of learning backed by extensive research, open-ended play,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: hands-on inquiry, and culturally responsive teaching differentiated by skilled human teachers to suit their specific population of students and their layered human stories.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: Parents don't know what companies have their kids'data or what products are being used directly by their children, used to surveil them, or used to shape their instruction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: And when they ask, they're told DOE either doesn't know or can't say due to NDAs.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kaiser
utterances.text: These unproven, biased, and dangerous products are sucking up valuable classroom time and public funding.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I appreciate your testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And as I mentioned at the very beginning, two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: If there are further questions, fine.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: But I do also want to thank both of you, Eleni.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I know you've been particularly helpful in helping shed a light on some of the issues going on with the DOE.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You're reporting on it, your participation at the roundtable, so I want to thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to thank you both for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And I remind you again, written testimony can be submitted to the council at testimony at council.nyc.gov.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Our next panel, Stephen Stowe.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Beckham Cowley, Lisa Walker,

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Jay Edidin and Molly Senak.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to mix things up.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Let's start on my right this time.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: All right, go ahead, Molly.

chapters.summary: Senec from the Center for Independence of the Disabled New York warns that AI could mask critical symptoms and delay diagnoses for students with disabilities. She identifies risks including critical thinking erosion, bias, misinformation, mental health impacts, and trust erosion, and calls for getting ahead of AI regulation before problems seen with cell phones and social media recur.
chapters.title: Molly Senec of CIDNY on AI risks for students with disabilities

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: Chairs, good evening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: My name is Molly Senec.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: I am the Education and Employment Community Organizer at Center for Independence of the Disabled New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: Advancement in digital technology have provided critical access for people with disabilities to spaces that were previously far less accessible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: It has meant the ability to participate in activities remotely, more consistent use of assistive technology, and the immediate availability of practically infinite information, all things that can be and are incredibly valuable in an education setting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: However, digital technology has also made it easier to harass people and to perpetuate bias and to steal personal information, to spread or even generate misinformation, and to erode many of the skills like critical thinking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: thinking that schools are supposed to build.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: Over the last few years, New York has struggled to contend with having to retroactively set up guardrails inside the classroom for technology that has already had the chance to become ubiquitous outside the classroom, which obviously we know includes a cell phone ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: and an attempt to restrict social media usage for children and teens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: Now, there is a new technology that is developing rapidly enough that it's causing decision makers everywhere to rush to incorporate its usage

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: for fear of being left behind without necessarily considering that we actually have an opportunity to get ahead and avoid the problems that we saw with cell phones and with social media.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: As of right now, AI is developing so rapidly that we do not know enough about it or about the impact of its long-term usage on students, especially students with disabilities, to begin incorporating it into our schools without doing our due diligence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: While we cannot necessarily protect students outside the classroom, it remains our responsibility to protect them as best we can within it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: Just a couple of things, more details are in my written testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: We're looking at the impact of critical thinking, bias, misinformation, mental health, trust erosion, and also the conflation of assistive technology with AI, meaning that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: we don't yet know whether it masks critical symptoms and potentially delays diagnoses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Molly Senec
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Walker, a parent of two DOE device users, contradicts DOE testimony that YouTube is not on devices, saying her children have full internet and YouTube access. She describes AI-powered Amira collecting children's voices without parental consent and calls for transparency about what technology children interact with at school.
chapters.title: Lisa Walker on YouTube access and lack of safeguards on DOE devices

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Lisa Walker and I'm a mom.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: So earlier the first deputy

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: Chancellor said that YouTube is not on DOE devices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: YouTube is on DOE devices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: Both my seventh grader and first grader have DOE-issued devices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: And as far as I understand, according to New York State law, I thought YouTube is not supposed to be on these devices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: But at my school, every child has a device sent home with them, as young as four years old on weekends.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: I can see on these DOE devices that my children have full access to the internet, YouTube, and no real safeguards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: Parents can opt out of the device being sent home, but it's still there waiting for them at school.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: The DOE says, well, at least there's software installed so they can't watch porn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: Well, that's just not good enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: The DOE has not fulfilled their promise to keep kids safe and to protect kids'privacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: We use two apps at our school against our consent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: We use two apps at our school.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: One is AI-powered Amira, a cute avatar that talks to children collecting their voices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: Many parents don't even know their kids are using ed tech apps at school or using AI and being recorded.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: Instead of putting the onus on parents to ask questions, parents deserve transparency and should be told what technology their kids are interacting with at school.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: And we should also be asked for our consent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: Our kids deserve an education focused on real social connection to teachers and students, not AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Walker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Adedon, a freelance creative professional and parent, urges a moratorium on predatory LLMs, noting they are built on stolen work of writers and artists. They cite APA health warnings about adolescents and AI, documentation of chatbots inducing psychosis in adults and driving children to suicide, and argue AI will stunt critical growth for thousands of students.
chapters.title: Jay Adedon on AI built on stolen work and mental health risks

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: Okay, thank you, and thank you, by the way, for getting my name right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: That's a rare and exciting occurrence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: My name is Jay Adedon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: I'm a freelance creative professional and the parent of a New York City student, and I'm speaking here today to urge you to adopt a moratorium on the use of predatory LLMs, better known as AI, in New York City schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: So the case against AI schools is really substantial, so much that I can't fit into two minutes, so I'm going to sort of focus down on something that people haven't brought up so far.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: Specifically, that the largest large language models are built using the stolen work of thousands upon thousands of writers and artists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: And especially in a city where so many of us make our homes, the adoption of technology built on our non-consensual, uncompensated labor and intellectual property is at best an insult.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: But there's a bigger problem attached to it, which is the fact that adopting that technology into schools means trusting those same predatory companies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: Who stole all that information and all that work unapologetically with our children's sensitive data.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: We know that AI also undercuts children's social and emotional development, and I want to talk in particular about its impact on adolescents, because they're the age group targeted commercially and in our schools with the highest concentration of AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: And it's bad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: It's bad to the point that the APA has

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: has issued a health warning about adolescents and AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: And that in itself isn't much of a surprise, given that we've seen documentation in the last year that chatbots are capable of inducing psychosis in previously healthy adults and have driven multiple children to suicide.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: My kid isn't an adolescent, so she's just about to start pre-K, where tech magnets are already pushing an increasing use of AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: As you mentioned, kids as young as four are getting devices to take home.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: And as a parent, I want my kid to actually learn at school, not just facts, but to think and reason for herself, to solve problems and to grow as a human being.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: And introducing AI, as it now exists in New York City classrooms, will stunt that same critical growth for my kid and for thousands of others.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jay Adedon
utterances.text: Thanks for your time.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

chapters.summary: Stowe, a former CEC member, supports Intros 684 and 853, citing Chalkbeat data that 71% of private school tuition for students with disabilities went to white families who make up only 12.5% of students with disabilities. He calls for addressing awareness gaps and expanding G&T programs in underserved districts rather than making token efforts.
chapters.title: Stephen Stowe on racial disparities in special education funding

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And you may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Dinowitz and De La Rosa and other members of the Education and Technology Committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: My name is Stephen Stowe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: I'm a parent of two former public school students, and I previously served on a community education council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: I'm here to support bills INT 684 and INT 853, sponsored by Councilmember Zhuang.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: I agree with the premise of both these bills, that many issues in our education system come simply from a lack of awareness and knowledge of what's available to parents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: It's very concerning that white families get the majority of private school special education funding.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: As Chalkbeat reported, 71% of the private school tuition for students with disabilities who could not otherwise have their services met by the DOE went to white families.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: the fact that they make up only 12.5% of students with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: By comparison, as Councilwoman Zhuang was highlighting earlier, black and brown students make up 75% of students with disabilities, but only got 24% of the tuition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: This is due to very complex cultural, socioeconomic reasons, and educational reasons.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: In short, an awareness gap exists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: The City Council can take the lead on addressing these inequalities by advancing INT 853.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: On the G&T, for years G&T programs were systematically cut in black and brown neighborhoods for political reasons.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: Perhaps unsurprisingly, we also got a steady reduction in the number of black and brown students attending the most selective high schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: I applaud the prior administration for starting to expand the number of programs in underserved districts, but more should be done.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: There is still not enough awareness of these programs, and we can't just make token efforts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: Continue efforts to address gaps in awareness, which this bill will help do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: The council can take the lead on addressing this inequality by advancing INT 684.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: In conclusion, the DOE does offer an amazing range of programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: So much of the issue is knowledge and awareness with parents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: I support these bills and hope they are passed, acted on, and used to improve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: Real education policy in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Stowe
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you all for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I will now call the next panel.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Greg Pack.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Kristen?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Adrian Savore?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Carrie Hill, Ariana Rebellini, and Effie Zachary.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Oh, I do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: You may begin when you're ready.

chapters.summary: Zachary, former PEP representative, expresses dismay that privacy suggestions from years of task forces were ignored. She calls a two-year moratorium almost laughable given DOE's slowness, and argues AI is inevitable—the focus should be on safety measures, privacy, and integrity guardrails rather than avoidance.
chapters.title: Effie Zachary on DOE slowness and inevitability of AI

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Dinowitz and Chair DeRosa.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: My name is Effie Zachary.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: Formerly, I used to be the vice president of CCHS, and more recently, the Queen's representative on the PEP.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: I was elected by parents to both positions, not appointed by any politician.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: I wasn't going to speak, but I heard a lot today that made me do want to speak a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: Almost everything we heard today about contracts and privacy, nothing is new.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: It was discussed at least three, four years ago, especially when the Illuminate had a serious breach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: I was on many committees, many task force.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: We had questions, we gave a lot of suggestions, and I'm dismayed to see that almost every good, sensible suggestion that was made has been pretty much ignored, certainly not implemented.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: And when I hear the term like almost two-year moratorium, it almost makes me laugh.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: Almost by default, everything that the DOE starts to do has a two-year moratorium.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: They're so slow to move.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: In a topic like AI, which right now is one of the fastest evolving technology, jokes are posted with what I consider one of the slowest moving agencies in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: It's almost laughable that we're going to have an agency that moves very slow.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: Trying to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: contain something that moves very fast.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: I don't think there's any way to just avoid AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: It's the coming technology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: It's here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: It's a tool we need to use.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: What we do need to focus on is what are the safety measures, both privacy and integrity, things like that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: So let's forget about not letting it happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: It's going to happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: No way you can avoid progress.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: But we need to think, like, what are the guardrails that we need to put in place?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: That should be the focus.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Effie Zachary
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Comic book writer Greg Pak calls AI in schools 'child abuse,' arguing that writing is thinking and drawing is seeing. He says kids who type prompts instead of doing the work themselves are denied human learning experiences. He calls AI a tool of liars and scammers and warns billionaires can push a 'racism button' on these machines.
chapters.title: Greg Pak calls AI in schools a form of child abuse

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: I appreciate you having us, allowing us to speak.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: My name is Greg Pak.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: I am a comic book writer and also a New York City resident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: Had a child go through the public schools, product of public schools myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: I am here because I believe that AI

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: in the schools is child abuse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: It is a form of child abuse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: I don't use those terms lightly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: Writing is thinking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: Drawing is seeing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: If you get a kid to type in a prompt and generate text, type in a prompt and generate an image, and you say, good,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: You have failed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: In fact, you have denied that child a human experience of thinking and learning and doing stuff.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: Kids are not products, and they're not in school to create products.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: Kids are in school to learn, to go through the process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: We should be about process, not products.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: And...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: AI is also a tool of liars, scammers, and fraud.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: This is its primary use case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: Having kids use AI in schools sets them up to become losers who can't think for themselves and can't do things and cannot be part of a community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: When a kid draws, a kid has to look at you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: A kid studies your face.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: A kid takes note of the world and the people around them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: If you deny a kid that chance by having them just type in a prompt, they learn nothing and they do not participate in a wider community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: AI is child abuse, I'll say it again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: I also just wanted to take note, so I support the AI moratorium in the public schools, and in fact, we should make it, we should just burn all those machines.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: I also want to take note of the fact that these,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: There's a racism button on these machines, and the billionaires can push that racism button on the machines as they have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: And we don't want that.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Rebellini, a writer and mother, shares stories of her father's 43-year teaching career and the immeasurable impact teachers have that machines cannot replicate. She cites her own book about suicide to emphasize the consequences of losing human connection, noting black children are especially susceptible to isolation, and calls chatbots used as study guides harmful.
chapters.title: Arianna Rebellini on human teachers and the humanities

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I certainly wouldn't want my kids becoming losers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Pak
utterances.text: No, you don't.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I think you are learning something from our president.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm calling people losers.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, please.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: I'm Arianna Rebellini.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: I'm a mother to a soon-to-be second grader in Queens and a writer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: And I want to speak today about...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: My entire life, I've had people tell me my father changed their lives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Carlo taught high school English for 43 impassioned years, and if there's one thing I know beyond all doubt, it is that my father loved teaching and his students loved him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: When he retired, we set up a website where former students could leave well wishes, and it was a gift to see what they had each held on to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: From high fives in the hallways to a new respect for reading.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: My dad was born and raised in what he loves to call the boogie down Bronx, and rap has been a huge part of his life and syllabi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Multiple posts shouted out Wu-Tang Wednesday and Kendrick Friday.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: And I say all of this to highlight the varied and immeasurable impact of teachers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: And the incalculable losses we in our community will endure if we declare them redundant in the face of AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: If my father changed his students'lives, it was in ways that can't be replicated by a machine.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: It is something living and indelible and unpredictable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: I can't believe I need to say this, but educators should be people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Anyone who cares about children's well-being knows that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: I know you know that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: I know the DOE knows that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: This is a disingenuous conversation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Last year, I published a book about suicide, so I feel uniquely qualified to emphasize the dire emotional and material consequences of losing human connection.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Isolation and alienation are killing us, and I must note that black children are especially susceptible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Chatbots, which we are already seeing being used as study guides and tutors, are certainly not helping.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Our children cannot afford to lose even one person who is invested in their future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: I want to close with one message from my father.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: She wrote, your classroom is not just where I learned how to write essays or identify sentences, but it was where I learned how to be human.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: So when we talk about how AI is destroying the humanities, we're describing not only academics, but more profoundly the very substance of life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: It is your responsibility to protect our children's right to both, and the moratorium will be a good start.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You say your dad's from the Bronx?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Yeah, he is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Both parents.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Both parents?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Now you've got to tell me where in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Allerton Avenue was what they were near.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: I was born in the Bronx, but...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You were born in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You know, you're welcome back anytime.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Awesome.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Arianna Rebellini
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to call the next panel Iman God.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Charlotte Pope.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Andrea Ortiz.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Anita Rad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea Ortiz
utterances.text: I'll let you all decide.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea Ortiz
utterances.text: Fielder's choice.

chapters.summary: Gad urges redirecting funds from tech companies and surveillance tools toward organizations supporting young people's tech literacy and safety infrastructure including restorative justice and mental health access. She describes a youth town hall where students demanded best practices for AI, data privacy education, and consentful tech.
chapters.title: Iman Gad of Girls for Gender Equity on youth tech literacy funding

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: Thank you, Andrea.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Dinowitz, Chair De La Rosa, and members of the committees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: My name is Iman Gad, and I am the Policy and Advocacy Manager at Girls for Gender Equity, a black feminist organization that works to reshape culture and policy through advocacy, youth-centered programming, and narrative shift.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: So at a time when technology and AI are moving at an unprecedented pace, we are here today to urge the cities to prioritize the education, privacy, and safety of our students by redirecting all

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: funds currently going to tech companies and surveillance tools towards organizations that are doing the work to support young people's tech literacy and towards proven safety infrastructure such as restorative justice, mental health access, and immigrant protections.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: Last November, the Alliance on Gender Equity, our youth-led organizing base, held a town hall to inform the School Girls Demand campaign, a campaign with the purpose of uplifting the voices of young people and giving them the opportunity to ask their city for what they need.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: AI and tech were hot topics in that room of 70 students from schools across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: Students expressed grievances that included the need for best practices for utilizing AI in learning environments, the racial disparities in AI access,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Ann Blommenbill
utterances.text: This is an education

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: climate-conscious ways to use AI, and the desire to learn about data privacy, along with their digital footprint and methods to protect themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: As young people from our youth programs reflected on their time in programs and the lessons they walked away with, one student said, I feel like one way that I can achieve social justice is by telling my friends and family about consentful tech.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: I feel like that's very important to me now that AI is taking over.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: Making sure that you're using apps and devices that ask for consent before they just take the information that you're giving them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: Keeping our youth tech literate and informed about the importance of data privacy was a high priority for this year's program curriculum to meet the moment and heed the call of our youth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: While small organizations are doing the work to keep young people protected, the city needs to support these efforts by funding programming and safety infrastructures in schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Iman Gad
utterances.text: This is why we call on the city to fund our alliance on gender equity in the amount of $300,000, to continue equipping young black girls, queer and gender-expensive youth to fight for a New York City that is resilient, and I'll put the rest of my written testimony.

chapters.summary: Pope from the NYCLU focuses on the student surveillance industry, noting that FOILed data processing agreements are heavily redacted under trade secrets exemptions. She describes AI products that monitor devices, track physical movements and social connections, and assign mental health wellness scores, calling them a digitalized school-to-prison pipeline and questioning DOE's enforcement of prohibitions.
chapters.title: Charlotte Pope of NYCLU on student surveillance industry

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Charlotte Pope.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: I'm with the New York Civil Liberties Union.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: Thank you for convening this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: My comments focus on the student surveillance industry, where AI represents an imprecise blanket marketing term with no set meeting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: A note up top that we foiled for nondisclosure in data processing agreements, and they are full of redactions under the trade secrets exemption, and the refusal of ed tech companies to explain how their AI systems were built and what they intend.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: tend to do long term, protects these vendors from public scrutiny, and has enabled big data systems that claim to do one thing to accomplish another thing, which is the accumulation of student information and public money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: Even where we can explain how a system works, there's still the question of whether certain systems should exist in schools at all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: Schools are testing grounds for AI products that monitor school-issued devices, even simply typing and drafting of ideas, tracking physical movements and social connections in friend groups, and synthesizing moment-to-moment personal data spanning across years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: Some companies claim their AI surveillance systems can measure and track student emotions like aggression or generate and assign mental health wellness scores.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: All of this produces floods of sensitive student data for private harvesting and opens the door to a digitalized school-to-prison pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: It was confusing to hear today that DOE is not sure what surveillance software is in use other than GoGuardian,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: deferring that those purchases are made school by school, but also somehow very confident that certain GoGuardian products aren't in use and haven't been purchased.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: As you asked, in the AI guidance, surveillance and behavioral monitoring products are both named as beyond the scope of the guidance, but also never allowed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: But what is their definition of behavioral monitoring, and what is the mechanism for getting these prohibited products out?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: When students complained about AI-enhanced bathroom tracking apps, which is surveillance, DOE deferred.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: to principal discretion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: The guidance claims, and the DOE said again today, that these apps must serve a clearly defined educational purpose, but we doubt that schools are convincingly articulating what they're asking these private surveillance sector companies to solve for them.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

chapters.summary: Andrea testifies about excessive policing and surveillance in schools, noting black students make up less than 20% of enrollment but 45% of superintendent suspensions and 52% of police interventions. She calls for redirecting $90 million from school safety to restorative justice and mental health, and enacting a two-year AI moratorium.
chapters.title: Andrea of Dignity in Schools on policing, surveillance, and AI concerns

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: Hello, I'm Andrea from Dignity in Schools New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: Public schools should be places where students feel safe and supported to learn, yet excessive policing and surveillance continue to undermine student safety and well-being.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: While we appreciate Mayor Mamdani's executive budget's proposed reductions to school policing, we are deeply concerned by proposed cuts to restorative justice, the mental health continuum, and immigrant family outreach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: It is a missed opportunity not to reinvest the savings from school policing into protecting and baselining critical services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: The executive budget also fails to address schools'continued reliance on metal detectors, scanning, and surveillance systems that create barriers to learning and disproportionately impact black and immigrant students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: Investigations have found that while 33% of high school students go through metal detectors, 48% are black high schoolers, which contribute to the fact that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: in New York City, black students make up less than 20% of the enrollment, but accounted for 45% of superintendent suspensions and 52% of police interventions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: Therefore, we are concerned by growing calls to expand school policing, scanning, and surveillance in the name of safety despite clear evidence of their disparate impacts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: Schools are also increasingly adopting AI technologies that raise serious concerns about student privacy, data security, and surveillance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: Many AI-powered educational tools collect extensive information about students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: AI practices are particularly concerning for immigrant families at a time when federal immigration policies are expanding AI-driven surveillance and data sharing systems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: Families have little transparency into how student data is collected, stored, or shared, and these practices risk undermining student privacy protections under educational law 2D.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: Rather than investing in tech that monitors students, schools should invest in human relationships and support systems that help students thrive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: We call on the city to stop buying student surveillance and scanning equipment and create a plan to use those funds to institute a student support staff ratio of no greater than 1 to 100,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: freeze hiring for school safety agents, and eliminate vacant positions, generating $90 million in annual savings and using that savings to expand school-based restorative justice, mental health access,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: including $80 million to hire school climate coordinators in 500 schools, and enact a two-year moratorium on the use of AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrea
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I need a row.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You ready?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Okay, you'll go after the Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And Dr.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Kalidis Salas.

chapters.summary: Ramirez, a CEC District 4 member and neuroscientist, calls for a two-year AI moratorium, reduced screen time, and an audit of iReady, which she calls an ineffective tool costing over $100 million. She cites Dr. Jared Horvath's research showing ed tech has harmed students and argues adolescence now extends to age 30 due to lack of experiential learning.
chapters.title: Kalirisa Las Ramirez on screen time, iReady, and cognitive development

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Genowitz and Chair De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: My name is Kalirisa Las Ramirez, pronounced she, her, ella.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: I am going to speak a little bit in the first minute about the work that the Community Education Council and District 4

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: have been doing around providing feedback and policy recommendations for the Department of Education, not just around AI, which we have explicitly asked for a two-year moratorium.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: In addition, we are also asking for a reduction in screen time in our schools, as well as an audit on iReady, which also has AI embedded, but we have found that it is an ineffective tool that we have spent over $100 million

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: in in contracts to be able to do screeners and assessments in our students that, again, are not informing us of proficiency and growth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: And so in turn, when thinking about, again, the investments that we make in our students and trying to understand what helps students thrive, these tools, quote unquote tools,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: Department of Education claims to use to assess our kids are ineffective.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: And that has been shown by the work of Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: Jared Horvath, who continues to advocate for the fact that ed tech and the implementation of ed tech into our schools in the last 10 years have significantly harmed our students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: I want to highlight also the work that, and so I'm going to shift from CEC to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: Galiri Salas, neuroscientist and developmental behavioral neuroscientist, to say that now we know that adolescence has been extended until the age of 30.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: The reason for that is because we are implementing these computers and ed tech into our classrooms and not giving students the opportunity

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: to engage in experiential learning and engage in the science of learning, which means that we need to take them away from screens and give them the experiences that they need and the relationships with their teachers and their community

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: to develop the appropriate cognitive processes to be ready for an adult world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
