utterances.text: And unfortunately, we're not doing that in our classrooms by not providing with them the appropriate resources to do that, in addition to implementing a tool that is racially biased and impacting the diversity of our New York City public school students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kalirisa Las Ramirez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We will now...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: That concludes the in-person portion of our public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We will now move to remote testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: If you are testifying remotely, please listen for your name to be called.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Once your name is called, a member of our staff will unmute you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may then start your testimony once the sergeant at arms sets the clock and cues you to begin.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Professor Julius Stojanovic, you may begin once you are unmuted and the sergeant cues you.

chapters.summary: Stoyanovich, NYU professor and founding director of the Center for Responsible AI, makes three asks: prove AI tools work through independent efficacy and equity standards before classroom use, keep student data home rather than flowing to vendor platforms, and open data for independent verification. She warns AI tutors are flooding fastest into low-income schools.
chapters.title: Professor Julia Stoyanovich of NYU on AI governance principles

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: May I start?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: Thank you very much for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: My name is Julia Stojanovic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: I'm a professor of computer science and data science at NYU and the founding director of the Center for Responsible AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: I have worked on AI governance in the city for nearly a decade, and I'm a proud New Yorker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: I have three asks today and they share one principle.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: The city should not run AI systems on our children that no one outside the vendor can see, check or hold to account.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: First, prove it works.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: AI tutors are flooding into classrooms right now, fastest into low-income schools, because they promise to do more with less.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: And today, the vendors grade their own homework.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: They self-attest that their tools are tested, equitable, and safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: There is no independent yardstick.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: The city's own vetting process, IRMA, checks data privacy, but by its own account, it does not yet review whether a tool is effective or whether it's biased.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: One in three New York City third graders cannot read.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: A tutor that's quietly worse for multilingual learners scales that gap across thousands of kids at once.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: So my first ask, before a tool reaches a classroom, require it to meet an independent efficacy and equity standard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: Does it measurably improve learning?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: And does it work as well for English language learners?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: And students with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: Meets the standard should be a precondition for procurement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: Second, keep the data home.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: Those same AI tutors record how children think, struggle, and learn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: Some of the most sensitive data we collect on a young person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: Today, that data routinely flows out

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: through vendor APIs to models running on private platforms, where it can be retained and reused with little transparency and little recourse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: When I testified before the U.S.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: Congress on DeepSeek, I called this a loss of data sovereignty.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: The moment data leaves your control and falls under someone's control.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: The third is to keep the data open so that people can check whether these systems work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julia Stoyanovich
utterances.text: So in summary, prove it works, keep the data home, and open the data.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony, and if there's anything to expand upon, please submit written testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Aisha Ifran.

chapters.summary: Irfan, founder of Augmented Intelligence Advisory, cites Microsoft and Carnegie Mellon studies showing AI reliance reduces critical thinking. She warns of a cognitive divide along socioeconomic lines and urges deploying cognition-focused AI literacy where students form hypotheses before using chatbots, and adding a cognition check to Bill 2129 requiring evidence of a tool's effect on student reasoning.
chapters.title: Aisha Irfan on cognition-focused AI literacy and procurement checks

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: Hi folks, all good?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chairs Dino-Witson, De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: My name is Aisha Irfan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: I'm a proud product of New York City Public Schools, a former Gale Brewer and Google AI policy staffer, and the founder of Augmented Intelligence Advisory, where we partner with schools to protect student cognition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: New York City parents and electeds are organizing en masse against AI in schools out of a very real fear that untethered AI will cause students to lose the practice of thinking for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: They're not wrong.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: Recent studies by Microsoft, Carnegie Mellon, and Anthropic show that AI reliance reduces critical thinking effort and weakens conceptual understanding in experienced professionals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: If these are the effect on adults, the risk for students building foundational skills are even higher.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: Most concerningly, untethered AI use threatens to create a cognitive divide along socioeconomic lines.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: In tech-heavy households, adults model guarded AI use, and others AI replaces thinking entirely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: The answer is not a blanket ban, it's pedagogy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: I urge two immediate moves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: One, deploy cognition-focused AI literacy in classrooms across the city, where students practice using AI tools under the guidance of teachers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: This means students forming their own hypotheses, interrogating what they know before reaching for a chatbot, and creating AI output as a draft to push back against, rather than a final answer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: Teachers are the experts in how learning happens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: They know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: when a tool builds reasoning and when it short circuits it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: Teachers, not vendors, must guide this work in every classroom across the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: Second, add a cognition check on Bill T2129.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: The bill rightfully assesses tools for privacy and bias.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: It must also require evidence of a tool's effect on student reasoning, giving representative teachers a formal sign-off in the procurement process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: Thanks so much for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Aisha Irfan
utterances.text: I look forward to working with all of you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Next, we have Olympia Kazi.

chapters.summary: Kazi, a parent in District 6, describes a 'break in communications' between DOE and classrooms, saying her fifth grader used Canva AI and YouTube in class and her third grader is prompted by Google Gemini in Google Classroom. She cites a Columbia Teachers College study favoring textbooks and paper, and calls iReady—which has AI and is being sued—an equity issue.
chapters.title: Olympia Kazi on ed tech failures and need for moratorium

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: Hello?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: We hear you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotte Pope
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: Thank you for this critical hearing and your great questions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: My name is Olympia Kazi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: I'm a parent of a third and a fifth grader and the creator of Screen Break in District 6.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: Hearing the testimonies of the DOE reps here today, it is clear that we have an immensely serious break in communications.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: They don't realize what is going on in their classrooms and that their mandate for device-based screeners, Google Classroom, curriculums like HDMH, interventions like What I Need, We,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: and online academic so-called enrichment choices that are pre-approved as free online games have opened a tsunami on New York City public school students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: Our educators, and as a result our students, are inundated by learning tools that we actually know don't work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: There is a Columbia Teachers College 2024 study led by Karen Frout that has indicated that we need textbooks, papers, and pencil if we care about the students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: There is no separation between AI and edtech right now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: Their business model is that these companies integrate untested AI in every step of the way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: So let's be clear, not only do we need a moratorium for two years right away, but while we're doing the moratorium, we need to develop not just guidelines, but also meaningful tools for enforcement and oversight.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: My fifth grader used Canva AI twice this year in social studies and in literacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: He used YouTube consistently and he plays Play Protege.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: Hearing the first deputy chancellor I said this morning was a slam.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: She said that they do not endorse these things and that they are not supposed to be used.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: They said, oh, we approved the free account.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: Has he seen the free account?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: The free account prompts you to buy an account every five minutes during the so-called lesson.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: My third grader gets constantly asked by Google Gemini if she needs some help because she's in Google Classroom.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: So let's be honest.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: What are we talking about?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: These things are all integrated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: IRADI just became mandatory for math next fall.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: IRADI has AI and is being sued.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: And it's not working.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: It is an issue of equity above all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: This is a system that was already failing before the pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: And right now we have way too many kids that they are unhoused in our system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: So, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: So let's do the moratorium, and I'm going to submit also written comments if my time has expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: But there were a lot of things that were said today that were not true.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: And it's sad to know that DOE doesn't know what is going on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Olympia Kazi
utterances.text: So thank you for hosting the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Next, we have Mariela Benavides.

chapters.summary: Mariela, a parent of a child entering kindergarten, expresses fear about AI in classrooms, saying it is 'almost negligent' to put chatbots in front of the most vulnerable population. She describes parents working hard to limit technology at home only to have schools introduce AI, and calls for immediate action before summer.
chapters.title: Mariela on fears as parent of incoming kindergartener

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We hear you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: Oh, amazing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: Oh, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: I'm walking because I'm leaving from work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: Thank you for hosting this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: My name is Mariela.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: My daughter just turned five and she's going into kindergarten in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: And I am so concerned and worried.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: About

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: All of this technology, AI in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: I know so many parents that live in Greenpoint who are so careful with their kids.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: We work so hard to keep, to limit the amount of technology

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: with our kids and then now I find out that I'm going to be sending my daughter to school and she's going to be

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: in front of a screen with a chatbot that she's going to learn that she can just ask questions to the chatbot instead of coming to talk to me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: I think it's ridiculous.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: It is

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: dangerous.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: It is almost negligent to know all of the risks that we know of with technology and the internet and to put this in front of our kids, the most vulnerable

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: population that we have, to put this in front of them and just, you know, hope for the best.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: We definitely need to put a stop to this, like right now, before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: I know that this summer is going to start, and I don't know what happens in the public schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: Everybody goes on vacation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: I have no idea.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: But we cannot be so slow to...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: Do something about this, considering we are so fast to incorporate AI and technology in the schools, and yet we're so slow to actually do something to show that we care about our kids.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mariela
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Now, our next...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Witness is Sonali Thacker.

chapters.summary: Thacker, an NYU English professor and parent, calls the AI guidance sloppy and lacking any evidence-based rationale for AI as a pedagogical good. She argues the guidance represents capitulation to a contested technology, calls children 'not guinea pigs,' and warns that parents with means will exit public schools for those with restricted AI policies.
chapters.title: Sonali Thacker on sloppy AI guidance and capitulation to tech

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: Thank you to the council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: I'm Suneli Thacker, a parent of a rising second grader in Manhattan and an educator myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: I'm an English professor at NYU.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: I'm appalled at the sloppiness of the AI guidance that was released and strongly in favor of a two-year AI moratorium.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: The guidance does not offer, as far as I could see, one single affirmative, rigorous, evidence-based reason for the introduction of AI as a pedagogical good.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: Instead, the rationale for the guidance is that AI is here and here to stay, and so the school system must adapt and adopt.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: But this has a distinct whiff of if you can't beat them, join them, and it represents a capitulation to a supposed new status quo, when actually AI, its uses, benefits, costs, infrastructure are all deeply contested.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: The very last place to acquiesce to AI as some kind of foregone conclusion is in our classrooms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: Our children are not guinea pigs for under-regulated and rapaciously profit-seeking companies to refine their products.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: Mayor Mumdani's victory was met with immense joy by many, myself included, and his administration's stated values are about resisting corporate power.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: An encroachment, expanding childhood education, and keeping families committed to the city and to its public institutions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: Throwing the door open to AI as this guidance does would be a stunning betrayal of those values.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: The DOE cannot ask families to trust it to be a steward of childhood education and then announce that there are multiple domains, the so-called green light areas, where educators are told to proceed with using AI with confidence,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: even as that very same guidance acknowledges that the IRMA process is not yet capable of evaluating algorithmic bias, equity, impact, or instructional effectiveness in its own words.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: The guidance also acknowledges that the DOE does not yet have authoritative answers about AI's impact on biometric and behavioral data, academic integrity, family rights, cognitive offloading, and environmental impact.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: So seemingly everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: How on earth do you breezily tell educators to proceed with confidence without having this crucial information?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: AI will cheapen our students'educations, and I fear that parents with means will exit the public school system for private charter and alternative schools that commit to much more restricted and considered AI policies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sonali Thacker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Next, we have Jane Hatterer.

chapters.summary: Hatterer, an education technology policy consultant, opposes the two-year moratorium, citing Pew Research data that 51% of adults use AI chatbots and 95% of teens have heard of AI. She argues avoidance will diminish student agency and leave them less prepared, and references a Brookings Institute three-pillar strategy of Prosper, Prepare, and Protect rather than a pause.
chapters.title: Jane Hatterer opposes moratorium, citing AI ubiquity in teen lives

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Hi, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: My name is Jane Hatterer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: I'm an AI policy, AI literacy and civic education policy and advocacy consultant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: I've worked in the intersection of education and technology for 25 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: both at Columbia University and also working with secondary education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: I'm writing to express my strong opposition to the proposed two-year moratorium on generative AI in our schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: We are in a paddle boat facing a tsunami, and avoidance cannot be the strategy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: A two-year pause will not eliminate the technology, but only diminish our students'agency in a world already awash with AI, leaving them less prepared for college, the workforce, and civic life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: The proposed pause ignores the reality that AI is ubiquitous in our social, economic, and political landscape.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: According to Pew Research, Americans in AI 2026, approximately half of all U.S.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Adults, 51%, now use AI chatbots, as well as 21% of U.S.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Workforce report using AI in their jobs, up from 18% just a year ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: 38% utilize them specifically for work tasks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: In a Pew report, How Teens Use and View AI, 95% of teens have heard of AI and their use is diverse and deeply integrated into their lives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Over half of U.S.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Teens report using chatbots to help with schoolwork.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: 10% of teens already turn to AI for emotional support or advice, and that number is doubling.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: To 20% black teens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: According to 2026 Common Sense Media, a comprehensive report on teens, tweens, and AI, while 75% of students report that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Their schools have discussed rules for what they cannot do with AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Only just over 50% have been taught how to use it safely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Furthermore, nearly half of students have never had a conversation with their parents about AI safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Leaving students to navigate their high-stakes interactions alone is irresponsible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Black and Hispanic...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: It's time already?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: Oh, I'm so sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: I would like to just say one thing for the council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: The Brookings Institute report, the basis of which was used for a pause, in fact does not call for a pause or a freeze.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: It has a three-pillar strategy called Prosper, Prepare, and Protect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: If I can just articulate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: No, I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jane Hatterer
utterances.text: I think additional testimony you could submit in writing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And we will, as we always do, review the written testimony.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I thank you so much for your testimony for this very important hearing.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I will now call on Zoom David Monahan.

chapters.summary: Monahan from Fair Play describes a position statement signed by 260 organizations and experts calling for a five-year pause on Gen AI products for students. He lists five requirements for reintroduction: improved learning without cognitive offloading, absolute safety, no cheating, privacy and civil rights prioritization, and never replacing teachers for vulnerable populations.
chapters.title: David Monahan of Fair Play on five-year pause for Gen AI in schools

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: I'm David Monahan, Campaign Director at Fair Play.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: We're a national nonprofit organization that works to enhance children's well-being by eliminating the harmful business practices of marketers and big tech.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: I'm here today on behalf of thousands of our supporters who reside in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: I live in Massachusetts, but I'm a native of Brooklyn and a proud graduate of New York City's school system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: We urge you to hear the voices that are rising among the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: public urging caution and asking our education leaders to not use children as guinea pigs to test out unproven and unexamined technologies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: In April, Fair Play and our Screening Time Action Network led 260 organizations and experts in signing a position statement calling for a five-year pause on Gen AI products for students,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: saying those products pose significant harm to children.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: The signers included Fordham Law professors Zephyr Teachout, neuroscientist Jared Cooney-Horbath, and member orgs in New York City's AI Moratorium Coalition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: The position statement says a five-year pause on all products using Gen AI that impact children in schools would allow time for school communities to learn about the implications and uses of Gen AI in education, to ask questions,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: and to provide feedback.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: The statement says that after the pause, Gen AI products should only be introduced into schools if they meet five requirements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: Improve learning outcomes without causing cognitive offloading or impeding human relationships.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: Demonstrate absolute safety for students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: They won't be used for cheating or academic dishonesty.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: They consider and prioritize privacy, civil rights, ethics, justice, and climate impacts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: And they will never be used in place of teachers, especially for vulnerable populations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: Our letter was cited earlier this month when a majority of members of the City Council signed a letter to Mayor Mamdani and schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: Chancellor Samuels calling for a two-year moratorium on AI in New York City schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: Please see the opinion of these experts who say AI does not belong in schools until it's established that AI is safe and effective.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Monahan
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to speak with you this afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Dwyer from the Center for Democracy and Technology identifies four key AI risks from national research: data breaches, deepfakes, AI system failures, and troubling student-chatbot interactions. She recommends risk management, AI governance, transparency requirements for vendors, model procurement resources, and formal avenues to engage parents and students in AI decisions.
chapters.title: Maddie Dwyer of CDT on AI risks and governance recommendations

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Maddie Dwyer, you may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: Chairs and members of the committees, my name is Maddie Dwyer, and I'm a state policy analyst at the Center for Democracy and Technology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify on behalf of CDT regarding AI, student data, and privacy in New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: CDT is a nonprofit, nonpartisan organization that works to advance civil rights and civil liberties in the digital age for everyone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: A key part of that mission is ensuring the use of data and technology in K-12 education is centered around privacy, security, civil rights, safety, and trust.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: As has been acknowledged here today in the hearing, K-12 schools across the country, including in New York City, have rapidly adopted AI tools, which is reshaping how students, teachers, and school administrators learn, teach, and work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: This heightened acceleration of AI use in classrooms has happened even amid growing recognition of the risks this technology poses to students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: Our nationally representative polling research has uncovered four key risks of AI that all increase the more a school uses AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: Data breaches and ransomware attacks, proliferation of deepfakes and non-consensual intimate imagery, AI systems failing to work as intended, and troubling interactions between students.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: and chatbots.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: The council plays a critical role in addressing these risks so that the potential beneficial uses of AI in schools can be realized.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: In particular, the council can take action to address risk management, AI governance, and transparency, including encouraging New York City public schools to conduct more research on how this technology impacts students,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: establish transparency requirements for ed tech AI vendors, and mandate creation of model procurement resources to guide AI purchasing decisions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: As the Council and New York City Public Schools evaluate the potential benefits and risks of AI use in schools, they should most importantly

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: provide formal avenues to engage parents, students, and community members in decisions about whether and how to use AI in the classroom and use this feedback to drive their policymaking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: The written testimony I've submitted for the council goes into more detail on our research, these policy priorities, and highlights further policy interventions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: Once again, I want to express my gratitude for the opportunity to testify, and CDT remains available and ready to offer expertise.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie Dwyer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

chapters.summary: East, a parent in De La Rosa's district, shares her child's experience with Zurn and IXL, describing how these tools took her struggling learner back to lower levels rather than identifying the problem. She criticizes IXL's 'smart score' as devastating to children's self-esteem, contrasting AI tools with human teachers who can pivot approaches.
chapters.title: Alyssa East on ed tech's devastating impact on struggling learners

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And lastly, on Zoom, we have Alyssa East.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Dinowitz and Councilmember De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: I'm Alyssa East.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: I live in Councilmember De La Rosa's district.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: Thank you for representing for Uptown.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: And I'm not going to read from anything prepared.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: I think you guys have heard a lot of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: facts and data and very strong arguments against AI in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: I'm going to speak more personally about my son's experience with these tools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: Earlier, the deputy chancellor mentioned iReady, IXL, Zurn, and Grammarly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: My child is no longer in New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: We're a Carter case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: But when my child was in District 6 schools, he was regularly given Zurn and given IXL, and these were assigned to him because of his learning differences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: And these were devastating to my child, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: So I want you to understand that something that is built on the same technology that is an AI tool can be very destructive to a child's sense of self.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: And that is because these tools, unlike a human teacher, might recognize that a child is failing or struggling.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: And instead of identifying why the child is struggling, they take the child back to a lower level of learning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: is utterly ineffective.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: And some of these platforms, such as IXL, give the child a smart score.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: Now, if the child is struggling, they're getting a low smart score that has no reflection of their abilities or their intelligence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: This is how many of these apps work, and they are utterly devastating to struggling learners.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: They are utterly devastating to children, whereas human teachers recognize

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: They are skilled and trained to see, oh, this is what this child needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: They don't need to go backwards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: They maybe need a pivot or a slightly different approach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alyssa East
utterances.text: EdTech does not allow this.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And you can, thank you so much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And as a reminder, you could submit additional written testimony to testimony at council.nyc.gov.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I will now call.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Anita Rao and Joseph Sende for in-person testimony.

chapters.summary: Rao, a Citywide Council on Special Education member, testifies about the lack of adherence to federal special education law and the long-standing use of AI in assistive tech without guardrails. She asks why DOE doesn't mandate GoGuardian across all schools with a central budget rather than leaving it to individual school choice.
chapters.title: Anita Rao on special education, GoGuardian, and AI guardrails

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: Hello?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: Hi, it's amazing to see all of you in person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: I spent all day with all of you, virtually.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: And thank you for hearing my testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: It was an impromptu decision.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: I flew over here real quickly, so if it's a little shoddy, forgive me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: Yeah, so...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: You have an important, crucial role, and it's a big one, and we value your time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: We all do as a community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: I'm Anita Rao.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: I'm the former president of a PTA, now a member of the Citywide Council on Special Education, appointed by the Public Advocate Office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: I'm really excited to take on this role.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: And I see a lot of, oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: So I'm here this evening in my personal capacity and my opinions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: Today we heard from City Council members, members of the DOE Central, from DIAL, and consultants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: There are a lot of different angles and approaches we heard today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: The bottom line is there's a lack of, but the bottom line is currently there's still a lack of adherence to federal law around special education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: There's negligence of education around special education rights and service management, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: Many hearings could be avoided entirely if we simply through, if each and every school

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: consistently be aware of providing education, special needs, empowerment, evaluations, and more collaborative resolution practices to avoid gaps in academic and social-emotional learning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: It has been discovered that many

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: I'm going to stop right here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: I'll submit this written testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: But what I want to tell you is that AI has been long time used through the assistive tech devices in special needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: These ed tech software have been backed by AI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: And there's been no guardrails whatsoever.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: For example, GoGuardian is a vendor that exists already with the DOE.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: However, it's not being utilized.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: It's the choice of the school.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: And the budget.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: Why isn't the DOE Central providing a budget across the board mandating for GoGuardian to be utilized in the schools by the teachers and parents having access?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: There are certain independent schools and some charter schools that are already offering this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: Why can't we do this?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: Why can't we put this in place?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: While we're implementing a moratorium, which I hope happens.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anita Rao
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The writer closes out.

chapters.summary: Senda, a parent at PS 184, describes his son looking forward to classes because of games dressed up as education, warning of addictive technology attachment. He notes a power imbalance between DOE and the Council, where the Council seems to be treated like an outside entity, and asks how parents can support the Council's oversight efforts.
chapters.title: Joseph Senda on screen addiction and power imbalance with DOE

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: Hey.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: No pressure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: Joseph Senda.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: I'm a parent at PS 184 in

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: Manhattan, not too far from here in the District 1, I think.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: Our school has a committee that we've formed that is led by one of the moms who's a psychiatrist herself, and she's coming at this from...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: What is this doing to kids'brains?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: It's very, like, focused on the youngest grades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: That's where our concern is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: I think you're doing another hearing that's going to be more about screen time in September, so it might come back.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: But for now, yeah, I wanted to testify to what I'm seeing in my son.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: A lot of...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: Like, looking forward, you know, he's like looking forward to a particular class, and I say, what's going to happen in that class?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: And he's like, I'm going to be playing a game.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: You know, it's dressed up as education, but it's...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: It's really a game, and whether it's supposedly learning about English or learning about math, at the end of the day,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: I think it's like implanting an addictive type of attachment to technology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: I did listen to everything earlier, and I really, you know, we were wondering whether we should advocate at the district level, the city council level, the state or the federal level.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: I really appreciate everything I heard from you guys today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: I think there's a power imbalance between the DOE and the council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: It feels like you guys are almost like foiling, like as if you're an outside entity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: So we really appreciate your support, and we'd love to know what we can do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: You know, if it's as a parent association, individual parents, should I be contacting my council member?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Senda
utterances.text: Whatever it is, we want to support you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We do have one more on Zoom.

chapters.summary: Carmen, mother of two NYC public school graduates, testifies that Gen Z expresses distrust and alarm at AI's encroachment. She shares her daughter's experience with an AI job portal that misrepresented her resume, and her daughter's decision to stop using AI to avoid losing her ability to write. She warns AI in schools serves standardized testing obsession.
chapters.title: Kamala Carmen on young people's distrust of AI and resume concerns

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Kamala Carmen.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I am the mother of two children who graduated from New York City Public Schools and are now in their early 20s.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm sure you've read about this, but in polls and in the reaction to commencement speakers, young people of their generation, Gen Z, express distrust of and even alarm at the encouragement of AI into seemingly all aspects of their lives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This is in direct contrast to the woman who was...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: anti-the-moratorium before who was like, it's all over their lives and they love it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: No, they don't love it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: One of my daughters recently sent me screenshots of the portal that was being used for a job she was applying for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: She'd been instructed to upload her resume to the portal, which then distributed it into various sections, transforming it as it did so into language which was not her own.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And in her opinion, misrepresented her understanding of her abilities and workplace objectives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: She confided in me that she had started to use AI on her resume, but had decided not to do so in the future because it was, quote, too easy, and she didn't want to, quote, lose her ability to write.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I fear that when she was younger, this same child might not have had this wise realization.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Not that she wasn't wise.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: the time, but it is a bit of a mature realization, I think.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And it's terrifying that children, you know, who are going through this are feeling that they are going to be denied their abilities to develop as

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: as writers, as thinkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And I just want to close by saying that I know a lot of the ed tech that is coming into our schools is coming into it because we have an obsession with standardized testing and test scores.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And so a lot of the screeners, the corporate curriculum, et cetera, are all being put in at the service of raising test scores.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Time expired.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz thanks co-chair De La Rosa and all participants, summarizing that the hearing revealed significant gaps in DOE's AI policy including bias and equity evaluation, student safety, academic impact, and cognitive offloading. He commits to pushing for answers from DOE and shaping policy that keeps students safe while maintaining rigorous academic standards. The hearing is adjourned.
chapters.title: Chair Dinowitz closes hearing with summary of AI policy gaps

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: All right, I want to thank my fabulous co-chair, Chair De La Rosa, my neighbor to the south, for this really, really important hearing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think anyone who is watching saw that there are a significant number of gaps

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: in the DOE's AI policy, how we already have these tools in our classroom, and yet the guidance isn't in place for a number of critical elements, including bias and equity, including student safety, and including

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: academic impact and cognitive offloading.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is personal to us as parents, but it's also important to anyone in New York City who wants to see a bright future for our kids, kids who are critically thinking, kids who are going to be the future of our city.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So we look forward to more answers from the DOE, really shaping a policy that makes sense for our students, keeps them safe, keeps them protected, all while

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: expanding their knowledge and making sure we have rigorous academic standards here in our city.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you again, co-chair.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you to all the staff and the sergeants and everyone who testified today.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And the hearing is adjourned.

summary: Joint hearing of the Committees on Education and Technology examining AI use, student data privacy, and algorithmic tools in NYC public schools, with testimony from DOE officials and dozens of public witnesses.

Council members pressed DOE on gaps in AI guidance, lack of bias evaluation in the IRMA vetting process, low training completion rates, delayed data breach notifications, and the absence of empirical evidence supporting AI tools in classrooms.

Legislation discussed included bills on annual reporting of algorithmic tools (Intro 2129), quarterly data breach reporting (Intro 2130), school admissions disclosure (Intro 412), and studies on access to gifted/talented and special education programs (Intros 684 and 853).
title: Committee on Education
topic: Oversight - Artificial Intelligence, Student Data, and Privacy in NYC Public Schools
